> - **Course:** PECCT633 - AI AND ML IN CYBER SECURITY DEFENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Machine Learning in Cyber Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Machine Learning in Cyber Security** is the disciplined application of computational algorithms and statistical models that enable security systems to automatically learn, identify patterns, and make intelligent decisions regarding cyber threats — *without being explicitly programmed for every possible attack scenario*. It bridges the gap between traditional signature-based detection and the modern need for adaptive, intelligent threat mitigation.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020)**, the course defines this as a sub-domain of *Artificial Intelligence in Cyber Defense* where models are trained on massive datasets of network traffic, system logs, malware samples, and user behavior to autonomously detect intrusions, classify threats, and predict future attacks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECCT633 - Module 1):**
> Machine Learning in Cyber Security forms the foundational pillar of AI-driven defense. The student is expected to understand the *workflow of integrating ML into security operations* (SecOps), the *taxonomy of ML techniques* used, and the *evaluation metrics* that validate a security model.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Watchman"

Imagine a **night security guard** posted at the gate of a large residential colony.

- **Phase 1 — Training (Learning Phase):** For the first few weeks, the guard observes the residents. He learns that *Mr. A walks his dog at 6:30 AM*, *children leave for school at 8:00 AM*, *delivery boys arrive between 10 AM – 12 PM*, and *the colony is silent after 11 PM*. He builds an internal model of "normal behavior."

- **Phase 2 — Deployment (Inference Phase):** On day 30, at 3:00 AM, he sees a hooded figure trying the handle of a parked car. This **does not match the learned pattern** → he raises an alarm.

- **Phase 3 — Adaptation (Feedback Loop):** The next day, a resident informs him that *plumbers visit at 3 AM for an emergency repair*. He updates his model — he has *learned* a new "normal."

**This is exactly how Machine Learning operates in Cyber Security:**
- *Training data* = the historical logs of "normal" network behavior.
- *The model* = the guard's brain.
- *Anomalies / intrusions* = the hooded figure.
- *Feedback* = the SOC analyst confirming whether an alert was a *True Positive* or *False Positive*.

### 1.3 Key Terminology and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Terms You Must Know for KTU Examinations:**

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **TTP** | Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures (attacker behavior pattern) |
| **SOC** | Security Operations Center |
| **IDS** | Intrusion Detection System |
| **IPS** | Intrusion Prevention System |
| **TP / FP / TN / FN** | True Positive, False Positive, True Negative, False Negative |
| **IoC** | Indicator of Compromise |
| **CVSS** | Common Vulnerability Scoring System |
| **SIEM** | Security Information and Event Management |

The standard metrics **Accuracy**, **Precision**, **Recall**, and **F1-Score** are the mathematical yardsticks by which every ML-based security model is graded in the KTU evaluation scheme.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Workflow — How ML is Deployed in Cyber Defense

The integration of ML into a security pipeline follows a structured, repeatable workflow. Each step has direct KTU exam relevance:

**Step 1 — Data Acquisition & Aggregation**
Security data is harvested from heterogeneous sources: firewall logs, NetFlow records, DNS queries, endpoint telemetry, SIEM alerts, dark-web feeds, and honeypot captures. The volume of this data is enormous — often terabytes per day in enterprise environments.

**Step 2 — Data Pre-processing & Labelling**
Raw logs are noisy. Pre-processing involves *parsing, normalization, deduplication, and labelling* (for supervised learning). Class imbalance (e.g., only 0.01% of traffic being malicious) is corrected using techniques such as SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique).

**Step 3 — Feature Engineering & Extraction**
Raw packets and logs are converted into numerical *feature vectors*. For example, a network flow may be represented by features such as: *packet size, inter-arrival time, source port entropy, protocol type, TCP flag count, byte ratio*. Domain expertise (e.g., a SOC analyst's intuition) is critical here.

**Step 4 — Model Selection & Training**
An algorithm is chosen based on the task: classification (Random Forest, SVM), clustering (K-Means, DBSCAN), sequence modelling (LSTM, Transformers), or anomaly scoring (Isolation Forest, Autoencoders).

**Step 5 — Validation & Hyperparameter Tuning**
The model is tested on *unseen* data using k-fold cross-validation. Metrics such as *precision, recall, F1, ROC-AUC* are computed to tune parameters.

**Step 6 — Deployment & Continuous Learning**
The validated model is pushed into production (e.g., embedded into a SIEM or EDR). It must be **retrained periodically** because attackers evolve — this phenomenon is called *Concept Drift*.

### 2.2 The Three Paradigms of ML in Cyber Security

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 expects students to distinguish the three learning paradigms with real-world security examples:**

**A. Supervised Learning**
The model is trained on **labelled** data — samples already marked as *"malicious"* or *"benign"*. The model learns a mapping function *f(x) → y*. After training, it can classify new, unseen samples.
- *Examples in Cyber Security:* Spam email classification, malware family classification, phishing URL detection, DGA (Domain Generation Algorithm) detection.

**B. Unsupervised Learning**
The model is given **unlabelled** data and must discover hidden structures, clusters, or outliers on its own. It is invaluable when labelled attack data is scarce (which is the *real-world norm*).
- *Examples in Cyber Security:* Anomaly detection in network traffic, user behavior analytics (UBA), zero-day malware discovery, insider threat detection.

**C. Reinforcement Learning (RL)**
An *agent* interacts with an environment, takes *actions*, and receives *rewards* or *penalties*. Over time, it learns an optimal **policy** that maximizes cumulative reward.
- *Examples in Cyber Security:* Autonomous penetration testing, adaptive honeypot deception, dynamic firewall rule optimization, automated incident response.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Evaluation Metrics for Security ML Models

The following table is the **single most important cheat-sheet** for any KTU Part A or Part B question on this topic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** TP = True Positive, FP = False Positive, TN = True Negative, FN = False Negative. N = total samples.

| Metric | Formula | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN}$ | Overall correctness; **misleading on imbalanced data** (e.g., 99.99% benign traffic). |
| **Precision** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of all alerts raised, how many were *real* attacks? High precision = low false alarms. |
| **Recall (Detection Rate)** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Of all *actual* attacks, how many did we catch? High recall = low miss rate. **Most critical in security.** |
| **Specificity (TNR)** | $\frac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Ability to correctly identify benign traffic as benign. |
| **F1-Score** | $2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall — balances both for imbalanced security datasets. |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $\frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Probability of false alarm — must be minimized to avoid *analyst fatigue*. |
| **MCC (Matthews Correlation Coefficient)** | $\frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | Robust metric for *heavily imbalanced* datasets; range $[-1, +1]$. |
| **ROC-AUC** | Area under the TPR vs FPR curve | Threshold-independent measure of classifier discriminative power. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute Value Notation in KTU Answer Sheets:** When writing these formulas in your exam booklet, **never** use the vertical pipe `|` symbol inside markdown tables, as it breaks table syntax. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in LaTeX, or simply write "absolute value of x" in plain text within table cells.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Network IDS/IPS:** ML models deployed in tools like *Snort ML*, *Suricata*, and *Zeek* detect port scans, brute-force attempts, and botnet C2 traffic in real time.
- **Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR):** Products like CrowdStrike, Microsoft Defender, and SentinelOne use ML to flag process-injection, fileless malware, and credential dumping.
- **Email Security:** Gmail's spam filter (a deep neural network) blocks > 99.9% of spam, phishing, and malware before it reaches the inbox.
- **Fraud Detection:** PayPal, Visa, and Mastercard use gradient-boosted trees and deep learning to flag fraudulent credit card transactions within milliseconds.
- **Threat Intelligence:** NLP models mine dark-web forums and CVE databases to predict emerging exploits.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Core Evaluation Metrics from the Confusion Matrix

A **Confusion Matrix** is a 2×2 contingency table that maps *predicted labels* against *actual (ground-truth) labels* for a binary classifier (the most common case in security: *malicious vs benign*).

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}
\hline
 & \textbf{Predicted: Malicious} & \textbf{Predicted: Benign} \\
\hline
\textbf{Actual: Malicious} & TP & FN \\
\hline
\textbf{Actual: Benign} & FP & TN \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Step 1 — Definition of Accuracy.**
Accuracy is the proportion of *correct predictions* out of the *total predictions made*.

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{Number of Correct Predictions}}{\text{Total Predictions}}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the correct predictions from the confusion matrix.**
A correct prediction occurs when the predicted class matches the actual class. There are two such cases:
- *Actual Malicious AND Predicted Malicious* → counted as **TP**.
- *Actual Benign AND Predicted Benign* → counted as **TN**.

Therefore:

$$
\text{Number of Correct Predictions} = TP + TN
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the total predictions.**
The total is the sum of all four cells of the matrix:

$$
\text{Total Predictions} = TP + FP + TN + FN
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the Accuracy definition.**

$$
\boxed{\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN}}
$$

---

**Step 5 — Derivation of Precision.**
Precision is *"Of all the samples I flagged as Malicious, how many were actually Malicious?"* This is a **column-wise** view of the confusion matrix.

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{\text{Correctly Flagged Malicious}}{\text{Total Flagged Malicious}} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

---

**Step 6 — Derivation of Recall (Detection Rate).**
Recall is *"Of all the actually Malicious samples in the wild, how many did I successfully detect?"* This is a **row-wise** view.

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{\text{Correctly Detected Malicious}}{\text{Total Actually Malicious}} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

---

**Step 7 — Derivation of the F1-Score.**
The F1-Score is the **harmonic mean** of Precision (P) and Recall (R). The harmonic mean is chosen (rather than arithmetic mean) because it *penalizes extreme imbalance* between P and R.

The general harmonic mean of two numbers $a$ and $b$ is:

$$
H = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b}}
$$

Substituting $a = P$ and $b = R$:

$$
F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}}
$$

Bringing to a common denominator:

$$
F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{R + P}{P \cdot R}} = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A deployed ML-based Intrusion Detection System was tested on **10,000 network flows**. The confusion matrix obtained is as follows:
> - TP = 180, FP = 20
> - FN = 30, TN = 9,770
>
> **Compute:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and FPR. Comment on the model's suitability for a production SOC environment.

**Step 1 — Verify the total sample count.**

$$
\text{Total} = 180 + 20 + 30 + 9770 = 10{,}000 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Accuracy.**

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{180 + 9770}{10000} = \frac{9950}{10000} = 0.995 = 99.50\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Precision.**

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90 = 90.00\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Recall (Detection Rate).**

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{180}{180 + 30} = \frac{180}{210} \approx 0.8571 = 85.71\%
$$

**Step 5 — Compute F1-Score.**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.90 \cdot 0.8571}{0.90 + 0.8571} = \frac{1.5428}{1.7571} \approx 0.8781 = 87.81\%
$$

**Step 6 — Compute False Positive Rate.**

$$
FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{20}{20 + 9770} = \frac{20}{9790} \approx 0.00204 = 0.204\%
$$

**Step 7 — Engineer's Comment.**

The accuracy of **99.50%** appears excellent, but it is *deceptive* because the dataset is heavily imbalanced (only 210 attacks out of 10,000 flows ≈ 2.1% attack ratio). The Recall of **85.71%** means the IDS is **missing 14.29% of real attacks** (FN = 30) — *unacceptable in a production SOC*. The FPR of **0.204%** is low, meaning the model does not generate excessive false alarms. However, the model should be improved (e.g., via SMOTE resampling or cost-sensitive learning) to push the Recall above **95%** before deployment in a critical infrastructure environment.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which ML Paradigm in Security

| Security Problem | Data Availability | Best ML Paradigm | Recommended Algorithm |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spam / Phishing Email | Labelled corpus available | **Supervised** | Naive Bayes, SVM, BERT |
| Zero-Day Malware Discovery | No labels for new malware | **Unsupervised** | Isolation Forest, Autoencoder |
| Network Anomaly Detection | Mostly unlabelled traffic | **Unsupervised** | K-Means, DBSCAN, One-Class SVM |
| Insider Threat Detection | Limited labelled incidents | **Semi-Supervised** | Self-Training, Label Propagation |
| Adaptive Firewall Tuning | Reward signals from attacks blocked | **Reinforcement** | Q-Learning, Deep Q-Network (DQN) |
| Fraud Transaction Detection | Labelled historical fraud | **Supervised** | XGBoost, Random Forest, LSTM |
| Threat Intelligence Mining | Unstructured text (CVE, blogs) | **Unsupervised (NLP)** | Topic Modeling (LDA), Word2Vec |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024]**
*Define Machine Learning in the context of Cyber Security. List any two real-world applications where ML is deployed in modern Security Operations Centers (SOCs).*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Machine Learning in Cyber Security refers to the use of algorithms and statistical models that allow security systems to automatically learn from historical and real-time data, identify patterns indicative of cyber threats, and make intelligent decisions (such as classifying traffic as malicious/benign or detecting anomalies) *without being explicitly programmed with static rules for every threat*.

**Applications (1 Mark — any two):**
1. **Spam and Phishing Email Detection** — Gmail and Microsoft Outlook use ML classifiers to filter malicious emails.
2. **Network Intrusion Detection** — Tools like Suricata with ML modules detect port scans, DDoS, and botnet traffic.
3. **Malware Classification** — Antivirus engines use ML to classify malware into families (Trojan, Ransomware, Worm, etc.).
4. **Credit Card Fraud Detection** — PayPal and Visa use gradient-boosted models to flag fraudulent transactions.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Supervised, Unsupervised, and Reinforcement Learning in the context of cyber threat detection. Give one security use-case for each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Paradigm | Key Idea | Cyber Security Use-Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Supervised** | Learns from *labelled* data (input-output pairs) | Malware family classification using labelled samples. |
| **Unsupervised** | Discovers hidden structure in *unlabelled* data | Zero-day anomaly detection in network traffic. |
| **Reinforcement** | Agent learns via *reward/penalty* feedback | Adaptive firewall rule optimization using Q-learning. |

*(1 mark for correct distinction of each paradigm with a use-case; 1 mark total for table clarity.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the complete workflow of integrating a Machine Learning model into a Cyber Security pipeline. Describe each stage with an appropriate example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Machine Learning based malware detector was evaluated on 8,000 files. The resulting confusion matrix is:

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}
\hline
 & \textbf{Predicted Malware} & \textbf{Predicted Benign} \\
\hline
\textbf{Actual Malware} & 350 & 50 \\
\hline
\textbf{Actual Benign} & 30 & 7{,}570 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

Compute the **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and False Positive Rate**. Comment on whether this model is suitable for deployment in a production antivirus product. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Workflow (7 Marks)

**Stage 1 — Data Collection (1 Mark):**
Gather data from logs, network packets, endpoint telemetry, threat feeds. *Example: Collecting NetFlow records from edge routers and EDR alerts from endpoints.*

**Stage 2 — Data Pre-processing (1 Mark):**
Handle missing values, remove duplicates, normalize, and label data. *Example: Labelling flows as "Benign" or "DDoS Attack" using MITRE ATT&CK tagging.*

**Stage 3 — Feature Engineering (1 Mark):**
Transform raw data into numerical features. *Example: For network flows, extract packet size, duration, protocol, inter-arrival time, source-destination byte ratio.*

**Stage 4 — Model Training (1 Mark):**
Choose an algorithm (e.g., Random Forest, SVM, LSTM) and train on 80% of the data using cross-validation.

**Stage 5 — Validation (1 Mark):**
Test on the remaining 20% (unseen data). Compute metrics: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, ROC-AUC.

**Stage 6 — Deployment (1 Mark):**
Integrate the trained model into SIEM/EDR/SOC for real-time inference. *Example: Deploying the model as a microservice behind a Kafka stream processor.*

**Stage 7 — Monitoring & Continuous Learning (1 Mark):**
Monitor for *concept drift* (when attacker TTPs change), collect analyst feedback, and periodically retrain the model.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:** TP = 350, FP = 30, FN = 50, TN = 7570. Total = 8000.

**Step 1 — Accuracy (1 Mark):**

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{\text{Total}} = \frac{350 + 7570}{8000} = \frac{7920}{8000} = 0.9900 = 99.00\%
$$

**Step 2 — Precision (1 Mark):**

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{350}{350 + 30} = \frac{350}{380} \approx 0.9211 = 92.11\%
$$

**Step 3 — Recall (Detection Rate) (1 Mark):**

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{350}{350 + 50} = \frac{350}{400} = 0.8750 = 87.50\%
$$

**Step 4 — F1-Score (2 Marks):**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.9211 \cdot 0.8750}{0.9211 + 0.8750} = \frac{1.6119}{1.7961} \approx 0.8975 = 89.75\%
$$

**Step 5 — False Positive Rate (1 Mark):**

$$
FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{30}{30 + 7570} = \frac{30}{7600} \approx 0.00395 = 0.395\%
$$

**Step 6 — Suitability Comment (1 Mark):**
The model has high Accuracy (99.00%) and acceptable Precision (92.11%), meaning it does not generate excessive false alarms. However, the **Recall of 87.50%** is concerning — it **misses 12.50% of actual malware** (50 files out of 400). In a production antivirus deployed on millions of endpoints, even 1% miss rate is unacceptable. The model is **not yet suitable for production deployment**; it should be improved using techniques like SMOTE resampling, cost-sensitive learning, or a deeper neural architecture to push the Recall above 95%.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a)** Discuss the challenges and limitations of applying Machine Learning to Cyber Security. Mention at least four challenges. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Anomaly Detection** using Unsupervised Learning. Describe how the **Isolation Forest** algorithm works and why it is well-suited for detecting zero-day attacks. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Challenges (7 Marks)

**Challenge 1 — Adversarial Attacks (2 Marks):**
Attackers deliberately craft *adversarial examples* (e.g., perturbed malware binaries) that fool ML classifiers while remaining functional. Techniques like *model poisoning*, *evasion attacks*, and *data injection* can subvert the entire detection system. ML models that are 99% accurate on clean data can drop to 50% on adversarial data.

**Challenge 2 — Concept Drift and Evasion (1 Mark):**
Cyber threats evolve rapidly. A model trained on 2023 attack data may fail to detect 2025 threats because the attacker's TTPs, payload obfuscation, and command-and-control infrastructure change. Continuous retraining is mandatory but expensive.

**Challenge 3 — Data Imbalance and Label Scarcity (1 Mark):**
Malicious samples are *vastly outnumbered* by benign samples (often ratio 1:10,000). Labelling attack data requires expensive human expert time. This causes models to be biased toward the majority class (benign) and miss rare attacks.

**Challenge 4 — High False Positive Rate (Analyst Fatigue) (1 Mark):**
Security Operations Centers are overwhelmed by alerts. A model with even 1% FPR generates thousands of false alarms daily, causing *alert fatigue* and leading analysts to ignore real threats. Achieving low FPR *and* high Recall simultaneously is extremely difficult.

**Challenge 5 — Interpretability and Explainability (1 Mark):**
Most high-performing ML models (Deep Neural Networks, Gradient Boosting) are *black boxes*. Security analysts and regulators demand explanations: *"Why was this file flagged as malware?"* The lack of interpretability hinders trust, debugging, and legal admissibility.

**Challenge 6 — Privacy-Preserving Constraints (1 Mark):**
Security data often contains Personally Identifiable Information (PII). Training ML models on raw logs can violate GDPR, HIPAA, and DPDP Act (India 2023). Federated learning and differential privacy are emerging solutions but add complexity.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Isolation Forest & Anomaly Detection (7 Marks)

**Anomaly Detection Concept (2 Marks):**
Anomaly detection is the identification of *rare items, events, or observations* that deviate significantly from the majority of the data and do not conform to a well-defined notion of normal behavior. In cyber security, anomalies may indicate intrusions, insider threats, malware beaconing, or compromised accounts. Unsupervised learning is preferred because labelled attack data is scarce.

**Isolation Forest Algorithm — Working Principle (3 Marks):**
The Isolation Forest algorithm, proposed by Liu, Ting, and Zhou (2008), isolates anomalies instead of profiling normal points. The key insight is:

> *Anomalies are "few and different," hence they are easier to isolate than normal points.*

**Mechanism:**
1. The algorithm builds an ensemble of **isolation trees (iTrees)** by randomly selecting a feature and a split value between the min and max of that feature.
2. This recursive partitioning continues until all instances are isolated (each in its own leaf) or a max depth is reached.
3. **Anomalies require *fewer splits* to be isolated** because they lie in sparse, low-density regions. Normal points lie in dense regions and require *more splits*.
4. The **anomaly score** for a point $x$ is computed as:

$$
s(x, n) = 2^{\frac{E(h(x))}{c(n)}}
$$

where $E(h(x))$ is the average path length from the root to $x$ across all iTrees, and $c(n)$ is the average path length of an unsuccessful search in a Binary Search Tree:

$$
c(n) = 2 H(n-1) - \frac{2(n-1)}{n}
$$

where $H(k)$ is the harmonic number approximated as $\ln(k) + \gamma$ (Euler-Mascheroni constant $\gamma \approx 0.5772$).

- $s(x) \to 1$ → anomaly
- $s(x) < 0.5$ → normal
- $s(x) \approx 0.5$ → no clear anomaly

**Why Isolation Forest is Suited for Zero-Day Attacks (2 Marks):**
1. **Unsupervised:** It does not require labels of known attacks — perfect for *zero-day* scenarios where no prior signature exists.
2. **Low computational cost:** Linear time complexity $O(n \log n)$ and low memory — suitable for high-throughput network streams.
3. **Robust to irrelevant features:** Random feature selection prevents over-reliance on any single feature.
4. **Effective on high-dimensional data:** Works well on features extracted from network flows, system calls, and API sequences.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Pitfall 1 (1 Mark Lost):** Students often write only "Accuracy" and skip Precision, Recall, F1. For ML-in-Security questions, the examiner expects *all four* metrics because Accuracy alone is misleading on imbalanced data.
> - **Pitfall 2 (1 Mark Lost):** Students confuse **Precision and Recall**. Remember: *Precision* = "Of what I caught, how correct was I?" *Recall* = "Of what was out there, how much did I catch?"
> - **Pitfall 3 (1 Mark Lost):** Failing to provide a *use-case* for each ML paradigm in definition questions. KTU examiners award marks for the *application* dimension, not just textbook definitions.
> - **Pitfall 4 (1 Mark Lost):** Not commenting on the *practical suitability* of the numerical answer. A high-accuracy model with low Recall is *not* production-ready — students must say so explicitly.
> - **Pitfall 5 (2 Marks Lost):** Using markdown `|` inside LaTeX tables breaks table rendering. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Machine Learning in Cyber Security:**

- ✅ **Definition:** ML in Cyber Security = enabling security systems to learn from data and make intelligent threat decisions without explicit programming.
- ✅ **Three Paradigms:** Supervised (labelled data), Unsupervised (unlabelled, pattern discovery), Reinforcement (reward-based agent learning).
- ✅ **Workflow:** Data Collection → Pre-processing → Feature Engineering → Model Training → Validation → Deployment → Continuous Learning.
- ✅ **Key Use-Cases:** Spam filtering, network IDS, malware classification, fraud detection, insider threat detection, adaptive honeypots.
- ✅ **Confusion Matrix:** TP, FP, TN, FN — always draw the 2×2 table first in numerical problems.
- ✅ **Critical Metrics in Security:** **Recall (Detection Rate) is king** — missing an attack is far costlier than a false alarm. **Precision** must also be high to avoid analyst fatigue. **F1-Score** balances both.
- ✅ **Imbalanced Data:** Accuracy is misleading. Use Precision, Recall, F1, MCC, or ROC-AUC.
- ✅ **Challenges:** Adversarial attacks, concept drift, data imbalance, high FPR, interpretability, privacy.
- ✅ **Isolation Forest:** Unsupervised, low cost, ideal for zero-day anomaly detection; score near 1 = anomaly.
- ✅ **Concept Drift:** Attackers evolve; models must be periodically retrained on fresh data.
- ✅ **Real-world Tools:** Snort ML, Suricata, Zeek, CrowdStrike, Microsoft Defender, Splunk ML, Elastic ML.
- ✅ **Frameworks:** MITRE ATT&CK for labelling, CVE/NVD for vulnerability data, NIST CSF for governance.
- ✅ **Sample formula to memorize:** $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ and $\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Machine Learning: Concepts and Terminology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT633 - AI AND ML IN CYBER SECURITY DEFENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Machine Learning: Concepts and Terminology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Machine Learning (ML)** is a subset of Artificial Intelligence (AI) that enables computer systems to **learn patterns from data** and **improve their performance on a specific task** over time, **without being explicitly programmed**, by using statistical methods and computational algorithms.

In the context of **PECCT633 (AI and ML in Cyber Security Defence)**, Machine Learning refers to the design of algorithms that can **autonomously detect, classify, predict, and respond** to cyber threats by learning from historical security data such as network traffic logs, malware samples, intrusion detection system (IDS) alerts, and user behaviour logs.

The mathematical foundation can be formally stated as:
A machine learns from **experience (E)** with respect to a class of **tasks (T)** and a **performance measure (P)**, if its performance at tasks in **T**, as measured by **P**, improves with experience **E** (Mitchell, 1997).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Teaching a Child to Recognise Spam Emails**
>
> Imagine you are teaching a 6-year-old child to identify spam emails. You do **not** write a rulebook of "if-then" conditions. Instead, you show the child **100 examples** of spam and **100 examples** of legitimate email, and point out the differences (e.g., "Look — this one says 'You won a lottery!' — that's spam"). Over time, the child **generalises** the patterns and can correctly classify a *new* email they have never seen before. **That is Machine Learning.**
>
> - The **emails** = *data*
> - The **child's brain** = *model/algorithm*
> - The **examples shown** = *training set*
> - The **final decision** = *prediction*

### 1.3 Core Terminology (Must Memorise for KTU Exam)

| Term | Definition | Cyber-Security Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Dataset** | A collection of data instances (rows) and features (columns) | A log file of 1 million network connections |
| **Feature** | An individual measurable property of a phenomenon | Packet size, source IP, protocol type |
| **Label (Target)** | The output value to be predicted | *Normal* or *Attack* |
| **Training Set** | Subset of data used to train the model | 80% of labelled network traffic |
| **Test Set** | Held-out data used to evaluate the model | 20% of unseen network traffic |
| **Hypothesis (h)** | The function mapping inputs to outputs the model has learnt | $h(x) \to \hat{y}$ |
| **Model** | The mathematical representation after training | A trained Random Forest classifier |
| **Prediction ($\hat{y}$)** | The output produced by the model for a given input | Predicted class: *DDoS Attack* |
| **Ground Truth ($y$)** | The actual/correct label | True class: *DDoS Attack* |
| **Epoch** | One complete pass over the entire training dataset | 50 epochs over a malware dataset |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A student **must** be able to distinguish between **Feature (X)** and **Label (Y)**. In supervised learning, the model learns the mapping $X \to Y$. The **feature matrix** is conventionally denoted as $\mathbf{X}$ of shape $(n, m)$ where $n$ = number of samples and $m$ = number of features.

### 1.4 Why ML in Cyber Security? (Syllabus Context)

Traditional cyber defence relied on **signature-based detection** (e.g., antivirus databases). Modern attacks are **polymorphic, zero-day, and adversarial**, making static rules obsolete. ML provides:
- **Adaptability** — Learns from new attack patterns automatically.
- **Scalability** — Processes millions of events per second.
- **Anomaly Detection** — Identifies previously unseen attacks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Paradigms of Machine Learning

Machine Learning is broadly classified into three categories. KTU frequently asks **"Compare supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning"** — memorise this matrix.

#### 2.1.1 Supervised Learning
- The algorithm is given **labelled training data** $(\mathbf{X}, \mathbf{y})$.
- Goal: Learn a mapping $f: \mathbf{X} \to \mathbf{y}$.
- Two sub-tasks:
  - **Classification** — Output is a discrete category (e.g., spam/not-spam, malware/benign).
  - **Regression** — Output is a continuous value (e.g., predicting the number of attacks per hour).
- **Cyber-Security Examples:** Spam filtering, malware classification, intrusion detection (NSL-KDD, CICIDS-2017).

#### 2.1.2 Unsupervised Learning
- The algorithm is given **unlabelled data** $\mathbf{X}$ only.
- Goal: Discover hidden structure, clusters, or anomalies.
- Common methods: **K-Means**, **DBSCAN**, **Autoencoders**, **PCA**.
- **Cyber-Security Examples:** Detecting zero-day attacks via anomaly detection, user behaviour analytics (UBA), botnet discovery.

#### 2.1.3 Reinforcement Learning (RL)
- An **agent** interacts with an **environment** and learns an optimal **policy** by receiving **rewards** or **penalties**.
- Goal: Maximise cumulative reward over time.
- **Cyber-Security Examples:** Autonomous threat-hunting agents, adaptive honeypots, automated penetration testing.

### 2.2 The ML Workflow (Pipeline)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** Draw and explain the standard ML pipeline. Marks are awarded for **sequencing** the steps correctly.

1. **Problem Definition** — Identify the cyber-security task (e.g., "Detect phishing URLs").
2. **Data Collection** — Gather logs, packets, malware samples.
3. **Data Pre-processing** — Handle missing values, normalise, encode categorical features.
4. **Feature Engineering / Selection** — Extract discriminative features (e.g., URL length, number of special characters).
5. **Model Selection** — Choose algorithm (e.g., SVM, Random Forest, Neural Network).
6. **Training** — Optimise model parameters using training data.
7. **Evaluation** — Test on unseen data using metrics (accuracy, F1, AUC).
8. **Deployment & Monitoring** — Deploy in SOC (Security Operations Center) and monitor drift.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and are the most-tested in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.

#### 2.3.1 Loss Functions (How the Model Measures Its Error)

**Mean Squared Error (MSE)** — used for regression:

$$
\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - \hat{y}_i \right)^2
$$

**Mean Absolute Error (MAE)** — robust to outliers:

$$
\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \right\vert
$$

**Binary Cross-Entropy (Log Loss)** — used for binary classification (e.g., attack vs. normal):

$$
L = -\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \hat{y}_i) \right]
$$

**Categorical Cross-Entropy** — used for multi-class classification (e.g., 5 types of attacks):

$$
L = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{i,c} \log(\hat{y}_{i,c})
$$

#### 2.3.2 Evaluation Metrics (How We Judge the Trained Model)

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}
$$

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

$$
\text{Recall (Sensitivity, TPR)} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

$$
F_1\text{-Score} = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}
$$

$$
\text{False Positive Rate (FPR)} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}
$$

#### 2.3.3 Bias-Variance Decomposition

$$
\mathbb{E}\!\left[ (y - \hat{f}(x))^2 \right] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2_{\text{irreducible}}
$$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These equations and concepts are the **backbone** of every modern Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) system, Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) tool, and Network Intrusion Detection System (NIDS). Engineers at companies like **Darktrace, CrowdStrike, and Palo Alto Networks** deploy models that are continuously evaluated using the metrics above, with F1-score and AUC-ROC being the most reported KPIs in security research papers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Gradient Descent Update Rule

Gradient Descent is the **optimisation algorithm** at the heart of nearly all ML model training. The derivation below is a **guaranteed KTU question** in either Part A (3 marks) or Part B (7 marks).

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal of the Derivation:** Find the weight $w$ that minimises the cost function $J(w)$.

**Step 1 — Define the cost function (MSE) for a linear model.**
Let the hypothesis be $h_w(x) = wx + b$. For a single training example, the squared error is:

$$
\text{Error} = \left( h_w(x_i) - y_i \right)^2
$$

**Step 2 — Sum over all $n$ training examples to form the cost function $J(w)$.**

$$
J(w) = \frac{1}{2n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( h_w(x_i) - y_i \right)^2
$$

> The factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ is a mathematical convenience (it cancels the exponent 2 during differentiation).

**Step 3 — Compute the partial derivative of $J(w)$ with respect to $w$.**

We apply the chain rule. Let $u = h_w(x_i) - y_i = wx_i + b - y_i$. Then $J = \frac{1}{2n} \sum u^2$.

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial w} = \frac{1}{2n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} 2 \cdot (h_w(x_i) - y_i) \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial w} (h_w(x_i) - y_i)
$$

Since $\frac{\partial}{\partial w}(wx_i + b - y_i) = x_i$, we obtain:

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial w} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( h_w(x_i) - y_i \right) \cdot x_i
$$

**Step 4 — Formulate the gradient descent update rule.**
We move $w$ in the **opposite direction** of the gradient, scaled by the **learning rate** $\alpha > 0$:

$$
w := w - \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial J}{\partial w}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the derivative to obtain the final update equation.**

$$
w := w - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( h_w(x_i) - y_i \right) \cdot x_i
$$

This is the **Batch Gradient Descent** update rule. Variants include:
- **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD):** Update after **each** example.
- **Mini-Batch GD:** Update after a **batch** of $k$ examples.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Confusion Matrix Worked Example (Cyber-Security Case)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** A numerical on a **2×2 confusion matrix** with derived metrics appears almost every semester.

**Scenario:** A malware detector is tested on 200 files: 100 are malicious (positive), 100 are benign (negative). The model produces:
- **90** True Positives (TP) — correctly flagged as malware.
- **80** True Negatives (TN) — correctly flagged as benign.
- **20** False Positives (FP) — benign flagged as malware.
- **10** False Negatives (FN) — malware missed.

**Step 1 — Verify totals.**

$$
\text{TP} + \text{FN} = 90 + 10 = 100 \quad \checkmark \quad (\text{Actual Positives})
$$

$$
\text{TN} + \text{FP} = 80 + 20 = 100 \quad \checkmark \quad (\text{Actual Negatives})
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Accuracy.**

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{90 + 80}{90 + 80 + 20 + 10} = \frac{170}{200} = 0.85 \; (85\%)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Precision (Of files flagged as malware, how many are truly malware?).**

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{90}{90 + 20} = \frac{90}{110} \approx 0.8182 \; (81.82\%)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Recall (Of all actual malware, how many did we catch?).**

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{90}{90 + 10} = \frac{90}{100} = 0.90 \; (90\%)
$$

**Step 5 — Compute F1-Score.**

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8182 \cdot 0.90}{0.8182 + 0.90} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7364}{1.7182} \approx 0.8571
$$

**Step 6 — Compute False Positive Rate (FPR).**

$$
\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{20}{20 + 80} = 0.20 \; (20\%)
$$

### 3.3 Bias-Variance Trade-off (Conceptual Derivation)

The expected prediction error of any model can be decomposed into three irreducible components:

$$
\mathbb{E}\!\left[ (y - \hat{f}(x))^2 \right] = \underbrace{\left( \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x) \right)^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\!\left[ (\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2 \right]}_{\text{Variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Noise}}
$$

- **High Bias, Low Variance** → **Underfitting** (model is too simple, e.g., linear regression on non-linear data).
- **Low Bias, High Variance** → **Overfitting** (model memorises training data, fails on test data).
- **Sweet Spot** → A model that generalises well.

In cyber security, an overfitted IDS raises too many **false alarms**, while an underfitted IDS misses real **zero-day attacks** — both are unacceptable.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The ML Pipeline in Cyber Defence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Data Source 1: Network Logs] --> P[Data Preprocessing]
    B[Data Source 2: Malware Samples] --> P
    C[Data Source 3: User Behaviour] --> P
    P --> P1[Cleaning and Normalisation]
    P1 --> P2[Feature Engineering]
    P2 --> P3[Train and Test Split]
    P3 --> M[Model Selection]
    M --> M1[Supervised: SVM, RF, XGBoost]
    M --> M2[Unsupervised: K-Means, Autoencoder]
    M --> M3[Reinforcement: Q-Learning]
    M1 --> T[Training Phase]
    M2 --> T
    M3 --> T
    T --> E[Evaluation Phase]
    E --> E1[Metrics: Accuracy, F1, AUC]
    E1 --> D[Deployment to SOC]
    D --> MO[Continuous Monitoring and Retraining]
    MO --> P
```

### 4.2 Diagram: Types of Machine Learning (Hierarchical Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    ML[Machine Learning] --> SL[Supervised Learning]
    ML --> UL[Unsupervised Learning]
    ML --> RL[Reinforcement Learning]
    SL --> SL1[Classification]
    SL --> SL2[Regression]
    SL1 --> SL1A[Binary: Attack or Normal]
    SL1 --> SL1B[Multiclass: DDoS, Phishing, Malware]
    UL --> UL1[Clustering: K-Means]
    UL --> UL2[Dimensionality Reduction: PCA]
    UL --> UL3[Anomaly Detection: Isolation Forest]
    RL --> RL1[Agent and Environment Loop]
    RL1 --> RL1A[Adaptive Honeypots]
    RL1 --> RL1B[Automated Pentesting]
```

### 4.3 Diagram: The Bias-Variance Trade-off (Conceptual Curve)

```mermaid
graph LR
    axisX[Model Complexity Increases] --> B[Bias Decreases]
    axisX --> V[Variance Increases]
    B --> U[Underfitting Region: High Bias, Low Variance]
    V --> O[Overfitting Region: Low Bias, High Variance]
    U --> S[Sweet Spot: Low Total Error]
    O --> S
```

### 4.4 Diagram: Supervised Learning Workflow (Input → Output Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Input Features X] --> H[Model Hypothesis h theta]
    H --> YP[Predicted Output y hat]
    Y[True Label y] --> C[Loss Function L]
    YP --> C
    C --> G[Gradient Computation]
    G --> U[Parameter Update theta]
    U --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Loop:** The supervised learning workflow is **iterative**. The model produces a prediction, the loss function compares it to the true label, the gradient is computed, and the parameters are updated. This cycle repeats for many **epochs** until the loss converges.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
*Define Machine Learning. List and briefly explain any two types of machine learning paradigms with a cyber-security example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Machine Learning is a field of computer science that gives computers the ability to learn from data and improve their performance on a task without being explicitly programmed (Mitchell, 1997).

1. **Supervised Learning:** The model is trained on labelled data $(X, y)$ to learn a mapping $X \to y$. *Cyber-security example:* Classification of network traffic as *Normal* or *Attack* using a labelled NSL-KDD dataset.
2. **Unsupervised Learning:** The model is given unlabelled data and discovers hidden structures. *Cyber-security example:* Detecting anomalous user behaviour in a corporate network using K-Means clustering.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the two types with examples.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Differentiate between **features** and **labels** in a machine learning dataset used for malware detection. Give one example of each from the cyber-security domain.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Feature (X):** A measurable input variable used by the model to make predictions. It is independent. *Example:* The byte-size of a file, the entropy of a PE section, the number of API calls made by a process.
- **Label (Y):** The output variable the model is trying to predict. It is dependent. *Example:* Whether a given file is *Malicious* (1) or *Benign* (0).

The model learns the function $f: X \to Y$, i.e., it learns to map input features to the correct output label.

*(1 mark for each correct definition, 1 mark for the example.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions carry **14 marks** and typically have **internal choice**. The two alternatives below are designed in the exact KTU ESE pattern (sub-parts of 7 + 7 marks).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Theory + Numerical**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Supervised, Unsupervised, and Reinforcement Learning** with suitable cyber-security examples. Discuss the role of labelled data in each paradigm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A phishing email detector was tested on 500 emails. The confusion matrix obtained is:

|  | Predicted Phishing | Predicted Legitimate |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Phishing** | 180 (TP) | 20 (FN) |
| **Actual Legitimate** | 30 (FP) | 270 (TN) |

Compute the **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and False Positive Rate**. Comment on whether this model is suitable for deployment in a corporate email gateway. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Comparison Table (5 marks) + Cyber-security example discussion (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Supervised | Unsupervised | Reinforcement |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data** | Labelled $(X, y)$ | Unlabelled $(X)$ | Scalar reward signal |
| **Goal** | Learn mapping $X \to y$ | Discover hidden structure | Maximise cumulative reward |
| **Cyber Example** | Spam classification using Naive Bayes | Zero-day anomaly detection via Isolation Forest | Adaptive honeypot that learns attacker behaviour |
| **Feedback** | Direct (loss vs. label) | None | Delayed (reward/penalty) |
| **Algorithms** | SVM, Random Forest, CNN | K-Means, PCA, Autoencoders | Q-Learning, DQN, PPO |

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Identify the values:
$\text{TP} = 180, \; \text{FN} = 20, \; \text{FP} = 30, \; \text{TN} = 270$.

Step 2 — Accuracy:

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{180 + 270}{180 + 20 + 30 + 270} = \frac{450}{500} = 0.90 \; (90\%)
$$

Step 3 — Precision:

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{180}{180 + 30} = \frac{180}{210} \approx 0.8571 \; (85.71\%)
$$

Step 4 — Recall:

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90 \; (90\%)
$$

Step 5 — F1-Score:

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8571 \times 0.90}{0.8571 + 0.90} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7714}{1.7571} \approx 0.878
$$

Step 6 — False Positive Rate:

$$
\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{30}{30 + 270} = \frac{30}{300} = 0.10 \; (10\%)
$$

**Comment (extra credit):** With **90% recall**, the model catches 9 out of 10 phishing emails, which is strong. However, the **10% FPR** means 1 in 10 legitimate emails may be falsely flagged, which could disrupt business. A higher precision is preferred in this context.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Pure Theory + Diagram**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Draw and explain the **standard Machine Learning pipeline** in detail, with a focus on a cyber-security application such as Network Intrusion Detection. Mention the role of feature engineering and model evaluation in the pipeline. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Bias-Variance Trade-off** in machine learning. How does it relate to the problems of **overfitting** and **underfitting**? Use a labelled diagram and discuss the strategies to achieve the optimal balance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) ML Pipeline (7 marks):**
The pipeline consists of 8 stages (see Section 4.1 of these notes):

1. **Problem Definition:** Identify the cyber task — e.g., "Detect DDoS attacks in real-time."
2. **Data Collection:** Gather network traffic from routers, firewalls, IDS logs.
3. **Data Pre-processing:** Handle missing values, remove duplicates, normalise numeric features.
4. **Feature Engineering:** Extract discriminative features such as *packet rate, source/destination IPs, protocol type, flow duration, payload entropy*.
5. **Model Selection:** Choose classifiers (Random Forest, XGBoost) for supervised detection, or Autoencoders for anomaly detection.
6. **Training:** Optimise model parameters on the training split.
7. **Evaluation:** Compute metrics (accuracy, F1, AUC). Use **k-fold cross-validation** to ensure robustness.
8. **Deployment & Monitoring:** Deploy in a SOC, monitor for **concept drift** (attack patterns changing), and retrain periodically.

**(b) Bias-Variance Trade-off (7 marks):**
- **Bias** is the error introduced by approximating a complex real-world phenomenon with a simplified model. **High bias** leads to **underfitting** — the model is too simple (e.g., linear regression on non-linear traffic data) and misses attack patterns.
- **Variance** is the model's sensitivity to fluctuations in the training data. **High variance** leads to **overfitting** — the model memorises training data, including noise, and fails on unseen attacks.

The total expected error is:

$$
\text{Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2_{\text{noise}}
$$

- **Underfitting** → High Bias, Low Variance.
- **Overfitting** → Low Bias, High Variance.
- **Optimal Model** → Trade-off minimising total error.

**Strategies to balance:**
- **Regularisation (L1/L2):** Penalises overly complex models.
- **Cross-Validation:** Ensures the model generalises across different data splits.
- **Early Stopping:** Halts training before the model overfits.
- **Ensemble Methods:** Bagging reduces variance, Boosting reduces bias.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in PECCT633:**
>
> 1. **Confusing features and labels:** Students often swap $X$ and $y$. Always remember — **features are inputs, labels are the answers you want the model to predict**.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{2}$ in MSE derivation:** This is a KTU board favourite. Examiners check whether you understand *why* the constant is included (to cancel the power of 2 in differentiation).
> 3. **Mixing up precision and recall:** Precision answers *"Of the items I flagged, how many were correct?"* Recall answers *"Of the actual positives, how many did I catch?"* In cyber security, **recall is usually more important than precision** because missing a real attack is costlier than raising a false alarm.
> 4. **Omitting units in formula sheets:** Always state that accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 are **dimensionless ratios** in the range $[0, 1]$.
> 5. **Not drawing diagrams in Part B:** Even a hand-drawn pipeline diagram (labelled clearly) can fetch 1–2 extra marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorise Before Exam:**

- ⭐ **ML Definition:** Learning from data to improve task performance without explicit programming (Mitchell, 1997).
- ⭐ **Three Paradigms:** Supervised (labelled), Unsupervised (unlabelled), Reinforcement (reward-based).
- ⭐ **Feature vs Label:** Feature = input column ($X$); Label = target column ($y$).
- ⭐ **MSE Formula:** $\frac{1}{n} \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ — used in regression.
- ⭐ **Cross-Entropy:** $-\sum y \log(\hat{y})$ — used in classification.
- ⭐ **Accuracy Formula:** $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ — use only on balanced datasets.
- ⭐ **Precision Formula:** $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ — measures prediction *correctness*.
- ⭐ **Recall Formula:** $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ — measures *coverage* of actual positives.
- ⭐ **F1-Score:** Harmonic mean of precision and recall — best for imbalanced cyber datasets.
- ⭐ **Bias-Variance:** High Bias = Underfitting; High Variance = Overfitting.
- ⭐ **Gradient Descent Update:** $w := w - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial w}$ — learn rate $\alpha$ is critical.
- ⭐ **ML Pipeline (8 steps):** Problem → Data → Preprocess → Features → Model → Train → Evaluate → Deploy.
- ⭐ **Cyber-Security Tie-Ins:** Spam filtering, malware detection, IDS, anomaly detection, UBA, adaptive honeypots.
- ⭐ **Train/Test Split:** Typically 80:20 or 70:30.
- ⭐ **Key Insight:** In cyber-security, **recall > precision** because missing an attack is costlier than a false alarm.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Machine Learning in the Cyber Security Domain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT633 - AI AND ML IN CYBER SECURITY DEFENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Applications of Machine Learning in the Cyber Security Domain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Machine Learning (ML) in Cyber Security** refers to the systematic application of computational algorithms and statistical models that iteratively learn patterns, behaviours, and anomalies from historical and real-time security data, enabling automated detection, classification, prediction, and response to cyber threats without being explicitly programmed for each new attack vector.

In the context of KTU's **PECCT633** curriculum, ML in cyber security is positioned as a **defensive, proactive, and adaptive** layer that augments traditional signature-based and rule-based security mechanisms (such as firewalls, antivirus engines, and Intrusion Detection Systems) with intelligent, data-driven decision-making.

The underlying mathematical premise is that legitimate network traffic, benign software behaviour, and normal user activities follow **statistically learnable distributions**, whereas malicious traffic, malware, and attacker behaviour constitute **deviations from these distributions** that can be modelled, detected, and classified.

### Key Terminology Used in the KTU 2024 Module

| Term | Definition (as per KTU Module 1) |
|---|---|
| **Feature Vector** | A numerical representation $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ of a network packet, system call, or log record |
| **Label** | A ground-truth class $y \in \{0, 1\}$ (e.g., 0 = Benign, 1 = Malicious) |
| **Training Set** | A labelled dataset $D = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ used to fit the model |
| **Threat Actor** | An entity responsible for an incident impacting the security of an information system |
| **Anomaly** | An observation that deviates so much from other observations as to arouse suspicion of malicious origin |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly emphasises that ML in cyber defence is **not a replacement** for traditional security controls, but rather an **augmentation layer** that brings adaptability, scale, and the ability to detect **zero-day attacks** — threats previously unseen by any signature database.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine a **veteran security guard** stationed at the entrance of a large corporate office for **20 years**. He has never read a single rulebook. Yet he can:
- Instantly tell whether a person walking in looks like a regular employee, a first-time visitor, or someone suspicious.
- Notice subtle cues — a person nervously glancing at the CCTV cameras, carrying an unfamiliar bag, loitering near a restricted door.
- Over time, he gets **better** at his job. Every false alarm (a genuine employee who looked unusual) and every near-miss (a thief he caught) tunes his intuition further.

Now imagine we can **clone that guard 10,000 times** and put a clone at every entry point, every server room, every endpoint device — and we can also feed each clone a **lifetime of all previous guards' experiences** simultaneously. That is essentially what Machine Learning does for cyber security.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Three Pillars" Intuition for Beginners:**
> 1. **Past data is wisdom** — ML learns from previously observed attacks and normal traffic.
> 2. **Patterns, not signatures** — Unlike antivirus that looks for an *exact* malicious byte sequence, ML identifies *characteristic behaviours*.
> 3. **Self-improving** — As new attacks emerge, the model is retrained and adapts, without a human writing a new rule.

---

## 1.3 Core Performance Metrics in Cyber ML (Highlighted Constants)

The following metrics are universally evaluated in any KTU-level cyber ML question and must be memorised:

- **True Positive (TP)**: Malicious event correctly flagged as malicious.
- **True Negative (TN)**: Benign event correctly identified as benign.
- **False Positive (FP)** — *Type I Error*: Benign event wrongly flagged as malicious (false alarm).
- **False Negative (FN)** — *Type II Error*: Malicious event missed by the system (the most dangerous error).

The single most important cyber-security-specific metric is the **False Positive Rate (FPR)**, because in a production SOC (Security Operations Centre), even a **1% FPR** can generate **thousands of false alerts per day**, drowning the analyst team.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Workflow — How ML is Actually Applied in Cyber Defence

The end-to-end application of ML in cyber security follows a structured, repeatable pipeline. The KTU 2024 module expects students to articulate each stage clearly.

### Stage 1 — Data Acquisition & Labelling

- **Network Traffic Capture**: Raw packets are captured via tools like **Wireshark**, **tcpdump**, or tap ports on switches. These are stored in **PCAP** format.
- **System Telemetry**: Endpoint logs, process trees, registry modifications, syscalls.
- **Threat Intelligence Feeds**: Curated IOCs (Indicators of Compromise) from sources like **MITRE ATT&CK**, **AlienVault OTX**, **MISP**.
- **Labelling**: Requires expert analysts or honeypot data; this is the **most expensive and slowest** step in the pipeline.

### Stage 2 — Feature Engineering

Raw data is rarely fed directly to ML models. Domain-specific features are crafted:

- **Flow-based features** (NetFlow, CICFlowMeter output): packet size statistics, inter-arrival time, flag counts.
- **Payload-based features** (for malware): byte entropy, opcode n-grams, PE header field values.
- **Behavioural features** (for ransomware): file modification rate, crypto API call frequency, disk write volume.

### Stage 3 — Model Selection & Training

The choice of model is dictated by the **problem type**:

| Cyber Security Problem | Problem Type | Suitable ML Family |
|---|---|---|
| Spam / Phishing Email Detection | Binary Classification | Naive Bayes, SVM, Logistic Regression |
| Network Intrusion Detection (known attacks) | Multi-class Classification | Random Forest, XGBoost, k-NN |
| Zero-Day Anomaly Detection | One-class / Outlier Detection | Isolation Forest, Autoencoders, One-Class SVM |
| Malware Family Classification | Multi-class Image Classification | CNN (treating binaries as greyscale images) |
| User Behaviour Analytics (Insider Threat) | Sequence / Time-series | LSTM, GRU, HMM |
| DDoS Detection at High Speed | Streaming Classification | Online Learning, Hoeffding Trees |

### Stage 4 — Evaluation & Threshold Tuning

Models are evaluated on a held-out test set using metrics (formulas in §2.2). Critically, in cyber security the **decision threshold** is tuned **not** at the default 0.5 probability, but at the operating point that minimises the cost-weighted combination of FPR and FNR.

### Stage 5 — Deployment & Continuous Learning

The model is deployed as part of a **SIEM** (Security Information & Event Management) or **SOAR** (Security Orchestration, Automation & Response) platform. As new attack samples arrive, the model is periodically **retrained** to combat **concept drift** — the phenomenon where the statistical properties of network traffic shift over time (e.g., new applications, new encryption standards, new attack techniques).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concept Drift** is a frequently examined KTU concept. It occurs when $P(Y \mid X)$ or $P(X)$ changes after deployment, causing model accuracy to degrade silently. Counter-measures include **periodic retraining**, **online learning algorithms**, and **drift detection tests** like ADWIN or Page-Hinkley.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All symbols below are universally accepted in the cyber ML literature and must be memorised for the KTU ESE.

| # | Metric / Concept | Mathematical Formula | Description & Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness. **Misleading on imbalanced data** (e.g., only 0.01% of traffic is malicious). |
| 2 | **Precision** (Positive Predictive Value) | $\text{Prec} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of all alerts raised, how many were real attacks. Critical when analyst time is scarce. |
| 3 | **Recall** (Detection Rate / Sensitivity / TPR) | $\text{Rec} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Of all real attacks, how many did the model catch. **The most important metric in intrusion detection.** |
| 4 | **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Probability of raising a false alarm. Must be kept low to avoid alert fatigue. |
| 5 | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}}$ | Harmonic mean of precision and recall. **Single best scalar metric for imbalanced cyber datasets.** |
| 6 | **Specificity** (True Negative Rate) | $\text{Spec} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = 1 - \text{FPR}$ | Of all benign events, how many were correctly let through. |
| 7 | **Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC)** | $\text{MCC} = \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | Robust to class imbalance. Range $\vert \text{MCC} \vert \leq 1$; 1 = perfect, 0 = random, -1 = total disagreement. |
| 8 | **ROC-AUC** | $\text{AUC} = \int_{0}^{1} \text{TPR}(\text{FPR}) \, d(\text{FPR})$ | Probability that a randomly chosen malicious sample is ranked higher than a randomly chosen benign one. |
| 9 | **Detection Cost** | $C = C_{FP} \cdot \text{FPR} + C_{FN} \cdot (1 - \text{Rec})$ | Cost-weighted operating metric. $C_{FN} \gg C_{FP}$ in cyber defence. |
| 10 | **Log Loss (Cross-Entropy)** | $L = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i) \right]$ | Penalises confident wrong predictions heavily; standard loss for probabilistic classifiers. |
| 11 | **Anomaly Score (Isolation Forest)** | $s(x, n) = 2^{-\frac{E(h(x))}{c(n)}}$ | Where $E(h(x))$ is the average path length to isolate $x$, and $c(n)$ is the average path length of an unsuccessful search in a BST. |
| 12 | **Reconstruction Error (Autoencoder)** | $\mathcal{L}(x) = \Vert x - \hat{x} \Vert_2^2$ | Used for zero-day detection. High reconstruction error $\Rightarrow$ anomaly. |
| 13 | **Shannon Entropy** (for payload randomness) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Encrypted/packed malware exhibits entropy close to **8.0** bits/byte. |
| 14 | **Benford's Law Deviation** (fraud detection) | $P(d) = \log_{10}\left(1 + \frac{1}{d}\right), \quad d \in \{1,\ldots,9\}$ | Used to detect anomalous financial transactions / fraud logs. |

### Real-World Engineering Utility of These Metrics

- **F1-Score** is the de-facto metric reported in academic cyber ML papers (NSL-KDD, CICIDS-2017, UNSW-NB15 datasets) and is the most likely KTU exam formula.
- **MCC** is increasingly preferred over F1 in industry (e.g., Microsoft, Kaspersky research) because it remains stable on heavily imbalanced data.
- **Reconstruction Error** and **Anomaly Score** are the workhorses of **zero-day threat hunting** and frequently appear in Module 1 as the bridge between supervised and unsupervised learning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Confusion Matrix to Cost-Sensitive Decision Threshold

In classical ML, a classifier outputs a probability $P(y=1 \mid x)$ and decides class 1 if this probability exceeds **0.5**. In cyber security, this is suboptimal because the **cost of missing an attack ($C_{FN}$) is orders of magnitude greater than the cost of a false alarm ($C_{FP}$)**. We now derive the optimal decision threshold.

### Step 1 — Expected Loss Formulation

For a single test instance, the model chooses between action $a_0$ (declare benign) and $a_1$ (declare malicious). The expected loss of choosing $a_1$ is:

$$L(a_1 \mid x) = C_{FP} \cdot (1 - P(y=1 \mid x)) + C_{TN} \cdot P(y=1 \mid x)$$

The expected loss of choosing $a_0$ is:

$$L(a_0 \mid x) = C_{FN} \cdot P(y=1 \mid x) + C_{TP} \cdot (1 - P(y=1 \mid x))$$

### Step 2 — Decision Rule

We choose $a_1$ (flag as malicious) if and only if $L(a_1 \mid x) \leq L(a_0 \mid x)$. Expanding and rearranging:

$$C_{FP} \cdot (1 - P) + C_{TN} \cdot P \leq C_{FN} \cdot P + C_{TP} \cdot (1 - P)$$

where $P \equiv P(y=1 \mid x)$ for brevity. Collecting the $P$ terms on one side and the constants on the other:

$$P \cdot (C_{TN} - C_{FN}) + C_{FP} - P \cdot (C_{FP} - C_{TP}) \leq 0$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad P \cdot (C_{TN} - C_{FN} - C_{FP} + C_{TP}) \leq -C_{FP} + C_{TP}$$

### Step 3 — Simplification Using Cost Assumptions

In the standard cyber security setting, $C_{TN} = C_{TP} = 0$ (correct decisions incur no cost). Substituting:

$$P \cdot (- C_{FN} - C_{FP}) \leq -C_{FP}$$

Multiplying both sides by $-1$ (and flipping the inequality):

$$P \cdot (C_{FN} + C_{FP}) \geq C_{FP}$$

### Step 4 — Final Threshold

$$\boxed{\; P(y=1 \mid x) \geq \frac{C_{FP}}{C_{FP} + C_{FN}} \;}$$

This is the **Bayes-optimal cost-sensitive threshold**. Notice that when $C_{FN} = C_{FP}$, the threshold reduces to $0.5$ (the classical ML default). In cyber security, with $C_{FN} \gg C_{FP}$, the threshold becomes very small — e.g., if $C_{FN} = 1000$ and $C_{FP} = 1$, the threshold becomes $1/1001 \approx 0.001$, meaning the model flags as malicious even on very weak evidence.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: ROC-AUC and its Probabilistic Interpretation

The **Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC)** has a beautiful probabilistic interpretation that is a frequent KTU question.

### Step 1 — Definition

The ROC curve plots $\text{TPR}(t)$ against $\text{FPR}(t)$ as the decision threshold $t$ varies from $+\infty$ to $-\infty$:

$$\text{AUC} = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \text{TPR}(t) \cdot f_{\text{benign}}(t) \, dt$$

where $f_{\text{benign}}(t)$ is the PDF of the classifier score on benign samples.

### Step 2 — Wilcoxon–Mann–Whitney Equivalence

It can be shown (Hanley & McNeil, 1982) that AUC equals the probability that the classifier assigns a **higher score to a randomly chosen positive instance than to a randomly chosen negative instance**:

$$\text{AUC} = P\!\left(S(x^+) > S(x^-)\right)$$

where $S(\cdot)$ is the classifier's score function, $x^+$ is a malicious sample, and $x^-$ is a benign sample.

### Step 3 — Estimator from a Test Set

Given $N_+$ malicious and $N_-$ benign samples in the test set, the empirical AUC is:

$$\widehat{\text{AUC}} = \frac{1}{N_+ \cdot N_-} \sum_{i=1}^{N_+} \sum_{j=1}^{N_-} \mathbb{1}\!\left[S(x_i^+) > S(x_j^-)\right]$$

where $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ is the indicator function. This estimator is unbiased and is the standard implementation in scikit-learn's `roc_auc_score`.

### Step 4 — Engineering Significance

- $\text{AUC} = 1.0$ → perfect separation.
- $\text{AUC} = 0.5$ → random guessing (no skill).
- $\text{AUC} < 0.5$ → model is *worse than random*; its predictions can be inverted to improve.
- $\text{AUC}$ is **invariant to class prior**, making it ideal for the highly imbalanced cyber datasets (e.g., malicious traffic may be 1 in 100,000 packets).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem**: A deployed ML-based Intrusion Detection System on a campus network produces the following confusion matrix over a 24-hour window:

- $TP = 180$, $FP = 20$, $FN = 20$, $TN = 99{,}780$

**Total samples** $N = 100{,}000$. Compute: (a) Accuracy, (b) Precision, (c) Recall, (d) F1-Score, (e) FPR, (f) MCC, (g) Cost with $C_{FP} = 1$ and $C_{FN} = 1000$.

### Solution

**(a) Accuracy:**

$$\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{N} = \frac{180 + 99{,}780}{100{,}000} = \frac{99{,}960}{100{,}000} = 0.9996$$

**(b) Precision:**

$$\text{Prec} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90$$

**(c) Recall:**

$$\text{Rec} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90$$

**(d) F1-Score:**

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.90 \cdot 0.90}{0.90 + 0.90} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.81}{1.80} = 0.90$$

**(e) FPR:**

$$\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{20}{20 + 99{,}780} = \frac{20}{99{,}800} \approx 2.004 \times 10^{-4}$$

**(f) MCC:**

$$\text{MCC} = \frac{(180)(99{,}780) - (20)(20)}{\sqrt{(200)(200)(99{,}800)(99{,}960)}}$$

Let us compute the numerator and denominator separately.

Numerator:

$$(180)(99{,}780) = 17{,}960{,}400$$

$$(20)(20) = 400$$

$$\text{Num} = 17{,}960{,}400 - 400 = 17{,}960{,}000$$

Denominator (using $\sqrt{a \cdot b \cdot c \cdot d}$):

$$(200)(200) = 40{,}000$$

$$(99{,}800)(99{,}960) \approx 9.9760 \times 10^{9}$$

$$\text{Product} = 40{,}000 \times 9.9760 \times 10^{9} = 3.9904 \times 10^{14}$$

$$\sqrt{3.9904 \times 10^{14}} \approx 1.9976 \times 10^{7}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{MCC} = \frac{1.7960 \times 10^{7}}{1.9976 \times 10^{7}} \approx 0.8991$$

**(g) Cost:**

$$C = C_{FP} \cdot \text{FPR} + C_{FN} \cdot \text{FNR}$$

$$\text{FNR} = 1 - \text{Rec} = 1 - 0.90 = 0.10$$

$$C = (1)(2.004 \times 10^{-4}) + (1000)(0.10) \approx 100.0002$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight**: The accuracy of **99.96%** is deceptively high — a naïve student would say "great model". But the FNR is **10%**, meaning **1 in 10 real attacks is missed**, which is unacceptable for a campus network. The F1-score and MCC give the truthful picture of model quality. This is the classic "accuracy paradox" in cyber security.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which ML Approach

| Cyber Security Task | Data Availability | Recommended Approach | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Known malware detection | Large labelled corpus | Supervised CNN / XGBoost | High accuracy; signatures available |
| Zero-day exploit detection | Few or no labels | Autoencoder / Isolation Forest | Learns "normal"; flags deviations |
| Insider threat (UEBA) | Long behavioural sequences | LSTM / Transformer | Captures temporal dependencies |
| Phishing URL classification | Large labelled dataset | Random Forest + lexical features | Fast, interpretable, robust |
| DDoS at line rate | Streaming data | Hoeffding Tree / Online SGD | Constant memory; no re-training needed |
| APT campaign attribution | Heterogeneous OSINT | Graph Neural Network (GNN) | Models relationships between IOCs |
| Encrypted traffic analysis | No payload visibility | CNN on raw packet bytes | Avoids decryption overhead |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End ML Pipeline for Cyber Threat Detection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Network Traffic / PCAP] --> B[Feature Extraction]
    B --> C{Normalisation and Encoding}
    C --> D[Training Dataset D]
    D --> E[Model Training Phase]
    E --> F[Trained ML Model]
    F --> G[Deployment in SIEM or SOAR]
    G --> H[Live Traffic Stream]
    H --> I[Real Time Feature Extraction]
    I --> J[Model Inference Engine]
    J --> K{Prediction Confidence}
    K --> L[Class Label 0 Benign]
    K --> M[Class Label 1 Malicious]
    M --> N[Alert to SOC Analyst]
    N --> O[Feedback Loop and Labelling]
    O --> D
```

**Reading the diagram**: Arrows represent data flow. The left half is offline training; the right half is online inference. The feedback loop at the bottom is what makes the system *self-improving* — confirmed alerts are added to the training set for the next retraining cycle.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of an ML-Based Intrusion Detection System (NIDS)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputLayer["Input Layer"]
        P1[Packet Capture from NIC]
        P2[NetFlow Collector]
        P3[System Syscall Logger]
    end

    subgraph Preprocess["Preprocessing Stage"]
        Q1[PCAP to Flow Records]
        Q2[Statistical Aggregation]
        Q3[Behavioural Encoding]
    end

    subgraph FeatureStore["Feature Engineering"]
        R1[CICFlowMeter Output]
        R2[Byte N-gram Features]
        R3[Process Behavioural Vectors]
    end

    subgraph ModelLayer["ML Inference Layer"]
        S1[Supervised Classifier RF XGBoost]
        S2[Anomaly Detector Autoencoder]
        S3[Ensemble Combiner]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer["Decision and Response"]
        T1[Threshold and Cost Sensitive Decision]
        T2[Alert Generation]
        T3[Dashboard and SIEM Push]
    end

    P1 --> Q1
    P2 --> Q2
    P3 --> Q3
    Q1 --> R1
    Q2 --> R1
    Q2 --> R2
    Q3 --> R3
    R1 --> S1
    R1 --> S2
    R2 --> S1
    R3 --> S3
    S1 --> S3
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
```

**Key components**:
- **Input Layer**: Three heterogeneous data sources ensure multi-modal coverage.
- **Model Layer** combines a **supervised classifier** (high precision on known attacks) with an **anomaly detector** (recall on zero-days), then an **ensemble combiner** merges their scores.
- **Output Layer** applies the cost-sensitive threshold derived in §3.1 before alerting.

---

## 4.3 Decision Flow: Cost-Sensitive Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample arrives x] --> B[Compute P y equals 1 given x]
    B --> C{Compare P to threshold C FP over C FP plus C FN}
    C -->|P greater or equal threshold| D[Flag as MALICIOUS and Generate Alert]
    C -->|P less than threshold| E[Mark as BENIGN and Log]
    D --> F[Append to Confirmed Alerts Pool]
    F --> G[Periodic Retraining Trigger]
    G --> H[Update Model Weights]
    H --> B
```

This diagram visually encodes the central insight from the §3.1 derivation — the threshold is not 0.5 but is determined by the relative cost of false positives and false negatives.

---

## 4.4 Use-Case Mapping of ML Algorithms to Cyber Defence Domains

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ML in Cyber Security Applications]

    A --> B[Network IDS]
    A --> C[Malware Analysis]
    A --> D[Phishing Detection]
    A --> E[Fraud and Insider Threat]
    A --> B1[Spam Filtering]
    A --> C1[Encrypted Traffic Analysis]
    A --> D1[Endpoint EDR]
    A --> E1[Threat Intelligence and IOC Mining]

    B --> B_algo[Random Forest, XGBoost, LSTM]
    C --> C_algo[CNN on Binary Images, GBT]
    D --> D_algo[Logistic Regression, SVM, BERT]
    E --> E_algo[Isolation Forest, Autoencoder, HMM]
    B1 --> B1_algo[Naive Bayes, k-NN]
    C1 --> C1_algo[CNN, Byte Level Transformers]
    D1 --> D1_algo[Random Forest, LightGBM]
    E1 --> E1_algo[Graph Neural Networks, NLP]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question in the KTU ESE demands a crisp, definition-oriented answer of roughly 80–120 words. **Rote-memorised definitions fetch full marks; vague answers fetch 1–1.5 marks only.**

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]
**Define Machine Learning in the context of cyber security. List any two application domains where ML is deployed for cyber defence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Machine Learning in cyber security is the application of computational algorithms that learn patterns from historical security data — network traffic, system logs, malware samples — to automatically detect, classify, and respond to threats without explicit programming for every attack. The two principal application domains are: (1) **Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS)**, where ML classifies packets as benign or malicious and detects zero-day attacks that evade signature-based systems; and (2) **Malware Analysis**, where deep learning models (e.g., CNNs applied to binary images) classify malware families and detect polymorphic variants. Other important domains include phishing email filtering, fraud detection, and User and Entity Behaviour Analytics (UEBA).

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]
**Differentiate between signature-based detection and anomaly-based detection. Which one is suitable for detecting zero-day attacks and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Signature-based detection matches incoming events against a database of known attack patterns (signatures, hashes, or rules). It produces very few false positives but **cannot detect novel (zero-day) attacks** because no signature exists for them. Anomaly-based detection, in contrast, builds a statistical or ML model of *normal* behaviour from historical data and flags any significant deviation as suspicious. Anomaly-based detection is **suitable for zero-day attacks** because it does not require prior knowledge of the attack; any malicious activity that deviates from the learnt normal profile triggers an alert. The trade-off is a higher false positive rate, which is mitigated by combining it with supervised classifiers and cost-sensitive thresholding.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B questions carry 14 marks and offer an internal choice between two questions. Each question is split into sub-parts (typically 7 + 7 marks). Students must answer **any one** of the two. A well-structured answer that uses diagrams, bullet points, and formulas consistently scores higher than a paragraph-only answer.

---

### QUESTION A — (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, Module 1]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the end-to-end pipeline of an ML-based Network Intrusion Detection System. Clearly label the offline training phase and the online inference phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A deployed ML-based IDS produces the following confusion matrix on a test set of 10,000 network flows: $TP = 240$, $FP = 60$, $FN = 40$, $TN = 9{,}660$. Compute the **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC)**. Comment on the suitability of accuracy as a metric for this problem. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The pipeline of an ML-based NIDS consists of two distinct halves:

**Offline Training Phase** (left side):
1. **Data Collection**: Network packets are captured using tools like Wireshark, tcpdump, or SPAN ports, producing PCAP files. Historical labelled datasets such as NSL-KDD, CICIDS-2017, or UNSW-NB15 are used.
2. **Feature Extraction**: Tools like CICFlowMeter convert raw packets into statistical flow records containing features such as packet size, inter-arrival time, flag counts, and protocol type.
