3. **Preprocessing**: Features are normalised (e.g., Min-Max or Z-score), categorical fields are one-hot encoded, and missing values are imputed.
4. **Model Training**: A supervised classifier (Random Forest, XGBoost) or anomaly detector (Isolation Forest, Autoencoder) is trained on the labelled dataset.
5. **Evaluation**: The model is validated using k-fold cross-validation and the metrics in §3.1.

**Online Inference Phase** (right side):
1. **Live Capture**: Packets are captured in real time from a tap or SPAN port.
2. **Online Feature Extraction**: The same CICFlowMeter pipeline runs in streaming mode.
3. **Inference**: The trained model scores each flow and outputs $P(y=1 \mid x)$.
4. **Cost-Sensitive Thresholding**: The threshold from §3.1 is applied to decide benign vs. malicious.
5. **Alerting & Feedback**: Alerts are pushed to the SIEM; analyst-confirmed alerts are added to the labelled set for periodic retraining (closed loop).

**Diagram**: A flowchart is drawn (see §4.1) showing the two phases connected by the model artefact, with a feedback loop from inference back to training.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

Given: $TP = 240$, $FP = 60$, $FN = 40$, $TN = 9{,}660$, $N = 10{,}000$.

**(1) Accuracy:**

$$\text{Acc} = \frac{240 + 9{,}660}{10{,}000} = \frac{9{,}900}{10{,}000} = 0.9900$$

**(2) Precision:**

$$\text{Prec} = \frac{240}{240 + 60} = \frac{240}{300} = 0.8000$$

**(3) Recall:**

$$\text{Rec} = \frac{240}{240 + 40} = \frac{240}{280} \approx 0.8571$$

**(4) F1-Score:**

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8000 \times 0.8571}{0.8000 + 0.8571} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.6857}{1.6571} \approx 0.8276$$

**(5) MCC:**

$$\text{MCC} = \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$$

Numerator:

$$(240)(9{,}660) = 2{,}318{,}400$$

$$(60)(40) = 2{,}400$$

$$\text{Num} = 2{,}318{,}400 - 2{,}400 = 2{,}316{,}000$$

Denominator terms:

$$TP+FP = 300, \quad TP+FN = 280, \quad TN+FP = 9{,}720, \quad TN+FN = 9{,}700$$

$$\text{Product} = 300 \times 280 \times 9{,}720 \times 9{,}700$$

Computing step-by-step:

$$300 \times 280 = 84{,}000$$

$$9{,}720 \times 9{,}700 = 94{,}284{,}000$$

$$84{,}000 \times 94{,}284{,}000 = 7.92 \times 10^{12}$$

$$\sqrt{7.92 \times 10^{12}} \approx 2.814 \times 10^{6}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{MCC} = \frac{2.316 \times 10^{6}}{2.814 \times 10^{6}} \approx 0.823$$

**Comment on accuracy**: The accuracy of 99% is misleadingly high because the dataset is heavily imbalanced — only 280 flows out of 10,000 are malicious (2.8%). A trivial classifier that labels every flow as benign would achieve 97.2% accuracy. Precision, Recall, F1, and MCC give a much more honest assessment of the model's true detection capability. This illustrates the **accuracy paradox** in cyber security datasets, which is why KTU examiners expect students to compute multiple metrics and critically discuss them.

---

### QUESTION B — (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, Module 1]**
**(a)** Explain the **Confusion Matrix** and derive the expressions for **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and False Positive Rate**. State one engineering limitation of accuracy as an evaluation metric in cyber security. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Supervised, Unsupervised, and Reinforcement Learning** paradigms in the context of cyber threat detection. Give one specific use case of each in cyber defence. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

A **Confusion Matrix** is a $2 \times 2$ table that summarises the performance of a binary classifier on a labelled test set. The four cells are: **True Positive (TP)** — attack correctly flagged; **True Negative (TN)** — benign correctly let through; **False Positive (FP)** — benign wrongly flagged (false alarm); **False Negative (FN)** — attack missed.

**Derivations** (each metric is derived from the four cells of the confusion matrix):

The total number of test samples is $N = TP + TN + FP + FN$.

**Accuracy** is the fraction of all correct decisions:

$$\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

**Precision** is the fraction of positive predictions that are correct:

$$\text{Prec} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

**Recall** (also Detection Rate or TPR) is the fraction of actual positives that were caught:

$$\text{Rec} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

**F1-Score** is the harmonic mean of precision and recall, designed so that it is high only when both are high:

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}}$$

**False Positive Rate** is the fraction of actual negatives that were wrongly flagged:

$$\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$$

**Engineering limitation of accuracy**: In real cyber security datasets, the number of malicious events is often less than **0.1%** of total traffic. A degenerate classifier that always predicts "benign" achieves 99.9% accuracy but is completely useless. Accuracy treats false positives and false negatives symmetrically, but in cyber defence missing an attack (FN) is catastrophic while a false alarm (FP) is merely inconvenient. Hence, **F1, MCC, or cost-sensitive metrics** must be used instead.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

| Paradigm | Learning Signal | Cyber Use Case | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Supervised Learning** | Labelled examples $(x, y)$ | Malware family classification using CNN on greyscale binary images | The MalImg dataset provides thousands of labelled malware binaries; CNN learns family-specific texture patterns. |
| **Unsupervised Learning** | No labels; learns the structure of $X$ | Zero-day anomaly detection using Isolation Forest or Autoencoders on network flows | No prior examples of novel attacks are needed; the model learns "normal" and flags deviations. |
| **Reinforcement Learning** | Reward signal from environment | Autonomous cyber defence agent that learns optimal response actions in a simulated SOC | The agent receives +reward for correctly blocking an attack and -reward for false positives; over many episodes it learns an optimal defence policy. |

**Supervised learning** is best when large labelled datasets exist (e.g., CICIDS-2017 for intrusion detection, MalImg for malware). It achieves high accuracy on **known** attacks but cannot detect novel ones.

**Unsupervised learning** excels at **zero-day detection** because it does not require labels. Autoencoders are trained only on benign traffic; the reconstruction error for malicious traffic is significantly higher, triggering an alert. Isolation Forest works by randomly selecting features and split values; anomalies are isolated in fewer splits, giving them shorter path lengths.

**Reinforcement learning** is an emerging paradigm where an **agent** (e.g., a software-defined networking controller) takes **actions** (block IP, throttle traffic, isolate host) in an **environment** (the network) and receives **rewards** (reduced risk, fewer false positives). Over thousands of simulated attack scenarios, the agent learns a near-optimal defence policy. Notable frameworks include **MITRE's Caldera** and **OpenAI Gym**-based cyber simulators.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
> 1. **Confusion Matrix Reversal**: A common mistake is to swap FP and FN. Remember: **F**alse **P**ositive = *benign wrongly flagged* (analyst wasted); **F**alse **N**egative = *attack missed* (catastrophic).
> 2. **F1 ≠ Average of Precision and Recall**: It is the *harmonic* mean, not the arithmetic mean. Always show the substitution.
> 3. **Forgetting the Square Root in MCC**: The denominator has a $\sqrt{}$ over the product of four sums; do not drop it.
> 4. **Cost-Sensitive Threshold ≠ 0.5**: If a question mentions asymmetric costs, **derive** the threshold from $C_{FP}/(C_{FP}+C_{FN})$ and explicitly state it is *not* the default 0.5.
> 5. **Diagram Labelling**: Block diagrams without labelled arrows fetch 1–2 marks less than fully labelled ones. Always write the *data type* on each arrow (e.g., "PCAP", "Feature Vector", "Score").
> 6. **Forgetting the Cyber Context**: Students often write generic ML answers. Always tie the answer back to cyber security (mention attacks, defenders, false alarm cost, etc.) for full marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following bullet points are the **highest-yield items** for the KTU ESE Module 1 viva and written exam. Memorise them thoroughly.

- [x] **ML in cyber security** is a *defensive* augmentation of traditional signature-based systems, not a replacement.
- [x] **Signature-based vs. Anomaly-based** — the former is precise but cannot detect zero-days; the latter detects zero-days but has higher FPR.
- [x] **Confusion Matrix** has 4 cells: TP, FP, FN, TN. Memorise the meaning of each.
- [x] **Accuracy is misleading** on imbalanced data. Always compute F1, MCC, or cost.
- [x] **Precision** = "Of alerts raised, how many are real?" Critical for analyst workload.
- [x] **Recall (Detection Rate / TPR)** = "Of all attacks, how many did we catch?" **Most important metric in IDS**.
- [x] **FPR** = $FP / (FP + TN)$ — must be kept low to prevent alert fatigue.
- [x] **F1-Score** = harmonic mean of precision and recall. Single best scalar for imbalanced cyber data.
- [x] **MCC** ranges from -1 to +1; robust to class imbalance. Preferred by industry.
- [x] **ROC-AUC** = probability that a random malicious sample is scored higher than a random benign one.
- [x] **Cost-sensitive threshold** = $C_{FP} / (C_{FP} + C_{FN})$. When $C_{FN} \gg C_{FP}$, the threshold is very small (e.g., 0.001).
- [x] **Three ML paradigms**: Supervised (labelled, known attacks), Unsupervised (no labels, zero-days), Reinforcement (reward-based, autonomous response).
- [x] **Deep Learning** in cyber: CNN on binary images (malware), LSTM on system call sequences (UEBA), Transformers on URL strings (phishing).
- [x] **Datasets** to know by name: **NSL-KDD, CICIDS-2017, UNSW-NB15, MalImg**. These are the standard benchmarks cited in KTU reference material.
- [x] **Concept Drift** = $P(Y \mid X)$ or $P(X)$ changes after deployment; mitigated by periodic retraining or online learning.
- [x] **Reconstruction error** of an autoencoder is the standard anomaly score for zero-day detection.
- [x] **Shannon entropy** of a packed/encrypted payload is close to **8.0 bits/byte** — used as a feature in malware detection.
- [x] **MITRE ATT&CK** is the standard knowledge base for labelling adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs).
- [x] **Adversarial ML**: attackers can craft inputs to fool ML models (e.g., adversarial malware). This is a rapidly growing sub-field and may appear as a higher-order KTU question.
- [x] **Pipeline order**: Capture → Extract → Preprocess → Train → Evaluate → Deploy → Monitor → Retrain. Memorise this sequence.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machine Learning Tasks and Approaches: Supervised vs. Unsupervised Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT633 - AI AND ML IN CYBER SECURITY DEFENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Machine Learning Tasks and Approaches: Supervised vs. Unsupervised Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🧠 Module 1 — Machine Learning Tasks and Approaches

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Machine Learning (ML) Task?

A **Machine Learning (ML) Task** is a well-defined computational problem where an algorithm learns a mapping from inputs to outputs (or discovers hidden structure in data) by optimizing a performance criterion using example data, without being explicitly programmed with rule-based logic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECCT633):**
> Machine Learning tasks are broadly categorized by the **nature of the learning signal** (supervision) available to the learner and the **type of output** the system is expected to produce (prediction, grouping, transformation, etc.).

The four canonical ML task families recognized in the KTU 2024 Scheme curriculum are:

| ML Task Family | Output Type | Example Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Classification** | Discrete class label | Spam vs. Ham email detection |
| **Regression** | Continuous real value | Predicting DDoS attack traffic volume |
| **Clustering** | Group IDs (no labels) | Grouping similar malware families |
| **Dimensionality Reduction** | Lower-dim representation | Feature compression for IDS datasets |

### 1.2 Supervised Learning — Formal Definition

**Supervised Learning** is the ML paradigm in which the model is trained on a dataset consisting of **input–output pairs** $\{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$, where $y^{(i)}$ is the *ground-truth label* provided by an external "supervisor" (oracle/annotator). The goal is to learn a function $f_\theta : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ that minimizes a loss function $\mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x), y)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Guided Tutor**
> Imagine a medical student learning X-ray diagnosis. The professor shows 10,000 X-rays **along with the correct diagnosis label** for each. After enough examples, the student can predict diagnoses for *new* unseen X-rays. The label is the "supervisor." This is supervised learning.

**Cyber Security Example:** Training a Random Forest classifier on labeled network flows (each flow tagged `Normal` or `Attack`) to detect intrusions in real time.

### 1.3 Unsupervised Learning — Formal Definition

**Unsupervised Learning** is the ML paradigm in which the model is trained on a dataset consisting of **only inputs** $\{x^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{N}$, with **no ground-truth labels** available. The algorithm must discover intrinsic structure — clusters, anomalies, manifolds, or low-dimensional projections — purely from the statistical properties of the data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Self-Organizing Detective**
> A detective is handed a vault of 1 million intercepted packets with **no** annotations saying "this one is malicious." By finding packets that *look statistically strange* (e.g., unusual port patterns, anomalous payload sizes), the detective flags potential intrusions. No teacher — the detective learns by spotting *deviations* and *similarities*. This is unsupervised learning.

**Cyber Security Example:** Using K-Means clustering to group network events and identifying outlier clusters as candidate zero-day attacks.

### 1.4 Why This Distinction Matters in Cyber Defence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In real-world SOC (Security Operations Center) environments, **labeled attack data is rare and expensive** (analyst hours cost money, novel attacks have no labels). Hence, understanding *when* to use supervised vs. unsupervised methods is a **high-weight KTU exam concept** and a critical engineering skill.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Workflow — Supervised Learning

The supervised pipeline executes in **5 structured stages**:

1. **Data Acquisition:** Collect labeled dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$
2. **Preprocessing:** Clean, normalize, encode categorical variables, handle imbalance via SMOTE.
3. **Model Selection:** Choose hypothesis class $f_\theta$ (e.g., SVM, Decision Tree, Neural Network).
4. **Training:** Optimize parameters $\theta$ by minimizing empirical risk:
   $$\hat{\theta} = \arg\min_{\theta} \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathcal{L}\left(f_\theta(x^{(i)}),\, y^{(i)}\right)$$
5. **Evaluation & Deployment:** Test on held-out set; metrics: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, AUC-ROC.

## 2.2 Operational Workflow — Unsupervised Learning

1. **Data Acquisition:** Collect unlabeled dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{N}$.
2. **Preprocessing:** Feature scaling (StandardScaler, MinMax) is **critical** because distance-based algorithms are scale-sensitive.
3. **Model Selection:** Choose structure-discovery algorithm (K-Means, DBSCAN, PCA, Autoencoder).
4. **Pattern Discovery:** Algorithm optimizes an *intrinsic objective* (e.g., within-cluster sum of squares, reconstruction error).
5. **Interpretation:** Human-in-the-loop labels the discovered clusters/anomalies.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Approach

| Aspect | Supervised | Unsupervised |
|---|---|---|
| **Why use it?** | High accuracy on known attack signatures | Detect novel/zero-day threats |
| **Why labeled?** | Provides explicit error signal via loss | No labels exist for new attacks |
| **Why optimize loss?** | Directly minimizes prediction error | Optimizes a structural criterion (e.g., WCSS, reconstruction) |
| **Why care in cyber security?** | Signature-based IDS, malware classification | Anomaly detection, behavior profiling |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 Scheme board exams. Memorize the formulations, loss terms, and update rules.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Empirical Risk | $R_{emp}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x^{(i)}), y^{(i)})$ | Average loss over training set |
| 2 | MSE Loss (Regression) | $\mathcal{L}_{MSE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^{2}$ | Penalizes squared error |
| 3 | Cross-Entropy Loss (Classification) | $\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c^{(i)} \log(\hat{y}_c^{(i)})$ | Standard for multi-class |
| 4 | K-Means Objective (Unsupervised) | $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K}\sum_{x^{(i)} \in C_k}\Vert x^{(i)} - \mu_k \Vert^{2}$ | Within-cluster sum of squares |
| 5 | PCA Reconstruction (Unsupervised) | $\min_{W}\Vert X - XWW^{T} \Vert_{F}^{2}$ s.t. $W^{T}W = I$ | Minimize reconstruction error |
| 6 | Gradient Descent Update | $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ | $\eta$ = learning rate |
| 7 | Anomaly Score (Isolation Forest) | $s(x,n) = 2^{-\frac{E(h(x))}{c(n)}}$ | $E(h(x))$ avg path length |
| 8 | Information Gain (Decision Tree) | $IG(D, A) = H(D) - H(D \mid A)$ | Reduction in entropy |
| 9 | Entropy | $H(D) = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c \log_2 p_c$ | Impurity measure |
| 10 | Silhouette Score (Cluster Eval) | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i),\, b(i)\}}$ | $a$ = intra, $b$ = inter cluster |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The **Cross-Entropy loss** and **Anomaly Score** are the two most-tested formulas in PECCT633 exams. Cross-entropy drives nearly every classifier (spam filters, malware classifiers), while the anomaly score is foundational to modern unsupervised IDS systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) for Supervised Learning

**Starting Premise:** We seek parameters $\theta$ that minimize the expected loss over the true (unknown) data distribution $p(x,y)$:

$$R(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim p}\left[\mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x), y)\right]$$

Since $p(x,y)$ is unknown, we approximate it using the empirical distribution over the training set $\mathcal{D} = \{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$.

**Step 1 — Replace expectation with sample mean** (Law of Large Numbers assumption):

$$R(\theta) \approx \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathcal{L}\left(f_\theta(x^{(i)}), y^{(i)}\right) \equiv R_{emp}(\theta)$$

**Step 2 — Define the optimization problem:**

$$\hat{\theta} = \arg\min_{\theta} R_{emp}(\theta)$$

**Step 3 — Take the gradient and set to zero (for convex losses):**

$$\nabla_{\theta} R_{emp}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}\left(f_\theta(x^{(i)}), y^{(i)}\right) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Apply gradient descent iteratively:**

$$\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \cdot \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}\left(f_\theta(x^{(i)}), y^{(i)}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Convergence criterion:** Stop when $\Vert \theta^{(t+1)} - \theta^{(t)} \Vert_2 < \epsilon$ for a small tolerance $\epsilon$ (e.g., $10^{-4}$).

## 3.2 Derivation: K-Means Objective (Unsupervised)

**Step 1 — Define cluster centroids:** $\mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots, \mu_K$ in feature space $\mathbb{R}^{d}$.

**Step 2 — Define assignment function:** Each point $x^{(i)}$ is assigned to cluster $C_{c(i)}$ where:
$$c(i) = \arg\min_{k \in \{1,\ldots,K\}} \Vert x^{(i)} - \mu_k \Vert^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Define objective (WCSS — Within-Cluster Sum of Squares):**

$$J(\mu, c) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\Vert x^{(i)} - \mu_{c(i)} \Vert^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Alternating optimization (Lloyd's Algorithm):**
- **E-step (Expectation):** Fix centroids, assign each point to nearest centroid using rule in Step 2.
- **M-step (Maximization):** Fix assignments, recompute centroid as mean of assigned points:
  $$\mu_k \leftarrow \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert}\sum_{x^{(i)} \in C_k} x^{(i)}$$

**Step 5 — Convergence:** $J$ is monotonically non-increasing, and bounded below by $0$; hence converges to a local minimum.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Supervised vs. Unsupervised

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important comparison table** for KTU 2024 Scheme board exams on this topic.

| Dimension | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Requirement** | Labeled $(x, y)$ pairs | Unlabeled $x$ only |
| **Goal** | Learn $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ | Discover $p(x)$ or cluster structure |
| **Loss Signal** | Ground-truth label $y$ | No labels; intrinsic criterion only |
| **Output** | Prediction (class/real value) | Cluster ID, anomaly score, embedding |
| **Evaluation** | Accuracy, F1, AUC-ROC | Silhouette, Davies–Bouldin, reconstruction error |
| **Cyber Use Case** | Malware classification, spam detection | Zero-day anomaly detection, behavior clustering |
| **Limitation** | Costly labeling; weak on novel attacks | Harder to evaluate; less precise |
| **Examples** | SVM, Random Forest, CNN, LSTM | K-Means, DBSCAN, PCA, Autoencoder |
| **Overfitting Risk** | Higher (memorizes labels) | Lower (structure learning) |
| **KTU Exam Weight** | ~60% of question | ~40% of question |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Supervised Learning Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawData["Raw Labeled Dataset X and Y"]
    preprocess["Preprocessing Stage Normalization Encoding"]
    splitData["Train Test Split 80 20 Ratio"]
    modelTrain["Model Training Minimize Loss Function"]
    paramUpdate["Parameter Update via Gradient Descent"]
    modelEval["Model Evaluation Accuracy F1 AUC"]
    deploy["Deployment in IDS or SOC"]
    newInput["New Unseen Input X test"]
    prediction["Predicted Label Y hat"]

    rawData --> preprocess
    preprocess --> splitData
    splitData --> modelTrain
    modelTrain --> paramUpdate
    paramUpdate --> modelTrain
    modelTrain --> modelEval
    modelEval --> deploy
    deploy --> newInput
    newInput --> prediction

    classDef stageNode fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef outputNode fill:#065f46,stroke:#10b981,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    class rawData,preprocess,splitData,modelTrain,paramUpdate,modelEval,deploy,newInput stageNode
    class prediction outputNode
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Unsupervised Learning Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawUnlabeled["Raw Unlabeled Network Data"]
    cleanPre["Cleaning and Scaling Critical Step"]
    algoChoice["Algorithm Selection KMeans DBSCAN PCA Autoencoder"]
    patternMine["Pattern Discovery Cluster or Anomaly"]
    evalMetric["Evaluation via Silhouette and Reconstruction Error"]
    analystReview["Human Analyst Labels Discovered Groups"]
    actionOutput["Actionable Threat Intelligence"]

    rawUnlabeled --> cleanPre
    cleanPre --> algoChoice
    algoChoice --> patternMine
    patternMine --> evalMetric
    evalMetric --> analystReview
    analystReview --> actionOutput

    classDef inputNode fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#f97316,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef processNode fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef outputNode fill:#065f46,stroke:#10b981,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    class rawUnlabeled inputNode
    class cleanPre,algoChoice,patternMine,evalMetric,analystReview processNode
    class actionOutput outputNode
```

## 4.3 Architectural Comparison — Supervised vs. Unsupervised

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SUP["SUPERVISED LEARNING PIPELINE"]
        s1["Input Features X"] --> s2["Model f theta"]
        ytrue["Ground Truth Y"] --> loss["Loss Function L"]
        s2 --> loss
        loss --> opt["Optimizer Updates theta"]
        opt --> s2
    end

    subgraph UNSUP["UNSUPERVISED LEARNING PIPELINE"]
        u1["Input Features X"] --> u2["Model g phi"]
        u2 --> u3["Intrinsic Objective J phi"]
        u3 --> uopt["Update phi to Minimize J"]
        uopt --> u2
    end

    classDef supClass fill:#1e40af,stroke:#60a5fa,color:#ffffff
    classDef unsupClass fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fb923c,color:#ffffff
    class s1,s2,ytrue,loss,opt,uopt supClass
    class u1,u2,u3 unsupClass
```

## 4.4 Application Mapping — Cyber Security Use Cases

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((ML in Cyber Security))
    Supervised Tasks
      Malware Classification
      Spam Email Filtering
      Phishing URL Detection
      Intrusion Classification
    Unsupervised Tasks
      Zero Day Anomaly Detection
      User Behavior Profiling
      Network Traffic Clustering
      Outlier Botnet Discovery
    Hybrid Semi Supervised
      Active Learning for SOC
      Self Training IDS
      Pseudo Label Generation
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Scheme]**
**Differentiate between Supervised and Unsupervised Learning with one example each from the cyber security domain. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Type** | Labeled data $(x, y)$ | Unlabeled data $x$ only |
| **Objective** | Learn mapping $f(x) \to y$ | Discover hidden patterns/groups |
| **Feedback** | Error signal from label | No external feedback |
| **Cyber Example** | Classifying emails as spam/ham using Naive Bayes | Detecting anomalous network traffic using K-Means |
| **Evaluation** | Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1 | Silhouette score, reconstruction error |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023]**
**State and briefly explain the Empirical Risk Minimization principle. Why is it central to supervised learning? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM)** principle states that the optimal model parameters $\hat{\theta}$ are those that minimize the average loss over the training set:

$$\hat{\theta} = \arg\min_{\theta} \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathcal{L}\left(f_\theta(x^{(i)}), y^{(i)}\right)$$

It is central to supervised learning because:
1. The true data distribution $p(x,y)$ is unknown.
2. ERM provides a **tractable, computable proxy** for the true expected risk.
3. By the **Law of Large Numbers**, as $N \to \infty$, the empirical risk converges to the true risk, guaranteeing generalization (with appropriate regularization).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Pattern, 2024 Scheme]

**Q.A. (a)** Explain the workflow of Supervised Learning with a neat block diagram. Discuss any **two supervised algorithms** used in cyber security with their working principles. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Workflow of Supervised Learning (5 points):**
1. Collection of labeled dataset $(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$.
2. Preprocessing: normalization, encoding, handling imbalance.
3. Splitting into training, validation, and test sets.
4. Model training: optimize $\theta$ by minimizing loss via gradient descent.
5. Evaluation on held-out test set using accuracy, F1, AUC-ROC.

**Algorithm 1 — Support Vector Machine (SVM):**
- Finds the hyperplane that maximally separates classes in feature space.
- Decision function: $f(x) = \text{sign}(w^{T}x + b)$.
- Maximizes margin: $\max \frac{2}{\Vert w \Vert}$ subject to $y^{(i)}(w^{T}x^{(i)} + b) \geq 1$.
- **Cyber Use:** Malware classification based on opcode frequency vectors.

**Algorithm 2 — Random Forest:**
- Ensemble of decision trees trained on bootstrapped samples.
- Final prediction = majority vote (classification) or average (regression).
- **Cyber Use:** Network intrusion detection with high-dimensional flow features.

**Q.A. (b)** With suitable equations, derive the K-Means clustering algorithm. Explain its use in detecting anomalous network traffic. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**K-Means Derivation (4 points):**
1. Initialize $K$ centroids $\mu_1, \ldots, \mu_K$ randomly.
2. **Assignment step:** $c^{(i)} = \arg\min_k \Vert x^{(i)} - \mu_k \Vert^{2}$.
3. **Update step:** $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert}\sum_{x^{(i)} \in C_k} x^{(i)}$.
4. **Objective:** Minimize WCSS $J = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\Vert x^{(i)} - \mu_{c^{(i)}} \Vert^{2}$.
5. Iterate steps 2–3 until centroids stabilize.

**Use in Anomaly Detection (3 points):**
1. Cluster historical network flows into $K$ behavior clusters.
2. For new flows, compute distance to nearest centroid: $d(x) = \min_k \Vert x - \mu_k \Vert$.
3. If $d(x) > \tau$ (a calibrated threshold), flag as anomaly → candidate zero-day attack.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**Q.B. (a)** Discuss the key challenges in applying Unsupervised Learning to cyber security. Compare **three unsupervised algorithms** with their working mechanisms. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Challenges (3 points):**
1. **No labels for validation** — evaluation metrics are indirect.
2. **Curse of dimensionality** — distance metrics degrade in high dimensions.
3. **Concept drift** — network behavior evolves, so clusters may become stale.
4. **High false positive rate** — rare legitimate events flagged as anomalies.

**Three Unsupervised Algorithms (4 points):**

| Algorithm | Mechanism | Cyber Use |
|---|---|---|
| **K-Means** | Partition data into $K$ spherical clusters; minimize WCSS | Behavior baseline modeling |
| **DBSCAN** | Density-based clustering; points in low-density regions = outliers | Outlier-based IDS |
| **Autoencoder** | Neural net reconstructs input; high reconstruction error = anomaly | Zero-day malware detection |

**Q.B. (b)** Explain the Cross-Entropy loss function used in supervised classification. Show how gradient descent updates parameters to minimize this loss. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Cross-Entropy Loss Definition (3 points):**
For $C$ classes and $N$ samples:
$$\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c^{(i)} \log(\hat{y}_c^{(i)})$$

where $\hat{y}_c^{(i)}$ is the predicted probability (via softmax): $\hat{y}_c = \frac{e^{z_c}}{\sum_{j=1}^{C} e^{z_j}}$.

**Gradient Descent Derivation (4 points):**
1. Compute gradient: $\nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}_{CE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) \cdot x^{(i)}$ (for linear case).
2. Update rule:
   $$\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}_{CE}(\theta^{(t)})$$
3. Learning rate $\eta$ controls step size; too large → divergence, too small → slow convergence.
4. Iterate until $\Vert \theta^{(t+1)} - \theta^{(t)} \Vert < \epsilon$.

**Cyber Security Application:** Training a deep neural network classifier to distinguish ransomware vs. benign software based on API call sequences.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing clustering with classification** — students often write "K-Means classifies data." K-Means *clusters*; the cluster ID has no inherent class meaning.
> 2. **Forgetting the "no labels" property of unsupervised learning** — examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you imply any labeled supervision.
> 3. **Missing the loss function in supervised derivations** — always write the explicit loss equation and gradient.
> 4. **Not citing a cyber security example** — PECCT633 demands cyber context; generic ML answers lose marks.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram** — in 7-mark sub-questions, a labeled workflow diagram is worth **1–2 marks minimum**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme PECCT633 Module 1**

- ✅ **Supervised Learning** = Labeled data, learns $f: X \to Y$, uses loss with ground truth.
- ✅ **Unsupervised Learning** = Unlabeled data, discovers structure (clusters, anomalies, manifolds).
- ✅ **Key Formula (ERM):** $\hat{\theta} = \arg\min_{\theta} \frac{1}{N}\sum \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x^{(i)}), y^{(i)})$.
- ✅ **Key Formula (K-Means):** $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K}\sum_{x \in C_k} \Vert x - \mu_k \Vert^{2}$.
- ✅ **Key Formula (Cross-Entropy):** $\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum \sum y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)$.
- ✅ **Gradient Descent:** $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}(\theta)$.
- ✅ **Supervised Cyber Use Cases:** Malware classification, spam filtering, phishing URL detection, intrusion classification.
- ✅ **Unsupervised Cyber Use Cases:** Zero-day anomaly detection, user behavior analytics, botnet discovery, network clustering.
- ✅ **Hybrid (Semi-Supervised)** is the modern trend in SOCs — small labeled seed + large unlabeled corpus.
- ✅ **Evaluation metrics differ:** Supervised → Accuracy, F1, AUC; Unsupervised → Silhouette, Davies–Bouldin, Reconstruction Error.
- ✅ **Overfitting risk is higher in supervised** learning due to label memorization.
- ✅ **Feature scaling (StandardScaler/MinMax) is MANDATORY for unsupervised** distance-based methods.
- ✅ **Clustering ≠ Classification** — never interchange these terms in board exams.
- ✅ **Decision boundary vs. cluster boundary** — supervised learns explicit boundaries; unsupervised infers regions of density.
- ✅ **Pseudo-labeling** is the bridge technique between supervised and unsupervised paradigms.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anomaly Detection Techniques in Cybersecurity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT633 - AI AND ML IN CYBER SECURITY DEFENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Anomaly Detection Techniques in Cybersecurity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Anomaly Detection** (also called **Outlier Detection**) is the process of identifying data points, observations, or patterns that deviate significantly from the expected, normal behaviour of a system. In the context of cybersecurity, anomaly detection refers to the computational identification of rare events, network traffic patterns, system calls, or user behaviours that do not conform to a well-defined notion of normal activity, and which may indicate security threats such as intrusions, malware, insider attacks, or data exfiltration.

According to the **Chandola–Banerjee–Kumar taxonomy (2009)** — the de-facto academic reference — an anomaly is defined as:
> *"A pattern in data that does not conform to the well-defined notion of normal behaviour."*

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECCT633)** framework, anomaly detection is positioned as the foundational pillar of **AI-driven Cyber Defence**, enabling proactive identification of *zero-day attacks*, *Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs)*, and *insider threats* that evade signature-based defences.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Syllabus Highlight):**
> An anomaly is a quantitative or qualitative deviation from an established baseline profile. The baseline is learned from historical "normal" data using statistical, machine-learning, or deep-learning models.

## 1.2 The Three Fundamental Types of Anomalies

| **Type** | **Definition** | **Cybersecurity Example** |
|---|---|---|
| **Point (Global) Anomaly** | A single data point far outside the normal range | A sudden **CPU spike of 98%** on an idle database server |
| **Contextual (Conditional) Anomaly** | A point anomalous only in a *specific context* but normal otherwise | **200 login attempts** at 2 AM for a user who logs in only during office hours |
| **Collective Anomaly** | A *group* of points that is anomalous together, even if individual points are normal | A sequence of **10 small financial transactions** within 60 seconds from one account |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Intuition for Students):**
> Imagine a **security guard at a bank**:
> - A *Point Anomaly* → A masked person entering the bank at 2 PM on a Tuesday (globally strange).
> - A *Contextual Anomaly* → A customer withdrawing ₹50,000 at 3 AM (normal during the day, anomalous at night — context matters).
> - A *Collective Anomaly* → 20 people each withdrawing ₹1,000 in 5 minutes (each person looks normal, but the *pattern* of simultaneous small withdrawals is a coordinated attack signal).
>
> The guard's brain performs the same statistical and pattern-recognition task that an ML-based Intrusion Detection System (IDS) performs on network packets.

## 1.3 Why Anomaly Detection is Critical in Cyber Defence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Zero-Day Defence**: Signature-based systems (antivirus, firewalls) fail on novel attacks. Anomaly detection catches *behavioural deviation*, irrespective of the attack signature.
> - **Insider Threat Detection**: Malicious insiders use *valid credentials* — their traffic looks "valid" by signature but is *contextually anomalous*.
> - **Scale**: Modern networks generate **terabytes/day** of logs. Manual analysis is impossible; AI-driven anomaly detection operates at line-speed.

**Standard Performance Metrics** (to be evaluated in detail in Section 2): **Precision**, **Recall**, **F1-Score**, **AUC-ROC**, and **False Positive Rate (FPR)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Paradigms of Anomaly Detection

### Paradigm A — Statistical Methods (Pre-ML Era)
- **Assumption**: Normal data follows a known statistical distribution (typically **Gaussian/Normal Distribution**).
- **Mechanism**: Compute a *deviation score* (e.g., Z-score, Mahalanobis distance) and flag points whose score exceeds a threshold.
- **Strengths**: Mathematically interpretable, low computational cost.
- **Weaknesses**: Fails on high-dimensional, non-Gaussian cyber data.

### Paradigm B — Classical Machine Learning
- **Unsupervised**: k-Means, DBSCAN, Isolation Forest, Local Outlier Factor (LOF) — trained on unlabeled data assuming "normal is the majority".
- **Supervised**: SVM, Random Forest, k-NN — trained on labelled "attack/benign" data.
- **Semi-Supervised**: One-Class SVM, Autoencoders — trained *only* on normal data; anomalies are anything the model cannot reconstruct.

### Paradigm C — Deep Learning
- **Autoencoders**, **Variational Autoencoders (VAE)**, **Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)**, **LSTMs** for sequence anomalies.
- Captures complex non-linear patterns in high-dimensional traffic (e.g., NetFlow, packet payloads).

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Generic Anomaly Detection Pipeline

The following seven-step logic applies to *every* anomaly detection system, regardless of the underlying model:

1. **Data Acquisition**: Collect logs, NetFlow records, system calls, authentication events.
2. **Preprocessing**: Handle missing values, normalize features (Min-Max or Z-score), encode categoricals.
3. **Feature Engineering**: Extract statistical (mean, variance), temporal (inter-arrival time), and behavioural (failed-login rate) features.
4. **Model Training**: Fit a baseline profile of "normal" using one of the paradigms above.
5. **Scoring**: For each new observation $x$, compute an **anomaly score** $S(x)$.
6. **Thresholding**: Compare $S(x)$ with a threshold $\tau$. If $S(x) > \tau$, flag as anomaly.
7. **Action**: Generate an alert, log the event, or trigger an automated response (e.g., IP block).

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Z-Score?** Because under Gaussian assumption, $\approx 99.7\%$ of data lies within $\pm 3\sigma$ of the mean. So anything beyond $|Z| > 3$ is a statistical outlier.
>
> **Why Mahalanobis Distance?** Because in *multi-variate* cyber data (e.g., packet size + duration + port), features are *correlated*. Euclidean distance ignores correlation, leading to false alarms. Mahalanobis accounts for the **covariance structure** $\Sigma$.
>
> **Why Isolation Forest?** Because anomalies are *"few and different"* — they get isolated by random splits *faster* (shorter path length) than normal points. This is a brilliant insight: **anomalies are easier to isolate than normal points**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **LaTeX Form** | **Use Case** | **Threshold / Typical Value** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Arithmetic Mean | $\mu = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ | Baseline computation | N/A |
| Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2}$ | Dispersion measure | N/A |
| Z-Score (Univariate) | $Z_i = \frac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Outlier detection (1D) | $\vert Z \vert > 2.5$ or $3$ |
| Mahalanobis Distance | $D_M(x) = \sqrt{(x - \mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu)}$ | Multi-variate outlier | $D_M^2 > \chi^2_{k, 0.975}$ |
| Euclidean Distance | $d(x, y) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - y_i)^2}$ | k-NN based detection | Top-k distance percentile |
| LOF (Local Outlier Factor) | $\text{LOF}_k(x) = \frac{\sum_{y \in N_k(x)} \text{lrd}(y)}{\vert N_k(x) \vert \cdot \text{lrd}(x)}$ | Density-based detection | $\text{LOF} > 1.5$ |
| Reconstruction Error (AE) | $\mathcal{L}(x) = \Vert x - \hat{x} \Vert^2$ | Autoencoder-based | 95th percentile of training loss |
| Information Entropy | $H(X) = -\sum_{i} p(x_i)\log_2 p(x_i)$ | Detecting information-theoretic anomalies | Compare with baseline entropy |
| Shannon Anomaly Score | $S(x) = -\log P(x)$ | Likelihood-based | $S > \tau$ |
| Precision | $\text{Prec} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Classifier evaluation | Higher is better |
| Recall (TPR) | $\text{Rec} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Classifier evaluation | Higher is better |
| F1-Score | $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}}$ | Imbalanced data | Max = 1.0 |
| False Positive Rate | $\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | IDS evaluation | Target < 0.01 |
| AUC-ROC | $\int_0^1 \text{TPR}(\text{FPR})\, d(\text{FPR})$ | Threshold-free metric | Max = 1.0 |
| Isolation Forest Score | $s(x, n) = 2^{-\frac{E(h(x))}{c(n)}}$ | Tree-based scoring | $s \to 1$ = anomaly |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of These Equations:**
> - In an **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** (e.g., Snort + ML module), Mahalanobis distance flags multi-dimensional port-scan attacks.
> - In a **User Behaviour Analytics (UBA)** platform, LOF identifies insider threats.
> - In **fraud detection** (banking), autoencoder reconstruction loss catches fraudulent credit-card swipes.
> - In **SIEM systems** (Splunk, QRadar), information entropy detects DDoS attacks (entropy of source-IP distribution drops sharply during a botnet surge).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section presents **three complete worked examples** that KTU examiners frequently test:
- **Example 1**: Z-Score based univariate anomaly detection.
- **Example 2**: Mahalanobis distance for multi-variate detection.
- **Example 3**: Confusion-matrix based performance metric derivation.

---

## 3.1 Example 1 — Z-Score Based Detection (Worked KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem Statement:**
A network administrator logs the number of failed login attempts per user per day for 15 users. The dataset is:

$$X = \{3, 5, 4, 6, 2, 4, 5, 3, 4, 45, 5, 4, 3, 6, 4\}$$

Using a **Z-score threshold of $|Z| > 2.5$**, determine which user account(s) should be flagged as anomalous. Show every arithmetic step.

### Step 1: Compute the Mean $\mu$

The arithmetic mean is the sum of all observations divided by the number of observations.

$$\mu = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i = \frac{1}{15}(3+5+4+6+2+4+5+3+4+45+5+4+3+6+4)$$

Adding the values sequentially:
- $3 + 5 = 8$
- $8 + 4 = 12$
- $12 + 6 = 18$
- $18 + 2 = 20$
- $20 + 4 = 24$
- $24 + 5 = 29$
- $29 + 3 = 32$
- $32 + 4 = 36$
- $36 + 45 = 81$
- $81 + 5 = 86$
- $86 + 4 = 90$
- $90 + 3 = 93$
- $93 + 6 = 99$
- $99 + 4 = 103$

Therefore $\sum_{i=1}^{15} x_i = 103$, which gives:

$$\mu = \frac{103}{15} \approx 6.8667$$

### Step 2: Compute the Standard Deviation $\sigma$

The standard deviation requires the squared deviations from the mean. We compute $(x_i - \mu)^2$ for each observation. Using $\mu \approx 6.8667$:

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $x_i - \mu$ | $(x_i - \mu)^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3 | $-3.8667$ | $14.9513$ |
| 2 | 5 | $-1.8667$ | $3.4846$ |
| 3 | 4 | $-2.8667$ | $8.2179$ |
| 4 | 6 | $-0.8667$ | $0.7512$ |
| 5 | 2 | $-4.8667$ | $23.6846$ |
| 6 | 4 | $-2.8667$ | $8.2179$ |
| 7 | 5 | $-1.8667$ | $3.4846$ |
| 8 | 3 | $-3.8667$ | $14.9513$ |
| 9 | 4 | $-2.8667$ | $8.2179$ |
| 10 | **45** | $38.1333$ | $1454.1464$ |
| 11 | 5 | $-1.8667$ | $3.4846$ |
| 12 | 4 | $-2.8667$ | $8.2179$ |
| 13 | 3 | $-3.8667$ | $14.9513$ |
| 14 | 6 | $-0.8667$ | $0.7512$ |
| 15 | 4 | $-2.8667$ | $8.2179$ |

Summing all squared deviations:

$$\sum (x_i - \mu)^2 = 14.9513 + 3.4846 + 8.2179 + 0.7512 + 23.6846 + 8.2179 + 3.4846 + 14.9513 + 8.2179 + 1454.1464 + 3.4846 + 8.2179 + 14.9513 + 0.7512 + 8.2179$$

$$= 1575.7306$$

The variance is the average squared deviation:

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{\sum (x_i - \mu)^2}{N} = \frac{1575.7306}{15} \approx 105.0487$$

The standard deviation is the square root of the variance:

$$\sigma = \sqrt{105.0487} \approx 10.2493$$

### Step 3: Compute the Z-Score for the Suspicious Value $x = 45$

The Z-score formula is:

$$Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Substituting $x = 45$, $\mu = 6.8667$, and $\sigma = 10.2493$:

$$Z = \frac{45 - 6.8667}{10.2493} = \frac{38.1333}{10.2493} \approx 3.7204$$

### Step 4: Compare Against the Threshold and Decide

The threshold rule is $|Z| > 2.5$. We have:

$$|Z| = 3.7204 > 2.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{FLAG AS ANOMALY}$$

### Step 5: Verify All Other Values Are Normal

For any other $x_i \in X$ (e.g., the maximum non-anomalous value is $x = 6$):

$$Z = \frac{6 - 6.8667}{10.2493} = \frac{-0.8667}{10.2493} \approx -0.0846$$

$$|Z| = 0.0846 < 2.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{NORMAL}$$

### **Conclusion of Example 1**
> The user account with **45 failed login attempts** is flagged as anomalous. This is consistent with a **brute-force attack** or **credential-stuffing attempt** on that account. The remaining 14 accounts are statistically normal.

---

## 3.2 Example 2 — Mahalanobis Distance for Multi-Variate Anomaly Detection

**Problem Statement:**
A 2-D feature vector $(x_1, x_2)$ is monitored: $x_1$ = packet size (bytes), $x_2$ = connection duration (seconds). For the normal traffic baseline, the mean vector and covariance matrix are:

$$\mu = \begin{bmatrix} 500 \\ 30 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 400 & 50 \\ 50 & 25 \end{bmatrix}$$

A new observation arrives: $x = \begin{bmatrix} 1200 \\ 80 \end{bmatrix}$. Using the Mahalanobis distance with $\chi^2_{2, 0.975} = 5.99$ as the threshold, determine if the observation is anomalous.

### Step 1: Write Down the Mahalanobis Distance Formula

$$D_M^2 = (x - \mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu)$$

### Step 2: Compute the Inverse of the Covariance Matrix

The determinant of $\Sigma$ is:

$$\det(\Sigma) = (400)(25) - (50)(50) = 10000 - 2500 = 7500$$

The inverse is:

$$\Sigma^{-1} = \frac{1}{\det(\Sigma)} \begin{bmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{7500} \begin{bmatrix} 25 & -50 \\ -50 & 400 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\Sigma^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.00333 & -0.00667 \\ -0.00667 & 0.05333 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Deviation Vector $(x - \mu)$

$$x - \mu = \begin{bmatrix} 1200 - 500 \\ 80 - 30 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 700 \\ 50 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4: Compute $\Sigma^{-1}(x - \mu)$

Multiplying the inverse covariance by the deviation vector:

$$\Sigma^{-1}(x - \mu) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.00333 \cdot 700 + (-0.00667) \cdot 50 \\ -0.00667 \cdot 700 + 0.05333 \cdot 50 \end{bmatrix}$$

Row 1: $0.00333 \times 700 = 2.331$; $-0.00667 \times 50 = -0.3335$; sum = $1.9975$

Row 2: $-0.00667 \times 700 = -4.669$; $0.05333 \times 50 = 2.6665$; sum = $-2.0025$

$$\Sigma^{-1}(x - \mu) = \begin{bmatrix} 1.9975 \\ -2.0025 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 5: Compute the Final Mahalanobis Distance Squared

$$D_M^2 = (x - \mu)^T \Sigma^{-1}(x - \mu) = \begin{bmatrix} 700 & 50 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1.9975 \\ -2.0025 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$D_M^2 = (700)(1.9975) + (50)(-2.0025) = 1398.25 - 100.125 = 1298.125$$

$$D_M = \sqrt{1298.125} \approx 36.03$$

### Step 6: Compare Against $\chi^2$ Threshold

$$D_M^2 = 1298.125 \gg \chi^2_{2, 0.975} = 5.99$$

### **Conclusion of Example 2**
> The observation is **strongly anomalous** — likely a **data exfiltration attempt** (large packet size, long duration, deviating from baseline traffic).

---

## 3.3 Example 3 — Performance Metrics from a Confusion Matrix

**Problem Statement:** A network IDS produces the following confusion matrix on a test set of 1000 events:

| | Predicted Attack | Predicted Normal |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Attack** | TP = 85 | FN = 15 |
| **Actual Normal** | FP = 10 | TN = 890 |

Compute: **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, FPR**.

### Step 1: Accuracy

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + FN + TN} = \frac{85 + 890}{85 + 10 + 15 + 890} = \frac{975}{1000} = 0.975$$

### Step 2: Precision

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{85}{85 + 10} = \frac{85}{95} \approx 0.8947$$

### Step 3: Recall (True Positive Rate)

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{85}{85 + 15} = \frac{85}{100} = 0.85$$

### Step 4: F1-Score

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.8947 \cdot 0.85}{0.8947 + 0.85} = \frac{1.5210}{1.7447} \approx 0.8718$$

### Step 5: False Positive Rate

$$\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{10}{10 + 890} = \frac{10}{900} \approx 0.0111$$

### **Conclusion of Example 3**
> The IDS has **high accuracy (97.5%)** but a **recall of only 85%** — meaning it missed 15 actual attacks. In cybersecurity, **recall is more critical than accuracy** because missing a real attack is far costlier than raising a false alarm.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Method** | **Data Type** | **Supervised?** | **Handles High-D?** | **Handles Non-Linear?** | **Interpretability** | **Cyber Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Z-Score | Univariate | No | No | No | High | Threshold alarms |
| Mahalanobis | Multi-variate | No | Medium | No | High | Port-scan detection |
| k-NN | Both | Semi | No | Yes | Medium | Behavioural clustering |
| LOF | Both | No | Medium | Yes | High | Insider threat |
| Isolation Forest | Both | No | Yes | Yes | High | Network IDS (NSL-KDD) |
| One-Class SVM | Both | Semi | Yes | Yes | Low | Zero-day detection |
| Autoencoder | Both | Semi | Yes | Yes | Low | Advanced persistent threats |
| Supervised RF/SVM | Both | Yes | Yes | Yes | Medium | Known-attack classification |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Anomaly Detection Operational Pipeline (Block-Level Flow)

The following Mermaid block maps the **complete data-flow architecture** of an AI-driven cyber anomaly detection system — from raw packet ingestion to incident response.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[NETWORK TAP AND SENSOR LAYER] --> N2[LOG AGGREGATION AND SIEM INGEST]
    N2 --> N3[DATA PREPROCESSING MODULE]
    N3 --> N4[FEATURE ENGINEERING MODULE]
    N4 --> N5[ANOMALY DETECTION MODEL LAYER]
    N5 --> N6[ANOMALY SCORE GENERATION]
    N6 --> N7[THRESHOLD COMPARISON]
    N7 --> N8{SCORE GREATER THAN TAU}
    N8 -->|YES| N9[ALERT GENERATION]
    N8 -->|NO| N10[NORMAL TRAFFIC LOG]
    N9 --> N11[SECURITY ANALYST REVIEW]
    N9 --> N12[AUTOMATED INCIDENT RESPONSE]
    N12 --> N13[IP BLOCKING AND QUARANTINE]
    N11 --> N14[FEEDBACK TO BASELINE MODEL]
    N10 --> N14
    N14 --> N5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: The **feedback loop** (N14 → N5) represents the *online learning* paradigm — the model continuously updates its baseline as new "normal" traffic is observed, preventing **concept drift** and **stale-model bias**.

---

## 4.3 Isolation Forest Internal Mechanism (Schematic)

The following diagram illustrates how **Isolation Forest** isolates anomalies through random axis-aligned splits — anomalies require *fewer splits* (shorter path) than normal points.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    I1[INPUT FEATURE SPACE] --> I2[RANDOM FEATURE SELECTION]
    I2 --> I3[RANDOM SPLIT VALUE SELECTION]
    I3 --> I4{BUILD ISOLATION TREE}
    I4 --> I5[COMPUTE PATH LENGTH H X]
    I5 --> I6[REPEAT FOR N TREES]
    I6 --> I7[COMPUTE EXPECTED PATH E H X]
    I7 --> I8[COMPUTE ANOMALY SCORE S]
    I8 --> I9[S APPROACHES 1 MEANS ANOMALY]
    I8 --> I10[S LESS THAN 0.5 MEANS NORMAL]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Isolation Forest works for cyber IDS** (NSL-KDD, CICIDS-2017 datasets): Anomalous traffic (e.g., DoS, Probe, R2L) forms a *small, scattered* cluster. Random splits isolate them quickly. The expected path length $E(h(x))$ is small, making $s \to 1$.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

### **Q1. Define anomaly detection. Differentiate between point, contextual, and collective anomalies with cybersecurity examples.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition**: Anomaly detection is the process of identifying data points that deviate significantly from established normal behaviour, indicating potential security threats.

**Differentiation**:

| **Type** | **Meaning** | **Cyber Example** |
|---|---|---|
| **Point Anomaly** | A single data point deviating from the entire dataset | A single network packet of **5000 bytes** when average is 500 bytes |
| **Contextual Anomaly** | A point anomalous only in a specific context | A login from **New York** at 3 AM for a user who logs in only from **Kerala during 9 AM–6 PM** |
| **Collective Anomaly** | A group of points anomalous together | **200 DNS queries** within 10 seconds from one host (individual queries are normal) |

> *Awarding marks: 1 for definition + 2 for differentiation with examples.*

---

### **Q2. List any three statistical methods for anomaly detection. State one limitation of the Z-score method.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

Three statistical methods for anomaly detection are:
1. **Z-Score Method** (Standard score) — flags points where $\vert Z \vert > 3$.
2. **Mahalanobis Distance** — multi-variate outlier detection using covariance structure.
3. **Grubbs' Test** — for single outlier detection in normally distributed data.

**Limitation of Z-Score**: It assumes the data follows a **Gaussian (Normal) distribution**. Real-world cyber data (packet sizes, inter-arrival times) is typically **heavy-tailed and non-Gaussian**, making Z-score unreliable and prone to false alarms.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, PECCT633]**

---

### **Question A: Statistical & Distance-Based Anomaly Detection** `[14 Marks]`

**Q.A (a) Explain the Mahalanobis distance method for anomaly detection. How does it overcome the limitations of Euclidean distance in multi-variate cyber data? Derive the decision rule. `[7 Marks]`**

**Model Solution:**

The **Mahalanobis distance** $D_M$ measures the distance of a point $x$ from a distribution's mean $\mu$, accounting for the correlation between features via the covariance matrix $\Sigma$.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

$$D_M^2 = (x - \mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu)$$

**Decision Rule:**
$$D_M^2 > \chi^2_{k, \alpha} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Anomaly}$$

where $k$ = number of features, $\alpha$ = significance level (typically 0.05), and $\chi^2_{k, \alpha}$ is the chi-squared critical value.

**Limitations of Euclidean Distance**:
- Treats each feature as equally weighted and independent.
- Ignores **correlation** between features (e.g., packet size and duration are correlated in normal traffic).
- Produces distorted distances when features have different scales (e.g., bytes vs. milliseconds).

**How Mahalanobis Overcomes These**:
- The $\Sigma^{-1}$ term **standardises** the space — features with high variance contribute less.
- It rotates the space so that **correlated features are decorrelated** before distance computation.
- It is **scale-invariant** — works regardless of whether features are in bytes, seconds, or counts.

**Derivation of Decision Rule**: For multivariate Gaussian data, $(x - \mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu)$ follows a $\chi^2$ distribution with $k$ degrees of freedom. Hence, the threshold is the $\chi^2$ critical value at chosen significance level $\alpha$.

---

**Q.A (b) For a network with two monitored features — *packet size* ($x_1$) and *duration* ($x_2$) — compute the Mahalanobis distance for the observation $x = (1000, 60)$ given $\mu = (500, 30)$ and $\Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 10000 & 500 \\ 500 & 100 \end{bmatrix}$. Use $\chi^2_{2, 0.95} = 5.99$ and comment. `[7 Marks]`**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute $\Sigma^{-1}$**

$$\det(\Sigma) = (10000)(100) - (500)(500) = 1000000 - 250000 = 750000$$

$$\Sigma^{-1} = \frac{1}{750000} \begin{bmatrix} 100 & -500 \\ -500 & 10000 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.000133 & -0.000667 \\ -0.000667 & 0.01333 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Compute deviation vector**

$$x - \mu = \begin{bmatrix} 1000 - 500 \\ 60 - 30 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 500 \\ 30 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Compute $\Sigma^{-1}(x - \mu)$**

$$\Sigma^{-1}(x - \mu) = \begin{bmatrix} (0.000133)(500) + (-0.000667)(30) \\ (-0.000667)(500) + (0.01333)(30) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0665 - 0.0200 \\ -0.3335 + 0.4000 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0465 \\ 0.0665 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Compute $D_M^2$**

$$D_M^2 = (500)(0.0465) + (30)(0.0665) = 23.25 + 1.995 = 25.245$$

**Step 5: Compare with $\chi^2$ threshold**

$$D_M^2 = 25.245 \gg 5.99$$

**Comment**: The observation is **highly anomalous** — likely indicating a **data exfiltration or command-and-control beacon** event.

---

### **Question B: Machine Learning Approaches to Anomaly Detection** `[14 Marks]`

**Q.B (a) Compare and contrast supervised, unsupervised, and semi-supervised anomaly detection. Which is most suitable for detecting zero-day attacks and why? `[7 Marks]`**

**Model Solution:**

| **Aspect** | **Supervised** | **Unsupervised** | **Semi-Supervised** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Training Data** | Labelled (normal + attack) | Unlabelled | Only normal data |
| **Algorithm Examples** | SVM, Random Forest | k-Means, Isolation Forest, LOF | One-Class SVM, Autoencoder |
| **Label Requirement** | High (expensive) | None | Low |
| **Detection of Novel Attacks** | Poor (only known classes) | Good | Excellent |
| **False Positive Rate** | Low (with balanced data) | Higher | Moderate |
| **Cyber Use Case** | Known malware classification | Network traffic profiling | Zero-day intrusion detection |

**Most suitable for zero-day attacks: Semi-Supervised.**
- **Reason**: Zero-day attacks have *no historical labelled samples*. Semi-supervised models (e.g., One-Class SVM, Autoencoders) learn the *profile of normal traffic* and flag anything that fails to conform. The autoencoder's reconstruction error is the canonical anomaly score for novel attacks.
- Supervised models **fail** because the attack class was never seen during training. Pure unsupervised models generate too many false positives in noisy cyber data.

---

**Q.B (b) Explain the Isolation Forest algorithm in detail. Show how the anomaly score is computed and justify why it is computationally efficient for large-scale network monitoring. `[7 Marks]`**

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm Overview** (Liu, Ting, Zhou — 2008):

1. **Build Isolation Trees (iTrees)**: For each tree:
   - Select a random feature $q$ from the $d$ features.
   - Select a random split value $p$ uniformly in the range $[min(q), max(q)]$.
   - Recursively split until each point is isolated or max depth $\log_2(n)$ is reached.

2. **Path Length** $h(x)$: Number of edges traversed from root to isolate $x$. **Anomalies have shorter paths** (they get isolated quickly).

3. **Average Path Length** across $t$ trees: $E(h(x))$

4. **Anomaly Score**:
$$s(x, n) = 2^{-\frac{E(h(x))}{c(n)}}$$
where $c(n) = 2H(n-1) - \frac{2(n-1)}{n}$ is the average path length of an unsuccessful BST search.

**Score Interpretation**:
- $s \to 1$: Anomaly (short path, isolated quickly)
- $s < 0.5$: Normal point
- $s \approx 0.5$: Entire dataset has no clear anomaly

**Justification for Computational Efficiency**:
- **Linear time complexity** $O(t \cdot n \cdot \log n)$ — only random splits, no distance computation.
- **No pairwise distance matrix** required (unlike k-NN, LOF which need $O(n^2)$ memory).
- **Scales to millions of NetFlow records** — suitable for **ISP-level monitoring**.
- **Handles high-dimensional data** without curse-of-dimensionality issues.
- **Parallelizable** — each iTree can be built independently on a different CPU core.

**Cyber Application**: Used in **NSL-KDD, CICIDS-2017** benchmark datasets for benchmarking IDS performance; deployed in **AWS GuardDuty** for cloud anomaly detection.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU Valuation Key Insights):**
> 1. **Confusing *anomaly* with *fault* or *noise***: Noise is random, non-systematic deviation. Anomalies are systematic and potentially malicious. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Skipping preprocessing**: In a worked numerical, you **must** show $\mu$ and $\sigma$ computation explicitly. Students lose 2–3 marks for skipping the "show that $\mu = ...$" step.
> 3. **Forgetting the $\chi^2$ degree-of-freedom parameter**: Mahalanobis threshold depends on $k$ (number of features). A common error is using $\chi^2_{k=2} = 5.99$ when $k = 3$, where the correct value is $7.81$.
> 4. **Choosing the wrong metric**: In cybersecurity, **recall is more important than accuracy** because missing a real attack is costlier than a false alarm. Always justify your metric choice.
> 5. **Ignoring concept drift**: Static baselines become stale. Examiners frequently award bonus marks for mentioning **online learning / model retraining**.
> 6. **Mixing up precision and recall definitions**: $\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ — many students incorrectly write $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$.
> 7. **Writing only the algorithm but no cyber example**: Always anchor your answer to a cyber use case (e.g., IDS, SIEM, UBA) to fetch the "application" component of the marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This the Night Before the Exam:**

- **Anomaly**: A pattern deviating from "normal" behaviour. Three types: **Point**, **Contextual**, **Collective**.
- **Z-Score** $Z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$: Threshold $\vert Z \vert > 2.5$ (or $3$). Univariate, Gaussian assumption.
- **Mahalanobis Distance** $D_M^2 = (x - \mu)^T \Sigma^{-1}(x - \mu)$: Multi-variate, scale-invariant, decorrelates features. Threshold = $\chi^2_{k, \alpha}$.
- **Euclidean** vs **Mahalanobis**: Euclidean ignores feature correlation and scale; Mahalanobis accounts for both.
- **LOF (Local Outlier Factor)**: Compares local density of a point to its $k$-neighbours. $\text{LOF} > 1.5 \Rightarrow$ anomaly.
- **Isolation Forest**: Tree-based. Anomalies have **shorter average path length**; score $s = 2^{-E(h(x))/c(n)}$.
- **One-Class SVM**: Learns a boundary around normal data; flags anything outside.
- **Autoencoder**: Trains on normal data; anomaly score = reconstruction error $\Vert x - \hat{x} \Vert^2$.
- **Performance Metrics**: Accuracy (overall), **Precision** (TP / (TP+FP)), **Recall** (TP / (TP+FN)), **F1**, **FPR** (FP / (FP+TN)), **AUC-ROC**.
- **For cyber IDS, Recall > Accuracy**: Missing a real attack is catastrophic.
- **Zero-day defence → Semi-supervised** (One-Class SVM, Autoencoder).
- **Pipeline order**: Acquire → Preprocess → Feature Engineer → Train → Score → Threshold → Act → Feedback.
- **Real-world deployers**: AWS GuardDuty (Isolation Forest), Splunk MLTK (statistical + DL), Darktrace (unsupervised behavioural).
- **Always state assumptions**: Gaussianity (Z-score), sufficient labelled data (supervised), high-quality normal baseline (semi-supervised).

> **End of Module 1 Notes — PECCT633 — Anomaly Detection Techniques in Cybersecurity**

---
