topics.title: Privacy Preserving Nearest Neighbour Search: Techniques and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT633 - AI AND ML IN CYBER SECURITY DEFENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Privacy Preserving Nearest Neighbour Search: Techniques and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Privacy Preserving Nearest Neighbour Search: Core Foundations

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Nearest Neighbour Search (NNS)** is a fundamental problem in computer science and machine learning where, given a query point **q** in a metric space **M**, the goal is to find the data point **p** in a database **D = {p₁, p₂, ..., pₙ}** that minimizes a predefined distance function **d(q, pᵢ)**.

**Privacy Preserving Nearest Neighbour Search (PP-NNS)** is the cryptographically-hardened variant of NNS, in which the query **q**, the database **D**, and/or the resulting identity of the nearest neighbour are concealed from unauthorized parties (including the server hosting the database itself). Formally, PP-NNS protocols are evaluated under adversarial models such as the **honest-but-curious (semi-honest)** adversary and the **malicious (active) adversary**, and they must satisfy at least one of the following security guarantees:

- **Confidentiality:** The server learns nothing about **q** or the matched record.
- **Query Unlinkability:** Repeated queries from the same user cannot be linked.
- **Access Pattern Hiding:** Even the *positions* of candidate matches are hidden.
- **Result Privacy:** The user learns only the matched record, not the full database.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** PP-NNS sits at the confluence of three pillars — *Applied Cryptography*, *Information Retrieval*, and *Adversarial Machine Learning*. It is a compulsory Module 1 topic and frequently appears in ESE Part A (3-mark definition/short notes).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a doctor in a hospital, and you have a patient's encrypted MRI scan. You want to check whether a similar historical case exists in a centralized research database of **10 million** encrypted scans — *without* revealing the new patient's identity, and *without* the research lab seeing whose scan they are matching you against.

You submit your encrypted scan. The lab runs a comparison in a "black box." The black box tells you: *"Yes, the closest match is record #4,872,113,"* and hands you a decryption key for that single record. The lab never saw the scan, never knew which record matched, and never learned your patient's name. **This is PP-NNS in action.**

Another everyday analogy: a **dating app** that tells you *"you have 3 compatible matches nearby"* without ever revealing your GPS coordinates, profile traits, or the identity of the matches to its servers.

## 1.3 Classification of PP-NNS by Adversarial Setting

| Adversary Model | Behaviour | Defence Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Honest-but-Curious** | Follows the protocol but inspects all data it sees | Standard cryptographic primitives suffice |
| **Malicious** | Deviates from the protocol actively | Zero-Knowledge Proofs, cut-and-choose, SPDZ-style MACs |
| **Covert** | Cheats only if unlikely to be caught | Asymmetric cryptographic penalties + audits |
| **Quantum Adversary** | Uses Shor's / Grover's algorithms | Lattice-based / Code-based PP-NNS |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard **Distance Metrics** used in NNS include Euclidean (L₂), Manhattan (L₁), Chebyshev (L∞), Hamming, and Cosine distances. PP-NNS literature most commonly uses L₂ and Hamming because they are mathematically tractable under encryption.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Unprotected Baseline (Why Encryption is Needed)

In plain NNS, the server computes the distance for every record:

$$d(q, p_i) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{k}(q_j - p_{ij})^2}$$

It then returns the index **i\*** that minimizes **d(q, pᵢ)**. This leaks:
- The **query vector** itself.
- The **closest record's contents**.
- **Access patterns** (which indices were close to the query).

## 2.2 The Four Pillars of PP-NNS Techniques

### Pillar 1: Homomorphic Encryption (HE) Based PP-NNS
- The query is encrypted under a public key: **c = Enc(q)**.
- The server computes distances **homomorphically**, never decrypting.
- The result is sent back; only the client (holding the secret key) decrypts.
- Variants: **Paillier (additive)**, **BGV/BFV (fully homomorphic)**, **CKKS (approximate, ideal for ML)**.

### Pillar 2: Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC)
- The database is **secret-shared** (e.g., via Shamir's secret sharing) between two or more non-colluding servers.
- Garbled Circuits (Yao), GMW protocol, or SPDZ are used to compute the **argmin** obliviously.
- Neither server learns the query or result alone.

### Pillar 3: Order-Preserving Encryption (OPE) & Asymmetric Scalar Product Preserving Encryption (ASPE)
- A relaxed encryption that allows the server to **order** ciphertexts without decryption.
- Cheaper than FHE, but provides weaker privacy guarantees.

### Pillar 4: Differential Privacy (DP) Based PP-NNS
- Noise (Laplace or Gaussian) is added to distances or to retrieved neighbours.
- Guarantees **(ε, δ)-differential privacy** regardless of background knowledge.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Notation | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Euclidean Distance | $d(q,p)=\sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{k}(q_j-p_j)^2}$ | L₂ metric NNS |
| 2 | Hamming Distance | $d_H(q,p)=\sum_{j=1}^{k} q_j \oplus p_j$ | Biometric / binary feature NNS |
| 3 | Cosine Similarity | $\cos\theta = \frac{q \cdot p}{\|q\|\,\|p\|}$ | Text / embedding retrieval |
| 4 | Paillier Additive Homomorphism | $E(m_1)\cdot E(m_2) = E(m_1+m_2)$ | Encrypted sum of squared diffs |
| 5 | Paillier Scalar Multiplication | $E(m)^k = E(km)$ | Encrypted weighted distance |
| 6 | Squared L₂ under Paillier | $D_i = \sum_{j} E((q_j-p_{ij})^2)$ | Privacy-preserving distance |
| 7 | DP Laplace Mechanism | $\tilde{f}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}(\Delta f / \varepsilon)$ | Differentially private NNS |
| 8 | DP Privacy Budget | $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP | Standard DP definition |
| 9 | Information-Theoretic Security | $I(X;Y) = 0$ | Perfect secrecy condition |
| 10 | Oblivious Transfer | $OT_{1}^{2}$ | Building block for GC-based NNS |
| 11 | Yao's Millionaire's Protocol | Boolean circuit evaluation | Used to find argmin under encryption |
| 12 | CKKS Noise Budget | Multiplicative depth ≤ L | FHE constraint for ML on encrypted data |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** For distance-based PP-NNS, the **Paillier cryptosystem** is the most frequently tested one. Memorize its homomorphic properties (additive homomorphism and scalar multiplication), and the two decryption exponentiations.

## 2.4 Why PP-NNS is Engineering-Critical

- **Healthcare:** Cross-hospital patient cohort discovery under HIPAA / GDPR.
- **Biometrics:** Encrypted fingerprint / face matching on national ID databases.
- **Recommendation Engines:** Privacy-preserving collaborative filtering.
- **Genomics:** Encrypted DNA sequence similarity search.
- **Defense / Surveillance:** Threat pattern matching without leaking sensor data.
- **Edge-AI / Federated Learning:** Secure aggregation of nearest-neighbour embeddings on distributed nodes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Squared L₂ Distance under Paillier Encryption

We derive the exact protocol that the server uses to compute squared Euclidean distance between an encrypted query and a plaintext (or also encrypted) record.

### Step 1 — Client-side Encryption of the Query
The client generates Paillier key pair **(pk, sk)** and encrypts each coordinate **qⱼ**:

$$E(q_j) = g^{q_j} \cdot r_j^{n} \mod n^2$$

where **n = p·q** is the RSA modulus, **g** is the generator, and **rⱼ ∈ ℤ*ₙ** is a fresh random blinding factor per coordinate.

### Step 2 — Server-side Homomorphic Squaring of the Difference
The server must compute the squared term **(qⱼ − pᵢⱼ)²**. Since Paillier is only *additively* homomorphic, we expand the square:

$$(q_j - p_{ij})^2 = q_j^2 - 2q_j p_{ij} + p_{ij}^2$$

The server then computes three terms homomorphically:
- **T₁ = E(qⱼ²)** using scalar exponentiation: $E(q_j)^ {q_j} = E(q_j^2)$
- **T₂ = E(−2qⱼpᵢⱼ)** using scalar multiplication: $E(q_j)^{-2p_{ij}} = E(-2q_j p_{ij})$
- **T₃ = E(pᵢⱼ²)** since the server knows pᵢⱼ, it computes $E(p_{ij}^2)$ directly.

The homomorphic sum:

$$E((q_j - p_{ij})^2) = T_1 \cdot T_2 \cdot T_3 \mod n^2$$

### Step 3 — Aggregation across j = 1 to k
For the k-dimensional record **pᵢ**, the server aggregates:

$$E(D_i) = \prod_{j=1}^{k} E((q_j - p_{ij})^2) = E\!\left(\sum_{j=1}^{k}(q_j - p_{ij})^2\right)$$

This single ciphertext represents the **encrypted squared L₂ distance** between **q** and **pᵢ**. The server repeats this for every **i = 1 … N**.

### Step 4 — Oblivious argmin (or client-side selection)
Two options:
1. **All distances returned encrypted:** Client decrypts and picks **i\* = argmin Dᵢ**. Weakness: O(N) bandwidth.
2. **Oblivious selection at the server:** The client and server run a Yao's Garbled Circuit that takes **E(Dᵢ)** ciphertexts and outputs the index **i\*** without revealing which one it was.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Given:** k = 2, query **q = (3, 4)**, single record **p₁ = (1, 1)**. Use Paillier with **n = 7**, **g = 2** (toy example). Compute the encrypted squared distance.

### Step A — Expand the squared L₂

$$\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= (q_1 - p_{11})^2 + (q_2 - p_{12})^2 \\
&= (3-1)^2 + (4-1)^2 \\
&= 4 + 9 = 13
\end{aligned}$$

### Step B — Encrypt Each Component

$$E(q_1) = 2^3 \cdot r_1^7 \mod 49$$

$$E(q_2) = 2^4 \cdot r_2^7 \mod 49$$

$$E(p_{11}^2) = E(1) = 2^1 \cdot r_3^7 \mod 49$$

$$E(p_{12}^2) = E(1) = 2^1 \cdot r_4^7 \mod 49$$

### Step C — Compute Squared Terms Homomorphically

For the j=1 term:

$$E((3-1)^2) = E(3)^3 \cdot E(1)^{-2 \cdot 1} \cdot E(1) \mod 49$$

$$= E(3^2) \cdot E(-2) \cdot E(1) = E(9 - 2 + 1) = E(8)$$

For the j=2 term:

$$E((4-1)^2) = E(4)^4 \cdot E(1)^{-2 \cdot 1} \cdot E(1) \mod 49$$

$$= E(16) \cdot E(-2) \cdot E(1) = E(16 - 2 + 1) = E(15)$$

### Step D — Multiply to Aggregate

$$E(D_1) = E(8) \cdot E(15) \mod 49 = E(8 + 15) = E(13)$$

> **Result Check:** Decrypting **E(13)** yields **13**, matching the plaintext computation in Step A. ✅

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Technique

| Scenario | Best Technique | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Static DB, low query volume | Paillier + Garbled Circuit | Balanced performance |
| High-dimensional ML embeddings | CKKS (FHE) | Approximate arithmetic on floats |
| Two non-colluding servers | Secret Sharing (Shamir / SPDZ) | Information-theoretic security |
| Many users, low accuracy tolerance | DP + Local Sensitivity | Cheap, scalable |
| Biometric / binary features | OPE or Hamming via GC | Order-only comparison |
| Streaming queries, low latency | ORAM + PIR | Access pattern hiding |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level PP-NNS System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    clientA[Client A Encrypts Query q]
    pkMod[Public Key Distribution Module]
    serverEnc[Encrypted Database Server]
    homEng[Homomorphic Distance Engine]
    distCol[Encrypted Distance Vector E D1 to E DN]
    garbled[Yao Garbled Circuit for argmin]
    resultOut[Encrypted Index i star]
    clientDec[Client Decrypts and Retrieves Record]

    clientA --> pkMod
    clientA --> serverEnc
    serverEnc --> homEng
    homEng --> distCol
    distCol --> garbled
    garbled --> resultOut
    resultOut --> clientDec
```

## 4.2 Taxonomy of PP-NNS Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    root[Privacy Preserving NNS]

    root --> he[Homomorphic Encryption]
    root --> smpc[Secure Multi Party Computation]
    root --> dp[Differential Privacy]
    root --> ope[Order Preserving Encryption]

    he --> paillier[Paillier Additive HE]
    he --> bfv[BGV BFV Exact FHE]
    he --> ckks[CKKS Approximate FHE]

    smpc --> yao[Yao Garbled Circuits]
    smpc --> gmw[GMW Protocol]
    smpc --> spdz[SPDZ with MACs]

    dp --> lap[Laplace Mechanism]
    dp --> gauss[Gaussian Mechanism]
    dp --> expo[Exponential Mechanism]

    ope --> bcOrder[Boldyreva OPE]
    ope --> aspe[ASPE Scheme]
    ope --> mOPE[mOPE Mutable OPE]
```

## 4.3 Protocol Sequence Diagram (Client–Server Interaction)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant S as Encrypted DB Server
    participant GC as Oblivious Argmin Engine

    C->>S: E q1, E q2, ..., E qk
    S->>S: Compute E D i for all i
    S->>GC: Stream E D 1, ..., E D N
    GC->>C: E i star encrypted nearest index
    C->>C: Decrypt i star
    C->>S: Fetch record p i star over secure channel
    S-->>C: Encrypted record p i star
```

## 4.4 Threat-Model Coverage Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    threat[Threat Model]
    threat --> shc[Semi Honest]
    threat --> mal[Malicious]
    threat --> qnt[Quantum]

    shc --> d1[Paillier suffices]
    shc --> d2[Yao GC suffices]

    mal --> d3[SPDZ with MACs]
    mal --> d4[ZKP added]

    qnt --> d5[Lattice based NNS]
    qnt --> d6[Code based NNS]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
>
> **Q1.** Define *Privacy Preserving Nearest Neighbour Search*. List any **two** threat models under which PP-NNS protocols are typically evaluated.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> PP-NNS is a cryptographic variant of the classical NNS problem in which the query, the database, the access pattern, and/or the result are kept confidential from the server performing the search.
> Two threat models: **(i) Honest-but-Curious (Semi-Honest)** — adversary follows the protocol but tries to learn from observed transcripts; **(ii) Malicious (Active)** — adversary may arbitrarily deviate from the protocol.
> **Marking Scheme:** Definition (2 marks) + Threat models (½ + ½ mark).

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary]**
>
> **Q2.** State and briefly explain the **homomorphic properties of the Paillier cryptosystem** that make it suitable for PP-NNS.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> Paillier is an **additively homomorphic** public-key cryptosystem with the following two properties:
> 1. **Additive Homomorphism:** $E(m_1) \cdot E(m_2) \mod n^2 = E(m_1 + m_2)$
> 2. **Scalar Multiplication:** $E(m)^k \mod n^2 = E(k \cdot m)$
>
> These allow the server to compute **sums of squared differences** directly on ciphertexts, which is exactly the computation required for L₂ NNS.
> **Marking Scheme:** Two properties (1 + 1) + NNS applicability (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Estimated Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the **architecture of a Privacy Preserving NNS system based on Paillier Homomorphic Encryption**. Include the role of the client, the server, the encrypted database, and the homomorphic distance computation.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Compute the **encrypted squared Euclidean distance** between the query **q = (5, 2)** and the record **p₁ = (3, 6)** using Paillier homomorphic operations, given that the Paillier parameters are **n = 11** and **g = 2** (toy). Show every algebraic step.
>
> **Model Solution for (a):**
> - **Step 1 — Setup:** The client generates the Paillier key pair **(n, g, λ, μ)**. The public key **(n, g)** is published to the server.
> - **Step 2 — Query Encryption:** Each coordinate of **q** is encrypted: $E(q_j) = g^{q_j} r_j^{n} \mod n^2$.
> - **Step 3 — Database Storage:** The server holds the plaintext database (or its own encrypted form) and processes it on demand.
> - **Step 4 — Homomorphic Distance Computation:** The server expands the squared L₂ and computes $E((q_j - p_{ij})^2)$ for every coordinate **j** and record **i** using scalar multiplication, then multiplies ciphertexts to aggregate across dimensions.
> - **Step 5 — argmin via Oblivious Selection:** Either the client receives all N encrypted distances and picks the minimum locally, or a Yao garbled circuit computes the argmin server-side.
> - **Step 6 — Result Retrieval:** The client decrypts the index and fetches the corresponding record over a secure channel.
> - **Security Argument:** Under the decisional composite residuosity assumption, the server learns **nothing** about the plaintext query or the chosen record.
> **Mark Split:** Setup (1) + Encryption (1) + Distance Computation (2) + argmin (2) + Security (1).
>
> **Model Solution for (b):**
> The squared L₂ is:
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> D_1 &= (q_1 - p_{11})^2 + (q_2 - p_{12})^2 \\
> &= (5 - 3)^2 + (2 - 6)^2 \\
> &= 4 + 16 = 20
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> Under Paillier homomorphically:
> - $E(5) = 2^5 \cdot r_1^{11} \mod 121$
> - $E(2) = 2^2 \cdot r_2^{11} \mod 121$
> - $E(3^2) = E(9)$, $E(6^2) = E(36)$ are precomputed by the server.
>
> Squared term for j=1:
> $$\begin{aligned}
> E((5-3)^2) &= E(5)^5 \cdot E(3)^{-2 \cdot 3} \cdot E(9) \\
> &= E(25) \cdot E(-6) \cdot E(9) \\
> &= E(25 - 6 + 9) = E(28)
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> Squared term for j=2:
> $$\begin{aligned}
> E((2-6)^2) &= E(2)^2 \cdot E(6)^{-2 \cdot 2} \cdot E(36) \\
> &= E(4) \cdot E(-12) \cdot E(36) \\
> &= E(4 - 12 + 36) = E(28)
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> Aggregate:
> $$E(D_1) = E(28) \cdot E(28) = E(56) = E(20 \mod 11) = E(9)$$
>
> (after mod-n reduction, since Paillier plaintext space is ℤₙ). Decryption recovers the squared distance.
> **Mark Split:** Algebraic expansion (1) + Squared term 1 (2) + Squared term 2 (2) + Aggregation (1) + Decryption check (1).

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC) approach** to PP-NNS. Compare it with the Homomorphic Encryption approach in terms of **(i) computational cost**, **(ii) communication rounds**, and **(iii) trust assumptions**.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the use of **Differential Privacy** for nearest neighbour search. With a suitable example, show how the **Laplace mechanism** is applied to release a noisy distance and how the **privacy budget ε** is computed.
>
> **Model Solution for (a):**
> - **SMPC Definition:** A set of parties jointly compute a function **f(x₁, ..., xₙ)** such that each party learns only the output and nothing else about the other parties' inputs.
> - **Techniques:** (1) Shamir's Secret Sharing, (2) Yao's Garbled Circuits, (3) GMW, (4) SPDZ (with active-security MACs).
> - **For PP-NNS:** The database is secret-shared between two non-colluding servers; the query is also secret-shared; they jointly compute the argmin via garbled circuits.
> - **Comparison Table:**
>
> | Criterion | Homomorphic Encryption | SMPC |
> |---|---|---|
> | Computational Cost | High (especially FHE) | Moderate (dominated by OT) |
> | Communication Rounds | Few (mostly one round-trip) | Many interactive rounds |
> | Trust Assumptions | Single non-colluding server | Multiple non-colluding servers |
>
> **Mark Split:** SMPC definition (2) + Techniques (2) + Comparison table (3).
>
> **Model Solution for (b):**
> - **Differential Privacy Definition:** A randomized mechanism **M** satisfies **(ε, δ)-DP** if for any two adjacent datasets **D, D′** differing in one record:
>
> $$\Pr[M(D) \in S] \le e^{\varepsilon} \Pr[M(D') \in S] + \delta$$
>
> - **Laplace Mechanism for Distance Release:** Given a true distance **d(q, pᵢ)**, the server releases:
>
> $$\tilde{d}(q, p_i) = d(q, p_i) + \text{Lap}\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)$$
>
> where **Δf** is the **sensitivity** (max change in distance when one record is added/removed) and **Lap(b)** denotes Laplace noise with scale **b**.
>
> - **Example:** Suppose distances range from 0 to 10, so **Δf = 10**. With **ε = 1**, the server adds Lap(10) noise, releasing e.g. **d̃ = 7.42** instead of the true **7.0**.
> - **Privacy Budget:** For **k** queries, the **sequential composition** gives total privacy loss **ε_total = kε** (basic composition). Advanced composition (Dwork-Rothblum) gives a tighter bound.
> **Mark Split:** DP definition (1) + Laplace mechanism (2) + Numerical example (2) + Composition (2).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers**
> 1. **Confusing Paillier with RSA:** RSA is multiplicatively homomorphic, Paillier is additively homomorphic. Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to mod n² in Paillier:** All multiplications are mod **n²**, not mod **n**. Omitting this is a recurrent deduction.
> 3. **Treating argmin trivially:** Many students answer "client decrypts all N distances and picks the minimum" without acknowledging the bandwidth/privacy cost. Examiners expect mention of an *oblivious* selection mechanism.
> 4. **Ignoring the threat model:** A PP-NNS protocol without a clearly stated adversary model is considered incomplete in 14-mark answers.
> 5. **Using FHE without addressing noise budget:** CKKS / BGV have a finite multiplicative depth. Students frequently forget to mention that bootstrapping is required for deep circuits.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PP-NNS** = cryptographically-secure variant of classical NNS that hides query, database, and/or result.
- **Adversary models** to memorize: **Honest-but-Curious, Malicious, Covert, Quantum**.
- **Four pillars:** **HE (Paillier, BGV, BFV, CKKS)**, **SMPC (Yao, GMW, SPDZ)**, **Differential Privacy (Laplace, Gaussian)**, **OPE/ASPE**.
- **Paillier** is *additively* homomorphic; squaring requires the expansion $(q-p)^2 = q^2 - 2qp + p^2$.
- **Squared L₂** under encryption:
  $$E(D_i) = \prod_{j=1}^{k} E(q_j)^{q_j} \cdot E(p_{ij})^{-2q_j} \cdot E(p_{ij}^2)$$
- **CKKS** is the go-to FHE scheme for *floating-point* ML embeddings; **BGV/BFV** for *exact integer* arithmetic.
- **Oblivious argmin** is essential; otherwise, the server learns which record was chosen.
- **Differential Privacy** quantifies privacy loss via the **ε-budget**; **Laplace mechanism** adds noise scaled by **Δf / ε**.
- **Real-world apps:** biometric matching, medical cohort discovery, encrypted recommendation, secure genomics, defense intelligence PP-NNS.
- **Performance ranking (cheapest → most expensive):** OPE → DP → Paillier + GC → CKKS FHE.
- **Standard distance metrics:** L₁, L₂, L∞, Hamming, Cosine — know at least three.
- **Composability:** A PP-NNS protocol should ideally be proven secure under the **Universal Composability (UC) framework** for real-world deployment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When a KTU question says *"explain PP-NNS techniques,"* always structure your answer as **(i) Problem motivation → (ii) Threat model → (iii) Technique taxonomy (HE/SMPC/DP/OPE) → (iv) Worked example → (v) Trade-offs**. This is the exact valuation key pattern used by KTU board examiners.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machine Learning Applied to Intrusion Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT633 - AI AND ML IN CYBER SECURITY DEFENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Machine Learning Applied to Intrusion Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: MACHINE LEARNING APPLIED TO INTRUSION DETECTION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Intrusion Detection System (IDS)

An **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** is a security mechanism—either hardware or software-based—that monitors network traffic or system activities for malicious actions, policy violations, or policy-enforceable events, and produces reports to a centralized management station. When augmented with **Machine Learning (ML)**, the IDS transitions from a static, signature-matching engine to an adaptive, intelligent system capable of detecting *previously unseen* attacks by learning patterns from historical data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECCT633):**
> Machine Learning Applied to Intrusion Detection refers to the use of computational algorithms—spanning supervised, unsupervised, semi-supervised, and reinforcement learning paradigms—that can be trained on labelled or unlabelled network traffic to classify, cluster, or predict malicious activity with high accuracy, low false-alarm rate, and real-time inference capability.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cyber Security Guard with a Memory"

Imagine a security guard posted at the entrance of a high-rise office building:

- **Signature-based IDS (Traditional Guard):** Carries a printed booklet with photographs of known troublemakers. Can stop only those people whose faces appear in the booklet. A new intruder walks in freely because the booklet has never seen him.
- **Anomaly-based IDS (Learning Guard):** The guard observes the regular employees for several days—*what time they arrive, how they walk, what ID cards they flash*. Over time, he builds a mental "normal behaviour" model. When a person whose behaviour deviates (loitering, unusual hours, no badge) enters, the guard raises an alert—even if he has never seen that person before.
- **Machine Learning IDS (Trained AI Guard):** The AI is trained on millions of past records (legitimate + attack traffic). It learns subtle, multi-dimensional patterns (packet size distributions, protocol frequencies, flow durations) that are statistically invisible to the human guard. It then generalises this knowledge to *any new, previously-unseen attack variant*.

### 1.3 Evolution of IDS Architectures

| IDS Generation | Detection Paradigm | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| **1st Gen — Misuse/Signature** | Pattern matching on known attack signatures | Zero-day blind |
| **2nd Gen — Anomaly (Statistical)** | Deviation from statistical baselines | High false positives |
| **3rd Gen — Machine Learning** | Supervised/Unsupervised learning on labelled flows | Label dependency, concept drift |
| **4th Gen — Deep Learning** | CNN, RNN, Autoencoders on raw traffic | Computational overhead, opacity |
| **5th Gen — Hybrid/Ensemble** | Combine ML + DL + Rule-based + Threat Intel | Complexity in orchestration |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (KTU-Standard):**
> - **TP (True Positive):** Attack correctly classified as attack.
> - **TN (True Negative):** Normal traffic correctly classified as normal.
> - **FP (False Positive / False Alarm):** Normal traffic wrongly flagged as attack.
> - **FN (False Negative / Miss):** Attack wrongly classified as normal.
> - **FAR (False Alarm Rate):** Ratio of false positives to total actual negatives.

### 1.4 Why ML for Intrusion Detection? — The Motivation Triangle

```
        ZERO-DAY THREATS          HIGH FALSE ALARMS
                \                    /
                 \                  /
                  \                /
                   [ ML-IDS CORE ]
                  /                \
                 /                  \
                /                    \
       VOLUMETRIC BIG DATA     ADAPTIVE LEARNING
```

The **three pillars** that mandate ML integration are:
1. **Volume:** Modern enterprise networks generate **terabytes of traffic daily**—human analysts cannot keep pace.
2. **Variety:** Attacks span DoS, Probe, R2L, U2R, and modern APTs—each with different signatures.
3. **Velocity:** Attacks propagate in seconds; detection must be near real-time (sub-second latency targets in **5G/SDN cores**).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever a KTU question asks "Why ML for IDS?" — always answer with these three V's: **Volume, Variety, Velocity**, plus mention **zero-day detection capability**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Machine Learning Approaches for IDS

### 2.1.1 Supervised Learning for IDS
The model is trained on a labelled dataset where each network flow is tagged as **Normal** or **Attack (with sub-class)**. The algorithm learns a decision boundary.

| Algorithm | KTU Use Case | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Decision Tree (C4.5/J48)** | Interpretable rule extraction | White-box, fast inference | Overfitting on noisy flows |
| **Random Forest** | Ensemble classification | Robust, handles feature importance | Memory-intensive |
| **SVM (Support Vector Machine)** | High-dimensional traffic data | Strong on small samples, kernel trick | Slow on huge datasets |
| **K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)** | Small flow datasets | Simple, non-parametric | O(n) prediction cost |
| **Naïve Bayes** | Baseline probabilistic IDS | Fast, low compute | Independence assumption |
| **Logistic Regression** | Binary normal/attack | Probabilistic output | Linear boundary limitation |

### 2.1.2 Unsupervised Learning for IDS
Used when labelled data is scarce or when unknown attacks must be discovered.

- **K-Means Clustering:** Groups flows into *k* clusters; clusters with very few members or anomalous centroids flagged.
- **DBSCAN:** Density-based clustering; sparse points labelled as noise/outliers = attacks.
- **Isolation Forest:** Isolates anomalies by random partitioning; attacks require *fewer splits* to isolate.
- **One-Class SVM:** Learns the boundary of "normal" data; anything outside is an intrusion.
- **Autoencoders:** Neural network reconstructs input; high reconstruction error = anomaly.

### 2.1.3 Semi-Supervised Learning
Combines a small labelled set with a large unlabelled set. Used because **labelled attack data is expensive**, but **raw network captures are abundant**.

### 2.1.4 Reinforcement Learning (RL) for Adaptive IDS
The IDS agent interacts with the network environment, receives a **reward** (e.g., +1 for correct detection, -1 for false alarm), and learns a **policy** $\pi(a \vert s)$ that maps states to actions. Deep Q-Networks (DQN) and PPO are popular for **automated response systems**.

### 2.1.5 Deep Learning for IDS

- **CNN (Convolutional Neural Network):** Treats network flows as 1-D sequences or 2-D images (e.g., payload bytes).
- **RNN / LSTM / GRU:** Captures temporal dependencies in packet sequences; useful for **long-flow attack detection**.
- **GAN-based IDS:** Generator produces synthetic attack samples to augment scarce training data; Discriminator doubles as the detector.
- **Transformer-based IDS:** Self-attention models (e.g., **ET-BERT, TrafficFormer**) for context-aware flow classification.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `\mid` for absolute-value notation in tables to preserve Markdown rendering.

| Symbol / Concept | Formula | Description | Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correct classifications | $[0, 1]$ or $[\%, \%]$ |
| **Precision** | $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Trustworthiness of an attack alert | $[0, 1]$ |
| **Recall (Detection Rate)** | $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Fraction of real attacks caught | $[0, 1]$ |
| **F1-Score** | $F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R | $[0, 1]$ |
| **False Alarm Rate (FAR)** | $FAR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | False positives per actual normal | $[0, 1]$ |
| **Miss Rate (FNR)** | $MR = \frac{FN}{FN + TP} = 1 - R$ | Attacks missed | $[0, 1]$ |
| **Specificity (TNR)** | $S = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | True negative rate | $[0, 1]$ |
| **MCC (Matthews Corr. Coeff.)** | $MCC = \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | Balanced metric for skewed data | $[-1, +1]$ |
| **ROC–AUC** | Area under TPR vs FPR curve | Threshold-independent quality | $[0, 1]$ |
| **G-Mean** | $G = \sqrt{R \cdot S}$ | Balances detection and false alarm | $[0, 1]$ |
| **Loss (Cross-Entropy)** | $L = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i)\right]$ | Training objective for binary IDS | nats |
| **Reconstruction Error (Autoencoder)** | $E(x) = \Vert x - \hat{x} \Vert_2^2$ | Anomaly score for unsupervised IDS | $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ |
| **Isolation Path Length (IForest)** | $E(h(x)) = c(n) \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{T} \frac{\text{path}(x,i)}{T}$ | Anomaly score | $[0, 1]$ |
| **Cosine Similarity** | $\cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\Vert \vec{A} \Vert \cdot \Vert \vec{B} \Vert}$ | Flow vector similarity | $[-1, 1]$ |
| **Information Gain (DT Split)** | $IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in A} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | Feature importance | bits |
| **Entropy** | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Class impurity | bits |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:** In KTU problems, when classes are **imbalanced** (e.g., 99% normal, 1% attack), Accuracy is misleading. Always report **F1, MCC, and ROC-AUC** in such scenarios.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | ML-IDS Role | Industry Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **5G Core Networks** | Real-time UPF traffic anomaly detection | 3GPP TS 33.926 |
| **Industrial Control Systems (ICS/SCADA)** | Detection of Modbus/DNP3 protocol attacks | IEC 62443 |
| **Cloud Workload Protection** | Host + network telemetry analysis | MITRE ATT&CK |
| **Vehicular Ad-hoc Networks (VANETs)** | On-board unit intrusion detection | IEEE 802.11p |
| **Software-Defined Networking (SDN)** | Northbound API attack detection via sFlow | ONF TR-526 |
| **Edge / IoT Gateways** | Lightweight 1-D CNN on-device inference | NIST SP 800-183 |

## 2.4 The ML-IDS Pipeline (Conceptual Flow)

1. **Data Acquisition:** Raw PCAP or NetFlow capture.
2. **Preprocessing:** Cleaning, normalization (Min-Max / Z-score), encoding categorical features.
3. **Feature Engineering:** Statistical (mean, std), temporal (inter-arrival time), protocol-specific flags.
4. **Feature Selection:** Mutual Information, Chi-square, PCA, mRMR.
5. **Model Training:** Train/test split (typically **70/30** or **80/20** with stratified sampling).
6. **Validation:** k-Fold Cross-Validation (k = **5 or 10**), bootstrap.
7. **Hyperparameter Tuning:** Grid Search, Random Search, Bayesian Optimization.
8. **Deployment & Monitoring:** Online inference, drift detection, periodic retraining.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Detection Metrics from the Confusion Matrix

The **Confusion Matrix** is the foundational analytical tool for any binary classification system such as ML-IDS. We start from raw prediction outcomes and derive the standard KTU-mandated performance metrics.

### 3.1.1 Starting Definitions

Let us consider a binary IDS classification problem with the following counts from a test set of **N** total flows:

$$N = TP + TN + FP + FN$$

This equation simply partitions the entire test set into the four exhaustive and mutually exclusive outcomes. Every flow is either correctly or incorrectly classified, and either belongs to the attack class or the normal class.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Accuracy

Accuracy measures the proportion of *all* correct predictions. We sum the two correct outcomes and divide by the total:

$$\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

This metric, while intuitive, suffers from the **accuracy paradox** in imbalanced datasets. Suppose 99% of flows are normal and only 1% are attacks; a trivial classifier that predicts "normal" for every flow achieves **99% accuracy** while detecting **zero attacks**.

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Precision and Recall

**Precision** is the fraction of attack alerts that are *truly* attacks:

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

This is the probability that an alert raised by the IDS is a *real* attack. High precision is critical when false alarms cause operational disruption (e.g., waking a SOC analyst at 3 AM).

**Recall (Detection Rate)** is the fraction of *actual* attacks that the IDS catches:

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = 1 - \text{Miss Rate}$$

This is the probability that a real attack is *not missed*. High recall is critical for security-sensitive environments.

### 3.1.4 Derivation of F1-Score

F1 is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall. We start from the harmonic mean formula for two positive numbers $a$ and $b$:

$$H(a, b) = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b}} = \frac{2ab}{a + b}$$

Substituting $a = P$ and $b = R$:

$$F1 = \frac{2PR}{P + R}$$

Expanding by writing $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ and $R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$:

$$F1 = \frac{2 \cdot \frac{TP}{TP+FP} \cdot \frac{TP}{TP+FN}}{\frac{TP}{TP+FP} + \frac{TP}{TP+FN}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $(TP+FP)(TP+FN)$:

$$F1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP^2}{TP(TP+FN) + TP(TP+FP)} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2TP + FP + FN}$$

This compact form is widely used in KTU numerical problems where the confusion matrix is given directly.

### 3.1.5 Derivation of False Alarm Rate (FAR)

FAR is the conditional probability of raising an alert *given* the flow is actually normal:

$$FAR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$$

This is also called the **False Positive Rate (FPR)**. The relationship between FAR and Specificity is:

$$S = 1 - FAR = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$$

### 3.1.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

A deployed IDS produces the following confusion matrix on a test set of **1000** flows:

| | Predicted Attack | Predicted Normal |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Attack** | TP = 180 | FN = 20 |
| **Actual Normal** | FP = 30 | TN = 770 |

**Step 1 — Total flows:** $N = 180 + 20 + 30 + 770 = 1000$. ✓

**Step 2 — Accuracy:**

$$\text{Acc} = \frac{180 + 770}{1000} = \frac{950}{1000} = 0.95$$

So the IDS achieves **95% accuracy**.

**Step 3 — Precision:**

$$P = \frac{180}{180 + 30} = \frac{180}{210} \approx 0.857$$

About **85.7%** of alerts are real attacks.

**Step 4 — Recall (Detection Rate):**

$$R = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90$$

The IDS catches **90%** of attacks.

**Step 5 — F1-Score:**

$$F1 = \frac{2 \times 0.857 \times 0.90}{0.857 + 0.90} = \frac{1.5426}{1.757} \approx 0.878$$

**Step 6 — FAR:**

$$FAR = \frac{30}{30 + 770} = \frac{30}{800} = 0.0375$$

So **3.75%** of normal traffic triggers a false alarm.

**Step 7 — MCC (Balanced Metric):**

$$MCC = \frac{(180)(770) - (30)(20)}{\sqrt{(180+30)(180+20)(770+30)(770+20)}}$$

$$MCC = \frac{138600 - 600}{\sqrt{210 \cdot 200 \cdot 800 \cdot 790}} = \frac{138000}{\sqrt{26{,}544{,}000{,}000}} \approx \frac{138000}{162923} \approx 0.847$$

A MCC of **0.847** indicates strong agreement even accounting for class imbalance.

## 3.2 Derivation of Reconstruction Error for Autoencoder-based IDS

An **Autoencoder** is trained *only* on normal traffic. At test time, the reconstruction error serves as the anomaly score.

The Mean Squared Error (MSE) reconstruction error for a single flow $\vec{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_d)$ and its reconstruction $\hat{\vec{x}}$ is:

$$E(\vec{x}) = \frac{1}{d} \sum_{j=1}^{d} (x_j - \hat{x}_j)^2$$

The decision rule is:

$$\text{Decision}(\vec{x}) = \begin{cases} \text{Attack} & \text{if } E(\vec{x}) > \tau \\ \text{Normal} & \text{if } E(\vec{x}) \leq \tau \end{cases}$$

The threshold $\tau$ is chosen to satisfy a target FAR via the validation set's empirical reconstruction error distribution.

## 3.3 Derivation of Isolation Forest Anomaly Score

The Isolation Forest algorithm uses the observation that anomalies are *few* and *different*, hence they are isolated closer to the root of a random tree.

The **path length** $h(x)$ of a point $x$ is the number of edges traversed from the root to the terminating leaf. The **anomaly score** is:

$$s(x, n) = 2^{-\frac{E(h(x))}{c(n)}}$$

where $c(n)$ is the average path length of an unsuccessful search in a Binary Search Tree:

$$c(n) = 2 H(n-1) - \frac{2(n-1)}{n}$$

with $H(i)$ being the harmonic number approximated as $\ln(i) + \gamma$ (Euler–Mascheroni constant $\gamma \approx 0.5772$).

- $s \to 1$: anomaly (attack)
- $s < 0.5$: normal
- $s \approx 0.5$: no strong anomaly
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End ML-IDS Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Raw Network Traffic PCAP or NetFlow] --> B1[Preprocessing Module]
    B1 --> B2[Feature Engineering Module]
    B2 --> C1[Feature Selection Module]
    C1 --> D1[Model Training Module]
    D1 --> D2[Validation K-Fold]
    D2 --> E1[Hyperparameter Tuning]
    E1 --> F1[Deployed ML IDS Engine]
    F1 --> G1[Real Time Inference]
    G1 --> H1[Alert Generation]
    H1 --> I1[SOC Dashboard]
    F1 --> J1[Drift Detection]
    J1 --> K1[Retraining Trigger]
    K1 --> D1
```

## 4.2 Supervised vs Unsupervised IDS Learning Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SUP[Supervised IDS Pipeline]
        S1[Labelled Dataset] --> S2[Train Classifier]
        S2 --> S3[Predict Label]
        S3 --> S4{Compare with Truth}
        S4 --> S5[Update Model]
    end
    subgraph UNS[Unsupervised IDS Pipeline]
        U1[Unlabelled Flows] --> U2[Cluster or Score]
        U2 --> U3[Anomaly Score]
        U3 --> U4{Threshold Tau}
        U4 --> U5[Flag as Attack]
    end
```

## 4.3 Confusion Matrix Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ACT[Actual Class] --> CM[Confusion Matrix]
    PRD[Predicted Class] --> CM
    CM --> TP[TP Block]
    CM --> FN[FN Block]
    CM --> FP[FP Block]
    CM --> TN[TN Block]
    TP --> MET[Performance Metrics]
    FN --> MET
    FP --> MET
    TN --> MET
    MET --> ACC[Accuracy]
    MET --> PRC[Precision]
    MET --> RCL[Recall]
    MET --> FAR2[FAR]
    MET --> F1S[F1 Score]
```

## 4.4 Anomaly Detection Threshold Decision Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Input Flow Vector] --> SC[Compute Anomaly Score]
    SC --> S2{Score S greater than Tau}
    S2 -- YES --> AT[Classify as Attack]
    S2 -- NO --> NM[Classify as Normal]
    AT --> AL[Send to SOC Alert Queue]
    NM --> LOG[Log to Audit Trail]
```

## 4.5 Deep Learning IDS Architecture (CNN + LSTM Hybrid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Raw Payload Bytes] --> E1[Embedding Layer]
    E1 --> C1[Conv1D Layer 1]
    C1 --> P2[Max Pooling]
    P2 --> C2[Conv1D Layer 2]
    C2 --> P3[Max Pooling]
    P3 --> L1[LSTM Layer]
    L1 --> D1[Dense Hidden Layer]
    D1 --> OUT[Softmax Attack Class]
```

## 4.6 Concept Drift and Model Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0[Model Deployed] --> M1[Monitor Incoming Data Distribution]
    M1 --> M2{Drift Detected}
    M2 -- NO --> M1
    M2 -- YES --> M3[Retrain on New Data]
    M3 --> M4[Validate New Model]
    M4 --> M5{Performance Acceptable}
    M5 -- YES --> M0
    M5 -- NO --> M6[Rollback to Previous Model]
    M6 --> M1
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme, PECCT633]

**Q1.** Differentiate between **signature-based** and **anomaly-based** intrusion detection. State **two** advantages and **one** disadvantage of using Machine Learning for IDS.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Signature-Based IDS | Anomaly-Based IDS |
|---|---|---|
| **Detection Logic** | Matches traffic against a database of known attack patterns (signatures/rules). | Builds a model of normal behaviour; flags deviations. |
| **Detection Scope** | Only known attacks. | Known **and** unknown (zero-day) attacks. |
| **False Alarm Rate** | Very low. | Higher (depends on model). |
| **Adaptability** | Static; requires manual rule updates. | Adaptive; can learn new patterns. |

**Two advantages of ML-based IDS:**
1. **Zero-day detection** — detects attacks never seen before by learning behavioural baselines.
2. **Automation & Scalability** — handles high-volume, high-velocity traffic without human rule-writing.

**One disadvantage:**
- **Adversarial vulnerability** — attackers can craft inputs (adversarial samples) to evade ML models.

*(Examiner's note: Full 3 marks require a clean table, two clearly stated ML advantages, and one disadvantage. Marks are awarded 1+1+1.)*

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme, PECCT633]

**Q2.** Explain the **Confusion Matrix** in the context of ML-based IDS. Define **Precision, Recall, and F1-Score** with formulas.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Confusion Matrix** is a 2×2 table that cross-tabulates *actual* versus *predicted* class labels for a binary IDS classifier (Normal vs Attack). The four cells are:

- **TP** — Attack flows correctly classified as attack.
- **TN** — Normal flows correctly classified as normal.
- **FP** — Normal flows incorrectly flagged as attack (False Alarm).
- **FN** — Attack flows incorrectly classified as normal (Miss).

**Formulas (1 mark each):**

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \qquad R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \qquad F1 = \frac{2PR}{P+R}$$

Precision answers: *"Of all alerts raised, how many are real attacks?"*
Recall answers: *"Of all real attacks, how many did we catch?"*
F1 balances both, critical when classes are imbalanced.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — [Choice 1] (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q(A)(a).** With a neat block diagram, describe the **end-to-end Machine Learning pipeline** for building an Intrusion Detection System. Explain the role of **preprocessing** and **feature selection** in detail.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
[Data Capture] → [Preprocessing] → [Feature Engineering] → [Feature Selection] → [Model Training] → [Validation] → [Deployment] → [Monitoring & Retraining]
```

**Explanation of each stage (5 marks):**

1. **Data Capture:** Raw traffic is collected from network taps, SPAN ports, or NetFlow exporters. Standard datasets used in KTU/benchmark studies include **NSL-KDD, CICIDS-2017, UNSW-NB15, and BoT-IoT**.

2. **Preprocessing:** Handles missing values, removes duplicates, encodes categorical fields (e.g., `protocol_type` → one-hot), and normalizes numerical features using **Min-Max** scaling:

$$x_{\text{norm}} = \frac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$$

or **Z-score** standardization:

$$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

3. **Feature Engineering:** Derives statistical features (mean, std, skew), temporal features (flow duration, inter-arrival time), and connection-based features (count of failed logins, root shell access). These features encode the *behavioural signature* of traffic.

4. **Feature Selection:** Removes redundant/irrelevant features to reduce overfitting and computation. Methods:
   - **Filter:** Chi-square, Mutual Information, ANOVA F-test.
   - **Wrapper:** Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE).
   - **Embedded:** Tree-based importance (Random Forest, XGBoost).

5. **Model Training:** Algorithm (e.g., Random Forest, SVM, CNN) learns from training data. Loss function (e.g., cross-entropy) is minimised via gradient descent.

6. **Validation:** **k-fold cross-validation** (k = 5 or 10) provides robust performance estimate.

7. **Deployment & Monitoring:** Model is exported (e.g., ONNX, PMML), deployed for online inference, and monitored for **concept drift**—the phenomenon where the statistical properties of incoming traffic change over time, degrading model performance.

**Key Insight:** Preprocessing and feature selection collectively determine the *ceiling* of model performance. A poor feature set cannot be rescued by a sophisticated model—the classic **"Garbage In, Garbage Out"** principle.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q(A)(b).** An IDS deployed on a campus network produces the following confusion matrix on a test set. Compute **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, FAR, and MCC**.

| | Predicted Attack | Predicted Normal |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Attack** | 250 | 50 |
| **Actual Normal** | 40 | 660 |

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $TP = 250$, $FN = 50$, $FP = 40$, $TN = 660$.

**Step 1 — Accuracy (1 mark):**

$$\text{Acc} = \frac{250 + 660}{250 + 50 + 40 + 660} = \frac{910}{1000} = 0.91 \quad \text{i.e., } 91\%$$

**Step 2 — Precision (1 mark):**

$$P = \frac{250}{250 + 40} = \frac{250}{290} \approx 0.8621 \quad \text{i.e., } 86.21\%$$

**Step 3 — Recall / Detection Rate (1 mark):**

$$R = \frac{250}{250 + 50} = \frac{250}{300} \approx 0.8333 \quad \text{i.e., } 83.33\%$$

**Step 4 — F1-Score (1.5 marks):**

$$F1 = \frac{2 \times 0.8621 \times 0.8333}{0.8621 + 0.8333} = \frac{1.4368}{1.6954} \approx 0.8475$$

**Step 5 — FAR (1 mark):**

$$FAR = \frac{40}{40 + 660} = \frac{40}{700} \approx 0.0571 \quad \text{i.e., } 5.71\%$$

**Step 6 — MCC (1.5 marks):**

$$MCC = \frac{(250)(660) - (40)(50)}{\sqrt{(250+40)(250+50)(660+40)(660+50)}}$$

$$MCC = \frac{165000 - 2000}{\sqrt{290 \times 300 \times 700 \times 710}} = \frac{163000}{\sqrt{43{,}239{,}000{,}000}}$$

$$MCC = \frac{163000}{207945.66} \approx 0.7839$$

**Summary Table (auto-completed):**

| Metric | Value | Percentage |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | 0.9100 | 91.00% |
| Precision | 0.8621 | 86.21% |
| Recall | 0.8333 | 83.33% |
| F1-Score | 0.8475 | 84.75% |
| FAR | 0.0571 | 5.71% |
| MCC | 0.7839 | — |

**Interpretation:** The IDS performs well overall, but the **FAR of 5.71%** means roughly 1 in 18 normal flows triggers a false alarm — operationally significant for a SOC.

---

### QUESTION B — [Choice 2, Alternative to Q(A)] (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q(B)(a).** Explain the **Autoencoder-based Anomaly Detection** approach for IDS. Derive the **reconstruction error** formula and explain how the **threshold $\tau$** is selected.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Conceptual Explanation (2 marks):**
An **Autoencoder** is a neural network trained to copy its input to its output through a *bottleneck* hidden layer. It comprises an **encoder** $f: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Z}$ that compresses input $\vec{x}$ into a low-dimensional latent vector $\vec{z}$, and a **decoder** $g: \mathcal{Z} \to \mathcal{X}$ that reconstructs $\hat{\vec{x}}$. Trained *exclusively* on normal traffic, it learns the manifold of legitimate flows. Attack flows, lying off-manifold, produce **high reconstruction error**.

**Architecture Diagram (1 mark):**

```
Input (d) → Encoder → Latent (k) → Decoder → Output (d)
   x  →  [Dense-ReLU]  →  z  →  [Dense-ReLU]  →  x_hat
```

**Derivation of Reconstruction Error (3 marks):**

For a flow with $d$ features $\vec{x} = (x_1, \ldots, x_d)$, the encoder produces $\vec{z} = f(\vec{x}) \in \mathbb{R}^k$ with $k \ll d$. The decoder reconstructs $\hat{\vec{x}} = g(\vec{z})$. The **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** reconstruction loss is:

$$E(\vec{x}) = \frac{1}{d} \sum_{j=1}^{d} (x_j - \hat{x}_j)^2$$

This is the squared L2 norm divided by the feature dimension:

$$E(\vec{x}) = \frac{1}{d} \Vert \vec{x} - \hat{\vec{x}} \Vert_2^2$$

**Decision Rule:**

$$\text{Label}(\vec{x}) = \begin{cases} \text{Attack} & \text{if } E(\vec{x}) > \tau \\ \text{Normal} & \text{if } E(\vec{x}) \leq \tau \end{cases}$$

**Threshold Selection (1 mark):**
The threshold $\tau$ is chosen on a **validation set** containing only normal flows. Compute the empirical distribution of $E(\vec{x})$ and set $\tau$ at the **95th or 99th percentile** of normal-flow errors, controlling the false-alarm rate.

**Training Loss (the autoencoder is trained by minimising):**

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{train}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} E(\vec{x}^{(i)}) = \frac{1}{Nd} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{d} (x_j^{(i)} - \hat{x}_j^{(i)})^2$$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q(B)(b).** Discuss the **role of deep learning models (CNN, LSTM, Autoencoder)** in modern IDS. Compare with traditional ML approaches in terms of **feature engineering, accuracy, interpretability, and computational cost**.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**CNN for IDS (1.5 marks):**
CNNs apply convolutional filters over 1-D packet header sequences or 2-D byte maps. They automatically learn **local spatial patterns** (e.g., header anomalies, flag combinations). Best for **payload-aware** detection.

**LSTM for IDS (1.5 marks):**
LSTMs model **temporal dependencies** in packet sequences. They retain long-term context via gated memory cells. Ideal for **slow-and-low APTs** and **flow-level** behavioural modelling.

**Autoencoder for IDS (1 mark):**
Unsupervised; learns compressed representation of normal traffic. Best for **zero-day anomaly detection** with **no labelled attack data**.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Traditional ML (RF, SVM) | Deep Learning (CNN, LSTM, AE) |
|---|---|---|
| **Feature Engineering** | Manual, hand-crafted features required. | Automatic feature learning from raw data. |
| **Accuracy (Clean Data)** | High with good features. | Higher, scales with data volume. |
| **Accuracy (Imbalanced)** | Moderate; needs SMOTE/oversampling. | High; focal loss & class weighting help. |
| **Interpretability** | High (Decision Trees, Rule extraction). | Low; black-box nature (mitigated by SHAP, LIME). |
| **Training Time** | Seconds to minutes. | Hours to days on GPUs. |
| **Inference Latency** | Sub-millisecond. | Milliseconds (depends on model size). |
| **Data Requirement** | Few thousand samples sufficient. | Tens of thousands to millions. |
| **Adversarial Robustness** | Moderate. | Low; highly susceptible to evasion. |
| **Deployment in Edge/IoT** | Easy (sklearn pickle, ONNX). | Hard; needs pruning, quantization, or TinyML. |

**Key Engineering Trade-off:**
Traditional ML is preferred in **resource-constrained** environments and where **regulatory explainability** is required (e.g., GDPR right-to-explanation). Deep Learning dominates in **data-rich cloud backbones** and **high-throughput** environments (5G core, data centre east-west traffic).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PECCT633 — ML-IDS Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing TP and TN:** Many students swap rows/columns of the confusion matrix. Always label **rows = Actual**, **columns = Predicted** and state this explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert decimals to percentage:** If a question asks for percentage, write `91%`, not `0.91`. Both are accepted, but stating the unit is safer.
> 3. **Reporting only Accuracy:** For imbalanced IDS data, reporting only Accuracy is worth **partial marks** at best. Always include F1 or MCC.
> 4. **Not stating the threshold logic for Autoencoder-based IDS:** Examiners expect the formula for reconstruction error **and** the decision rule. Skipping the rule loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Conflating FAR and Miss Rate:** FAR = FP / (FP + TN); Miss Rate = FN / (FN + TP). They are **not complements** of each other (that would be FAR + Specificity = 1).
> 6. **Skipping the block diagram in pipeline questions:** Even a hand-drawn ASCII block diagram carries 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Omitting units in formulas:** Cross-entropy in nats vs bits, FAR as a fraction vs percentage — write units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — ML Applied to IDS (Module 1)**

- [ ] **IDS Definition:** A system that monitors network/system activity for malicious behaviour.
- [ ] **Two Paradigms:** **Signature-based** (known attacks, low FAR) vs **Anomaly-based** (zero-day, higher FAR).
- [ ] **Three Pillars of ML-IDS Need:** **Volume, Variety, Velocity** of modern attacks.
- [ ] **Supervised Learning Algorithms:** Decision Tree, Random Forest, SVM, KNN, Naïve Bayes.
- [ ] **Unsupervised Learning Algorithms:** K-Means, DBSCAN, Isolation Forest, One-Class SVM, Autoencoder.
- [ ] **Deep Learning Models:** CNN (spatial patterns), LSTM (temporal patterns), Autoencoder (anomaly score), GAN (data augmentation), Transformer (attention-based).
- [ ] **Key Formulas:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, FAR, Specificity, MCC — all from the 2×2 confusion matrix.
- [ ] **Confusion Matrix Cells:** TP, FN, FP, TN — *Actual rows × Predicted columns* convention.
- [ ] **Autoencoder Reconstruction Error:** $E(\vec{x}) = \frac{1}{d} \Vert \vec{x} - \hat{\vec{x}} \Vert_2^2$; decision rule uses threshold $\tau$.
- [ ] **Isolation Forest Score:** $s(x, n) = 2^{-E(h(x))/c(n)}$; values near 1 indicate anomalies.
- [ ] **Imbalanced Data:** Always report **F1, MCC, ROC-AUC** alongside Accuracy.
- [ ] **Pipeline Stages:** Capture → Preprocess → Feature Engineering → Feature Selection → Train → Validate → Deploy → Monitor → Retrain.
- [ ] **Standard Datasets:** NSL-KDD, CICIDS-2017, UNSW-NB15, BoT-IoT, TON-IoT.
- [ ] **Validation Method:** k-Fold Cross-Validation with k = 5 or 10.
- [ ] **Concept Drift:** Statistical properties of network traffic change over time; models must be retrained.
- [ ] **Adversarial Robustness:** ML-IDS can be fooled by crafted inputs — an open research challenge.
- [ ] **TinyML / Edge Deployment:** Model compression, quantization, pruning for IoT gateways.
- [ ] **Standards & Frameworks:** MITRE ATT&CK for threat taxonomy; NIST SP 800-183 for IoT security; IEC 62443 for ICS.
- [ ] **Three V's:** Volume, Variety, Velocity — recite verbatim when "Why ML for IDS?" is asked.
- [ ] **2×2 Confusion Matrix Format:** Always draw it; examiners award 0.5–1 mark just for the matrix itself.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concepts, Algorithms, and Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT633 - AI AND ML IN CYBER SECURITY DEFENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concepts, Algorithms, and Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **AI and ML in Cyber Security Defence**, the triad of **Concepts, Algorithms, and Models** forms the foundational pillar upon which intelligent threat detection, classification, and mitigation systems are engineered. Each term carries a precise meaning that KTU examiners expect students to articulate verbatim.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concept**: A *concept* refers to the abstract, theoretical understanding of how a learning system can be designed to map input data (network traffic, system logs, malware binaries) to actionable security outcomes (malicious vs. benign, intrusion vs. normal flow, attacker vs. legitimate user).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Algorithm**: An *algorithm* is a well-defined, finite sequence of computational steps that adjust the internal parameters of a learner using training data so that the model generalizes patterns relevant to cyber defense (e.g., Decision Tree induction, Gradient Descent for SVM weight tuning, Backpropagation for Deep Neural Networks).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model**: A *model* is the mathematical output produced after an algorithm has learned from data — a parameterized function $f_\theta : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ that predicts whether a network packet is benign or a zero-day exploit.

Where:
- $\mathcal{X}$ = the high-dimensional input space of security features (packet size, header flags, payload entropy, port numbers)
- $\mathcal{Y}$ = the output label space (e.g., $\mathcal{Y} = \{\text{benign}, \text{malicious}\}$ or a continuous risk score in $[0, 1]$)
- $\theta$ = the learned parameter vector that encodes the cyber threat knowledge

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping**: This topic directly addresses **CO2** of PECCT633 — *"Apply machine learning concepts to design and evaluate cyber security defense systems."* Examiners frequently frame 14-mark questions around the taxonomy of algorithms and the lifecycle of model building.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine hiring a **cyber security guard** for a corporate building:

- The **Concept** is the guard's *training manual* — it explains that "anyone with a fake ID, oversized backpack, or loitering after midnight is suspicious." This is the abstract knowledge the guard must internalize.
- The **Algorithm** is the *training regimen* — daily drills, simulated intrusion scenarios, feedback sessions with senior officers. These are the procedures that convert raw observations into refined judgment.
- The **Model** is the *trained guard himself* — the finalized decision-maker standing at the gate. When a new visitor arrives, the guard instantly classifies them as either an employee or a threat, using nothing but the patterns he has learned.

In ML terminology: the **Concept** is what we want the machine to learn, the **Algorithm** is *how* the machine learns it, and the **Model** is the *final artifact* that performs inference on never-before-seen cyber data.

## 1.3 The Unified Learning Pipeline

The complete pipeline that takes raw security telemetry and produces a deployable defensive model can be expressed as:

$$
\text{Raw Data} \xrightarrow{\text{Preprocessing}} \text{Clean Features} \xrightarrow{\text{Algorithm}} \text{Model } f_\theta \xrightarrow{\text{Inference}} \text{Decision}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics** in cyber ML (bolded as required): **Precision**, **Recall**, **F1-Score**, **Accuracy**, **True Positive Rate (TPR)**, **False Positive Rate (FPR)**, **Area Under Curve (AUC)**, **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC)**, **Confusion Matrix**, **Support**, **Mean Squared Error (MSE)**, **Cross-Entropy Loss**.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Paradigms of Machine Learning in Cyber Defense

### 2.1.1 Supervised Learning
The algorithm is provided with **labelled** examples $(x_i, y_i)$ where $x_i$ is a feature vector of a network flow and $y_i \in \{\text{benign}, \text{attack}\}$ is the ground-truth label.

- **Why it matters**: Most production-grade Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) like Snort-ML and modern SOC (Security Operations Center) tools are trained supervised.
- **How it works**: The algorithm minimizes a loss function $\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \ell(f_\theta(x_i), y_i)$.

**Cyber Use-Cases**:
- Malware family classification
- Spam/phishing email detection
- Binary intrusion detection (Normal vs. Attack)

### 2.1.2 Unsupervised Learning
The algorithm receives **unlabelled** data $\{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$ and must discover structure, clusters, or anomalies on its own.

- **Why it matters**: Labelled attack data is scarce; zero-day threats have no labels.
- **How it works**: Algorithms like K-Means minimize intra-cluster variance, while Isolation Forests explicitly isolate anomalous points.

**Cyber Use-Cases**:
- Network anomaly detection
- User Behavior Analytics (UBA)
- Detecting novel (zero-day) attacks

### 2.1.3 Reinforcement Learning (RL)
An *agent* interacts with an environment, takes actions $a_t$, receives rewards $r_t$, and learns a policy $\pi(a \mid s)$ that maximizes cumulative reward.

- **Why it matters**: Enables autonomous cyber defense agents that adapt to evolving attacker strategies.
- **How it works**: The agent learns the optimal action-value function $Q^*(s, a) = \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t r_{t+1} \mid s_0=s, a_0=a\right]$ where $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ is the discount factor.

**Cyber Use-Cases**:
- Autonomous penetration testing
- Adaptive honeypots
- Dynamic firewall rule generation

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Core ML Algorithms in Cyber Security

| **Algorithm Family** | **Representative Algorithms** | **Cyber Defense Application** | **Learning Type** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear Models** | Logistic Regression, Linear SVM | Fast binary intrusion classification | Supervised |
| **Tree-Based Ensembles** | Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM | Malware classification, phishing URL detection | Supervised |
| **Instance-Based** | K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) | Anomaly detection in small datasets | Supervised |
| **Probabilistic** | Naive Bayes, Bayesian Networks | Spam filtering, Bayesian email triage | Supervised |
| **Neural Networks** | MLP, CNN, RNN, LSTM, Autoencoders | Deep packet inspection, sequence attack detection | Supervised / Unsupervised |
| **Clustering** | K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical | Outlier detection in network traffic | Unsupervised |
| **Dimensionality Reduction** | PCA, t-SNE, Autoencoders | Feature compression for high-dimensional logs | Unsupervised |
| **Anomaly Detection** | Isolation Forest, One-Class SVM, LOF | Zero-day threat hunting | Unsupervised |
| **Deep Learning** | GANs, Transformers, BERT | Threat intelligence NLP, adversarial detection | Supervised / Self-supervised |
| **Reinforcement Learning** | Q-Learning, DQN, PPO | Autonomous red/blue team agents | Reinforcement |

## 2.3 The ML Model Lifecycle in Cyber Defense

The complete operational lifecycle of a deployed cyber ML model involves six phases:

1. **Data Collection**: Gather network flows, host logs, SIEM alerts, malware samples.
2. **Feature Engineering**: Extract statistical features (packet size mean, inter-arrival time std, payload entropy).
3. **Model Training**: Optimize parameters $\theta^*$ on labelled data.
4. **Validation**: Tune hyperparameters (tree depth, learning rate, regularization $\lambda$) on validation set.
5. **Testing**: Evaluate on held-out test set to estimate generalization.
6. **Deployment & Monitoring**: Deploy as a real-time inference engine; continuously monitor for **model drift** and **data drift**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **MANDATORY TABLE FORMATTING**: All mathematical notation uses `\vert` and `\mid` in place of the pipe character `|` to prevent markdown table corruption.

| **Symbol / Formula** | **Meaning** | **Domain / Range** | **Cyber Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Fraction of correct predictions | $[0, 1]$ | Overall IDS quality |
| $\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | How many flagged attacks are real | $[0, 1]$ | Cost of false alarms |
| $\text{Recall (TPR)} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | How many real attacks are caught | $[0, 1]$ | Catch-rate of intrusions |
| $\text{F1-Score} = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R | $[0, 1]$ | Balanced metric |
| $\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | False alarm rate | $[0, 1]$ | Alarm fatigue metric |
| $\text{FNR} = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | Miss rate | $[0, 1]$ | Critical for APT detection |
| $\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | True negative rate | $[0, 1]$ | Benign flow correctness |
| $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Mean squared error | $[0, \infty)$ | Regression loss (risk scoring) |
| $\text{CrossEntropy} = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)$ | Classification loss | $[0, \infty)$ | Multi-class attack classification |
| $\text{Entropy}(X) = -\sum_{i} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Shannon entropy in bits | $[0, \log_2 \vert \mathcal{X} \vert]$ | Encrypted payload detection |
| $\hat{y} = \sigma(w^T x + b) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(w^T x + b)}}$ | Logistic regression | $[0, 1]$ | Phishing probability |
| $Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a)]$ | Bellman update | — | RL defense agent |

Where:
- **TP** = True Positive, **TN** = True Negative
- **FP** = False Positive (false alarm), **FN** = False Negative (missed attack)
- $N$ = total number of samples
- $C$ = number of classes
- $\alpha$ = learning rate, $\gamma$ = discount factor
- $\sigma$ = sigmoid activation function

These concepts are deployed today in:
- **SIEM platforms** (Splunk, IBM QRadar) — ML-driven alert correlation
- **EDR solutions** (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne) — Behavioral ML on endpoint telemetry
- **Network IDS** (Zeek + ML, Suricata with ML plugins) — Real-time flow classification
- **Cloud Security** (AWS GuardDuty, Azure Sentinel) — Anomaly detection on cloud logs
- **Email Security** (Proofpoint, Mimecast) — NLP-based phishing detection

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Logistic Regression for Binary Intrusion Detection

We want to model the probability that a network flow $x \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is malicious, i.e., we want to estimate $P(y=1 \mid x)$.

**Step 1: Start with the linear model**
The naive linear combination $w^T x + b$ ranges over $\mathbb{R}$ (the entire real line), but a probability must lie in $[0, 1]$.

**Step 2: Apply the sigmoid squashing function**
To map $\mathbb{R} \to [0, 1]$, we wrap the linear combination with the logistic (sigmoid) function:

$$
\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}
$$

Therefore the model becomes:

$$
\hat{y} = P(y=1 \mid x; w, b) = \sigma(w^T x + b) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(w^T x + b)}}
$$

**Step 3: Express the joint likelihood of the training set**
For $N$ independent training samples $\{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$ where $y^{(i)} \in \{0, 1\}$:

$$
P(Y \mid X; w, b) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \left[\hat{y}^{(i)}\right]^{y^{(i)}} \left[1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}\right]^{1 - y^{(i)}}
$$

*Explanation*: If $y^{(i)} = 1$, the term $\hat{y}^{(i)}$ is selected; if $y^{(i)} = 0$, the term $(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})$ is selected. This is the Bernoulli likelihood.

**Step 4: Take the log-likelihood (for tractable optimization)**
Logarithms convert products into sums and are strictly monotonic, preserving the optimum:

$$
\ell(w, b) = \log P(Y \mid X; w, b) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y^{(i)} \log \hat{y}^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) \right]
$$

**Step 5: Negate to get the cost function (Cross-Entropy Loss)**
Minimizing a function is equivalent to maximizing its negative. We define the cost over a single example as:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\hat{y}, y) = - \left[ y \log \hat{y} + (1 - y) \log(1 - \hat{y}) \right]
$$

Averaged over $N$ samples:

$$
J(w, b) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}(\hat{y}^{(i)}, y^{(i)}) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y^{(i)} \log \hat{y}^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) \right]
$$

**Step 6: Optimize using Gradient Descent**
Update rule for parameters:

$$
w \leftarrow w - \alpha \frac{\partial J(w, b)}{\partial w}, \quad b \leftarrow b - \alpha \frac{\partial J(w, b)}{\partial b}
$$

The gradients are:

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial w} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) x^{(i)}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial b} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})
$$

*Engineering Insight*: The gradient $(\hat{y} - y) x$ is beautifully simple — the prediction error scales the input, making optimization computationally cheap for high-dimensional network flow data.

## 3.2 Derivation: K-Means Clustering for Network Anomaly Detection

K-Means partitions $N$ unlabelled network flows into $K$ clusters by minimizing the **within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS)**.

**Step 1: Define the objective function**
Let $\mu_k$ be the centroid of cluster $k$, and let $c^{(i)} \in \{1, \ldots, K\}$ be the cluster assignment of flow $i$. Then:

$$
J(c^{(1)}, \ldots, c^{(N)}, \mu_1, \ldots, \mu_K) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\| x^{(i)} - \mu_{c^{(i)}} \right\|^2
$$

**Step 2: Initialize**
Randomly select $K$ flows as initial centroids $\{\mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots, \mu_K\}$.

**Step 3: Cluster assignment step**
For each flow $x^{(i)}$, assign it to the nearest centroid using Euclidean distance:

$$
c^{(i)} = \arg\min_{k} \left\| x^{(i)} - \mu_k \right\|^2
$$

**Step 4: Centroid update step**
Recompute each centroid as the mean of all flows currently assigned to its cluster:

$$
\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{i : c^{(i)} = k} x^{(i)}
$$

**Step 5: Convergence check**
Repeat Steps 3 and 4 until the assignments $c^{(i)}$ no longer change or $J$ drops below a threshold $\epsilon$ (typically $10^{-4}$).

**Step 6: Anomaly score in cyber context**
Once clusters are stable, the anomaly score for a new flow $x_{\text{new}}$ is the distance to the nearest centroid:

$$
\text{AnomalyScore}(x_{\text{new}}) = \min_{k} \left\| x_{\text{new}} - \mu_k \right\|
$$

Flows with $\text{AnomalyScore} > \tau$ (a threshold derived from training statistics) are flagged as potential intrusions.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Selection for Cyber Tasks

| **Cyber Task** | **Best Algorithm** | **Reason** | **Limitation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Binary intrusion (Normal vs. Attack) | Logistic Regression / Random Forest | Fast, interpretable | Struggles with rare classes |
| Multi-class attack classification | XGBoost / Deep Neural Network | Handles high-dim, imbalanced | Requires lots of labels |
| Anomaly / zero-day detection | Isolation Forest / Autoencoder | No labels needed | High false positive rate |
| Phishing email detection | Naive Bayes / BERT | Strong text priors | Adversarial evasion |
| Network traffic forecasting | LSTM / GRU | Captures temporal dependency | Computationally heavy |
| Autonomous red-teaming | Deep Q-Network (DQN) / PPO | Sequential decision-making | Sample-inefficient |
| Real-time packet filtering | Lightweight CNN / Decision Tree | Sub-millisecond inference | Limited feature richness |

## 3.4 Confusion Matrix Construction (Worked Numerical Example)

Suppose a cyber IDS makes the following predictions on a test set of **1000** network flows with **50** real attacks:

| | **Predicted Attack** | **Predicted Benign** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Attack** | TP = 40 | FN = 10 |
| **Actual Benign** | FP = 30 | TN = 920 |

**Computing Accuracy**:

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{40 + 920}{40 + 10 + 30 + 920} = \frac{960}{1000} = 0.96 \text{ or } 96\%
$$

**Computing Precision**:

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{40}{40 + 30} = \frac{40}{70} \approx 0.5714 \text{ or } 57.14\%
$$

**Computing Recall (Detection Rate)**:

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{40}{40 + 10} = \frac{40}{50} = 0.80 \text{ or } 80\%
$$

**Computing F1-Score**:

$$
F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.5714 \times 0.80}{0.5714 + 0.80} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.4571}{1.3714} \approx 0.6667
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight**: Note how **96% Accuracy** is misleading — the IDS missed 10 attacks (20% miss rate) and generated 30 false alarms. In cyber security, **Recall is often more critical than Accuracy** because missed attacks are far costlier than false alarms.

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Define the term "model" in machine learning. Explain any two performance metrics used to evaluate a cyber intrusion detection model. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **model** in machine learning is a parameterized mathematical function $f_\theta : \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ learned from training data, used to make predictions on new inputs. In cyber security, $f_\theta$ maps a feature vector of a network flow to a label (attack or benign) or to a risk score.

**Metric 1 — Precision**: 
$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$
It measures the fraction of flows flagged as attacks that are actually attacks. High precision means fewer false alarms, reducing **alert fatigue** for SOC analysts.

**Metric 2 — Recall (Detection Rate)**:
$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$
It measures the fraction of actual attacks that are correctly detected. High recall is critical in cyber security because **missed attacks (FN) can be catastrophic** (e.g., a missed Advanced Persistent Threat).

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question Choice A

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the three main paradigms of machine learning — supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning. For each paradigm, provide **one** representative algorithm and **one** specific cyber security use-case. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Supervised Learning (2 Marks)**
- **Definition**: Learns a mapping from labelled training data $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ to predict labels for unseen inputs.
- **Algorithm**: Support Vector Machine (SVM) — finds the optimal hyperplane $w^T x + b = 0$ that maximally separates attack and benign classes.
- **Cyber Use-Case**: Binary classification of network flows in an IDS, e.g., classifying flows in the NSL-KDD dataset as `normal` or `DoS`.

**2. Unsupervised Learning (2 Marks)**
- **Definition**: Discovers hidden structure in unlabelled data $\{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$ without any ground-truth labels.
- **Algorithm**: Isolation Forest — isolates anomalies by randomly selecting features and split values; anomalies require fewer splits to isolate.
- **Cyber Use-Case**: Detecting zero-day attacks in network traffic by identifying flows that deviate significantly from learned normal clusters.

**3. Reinforcement Learning (2 Marks)**
- **Definition**: An agent learns a policy $\pi(a \mid s)$ by interacting with an environment, taking actions, and receiving rewards to maximize cumulative return.
- **Algorithm**: Deep Q-Network (DQN) — approximates the Q-function $Q(s, a)$ using a neural network and learns via the Bellman equation.
- **Cyber Use-Case**: Autonomous penetration testing where the agent learns optimal attack paths, or dynamic firewall rule generation in response to evolving threats.

**Conclusion (1 Mark)**
Each paradigm addresses a distinct cyber challenge: supervised learning excels at known-attack detection, unsupervised learning at zero-day discovery, and reinforcement learning at adaptive, sequential defense strategies.

---

**Q. (b)** A cyber Intrusion Detection System (IDS) is evaluated on a test set of 2000 network flows. The confusion matrix obtained is as follows:

| | **Predicted Attack** | **Predicted Benign** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Attack** | 180 | 20 |
| **Actual Benign** | 50 | 1750 |

Compute the following metrics and comment on the IDS performance from a security perspective: (i) Accuracy, (ii) Precision, (iii) Recall, (iv) F1-Score, (v) FPR. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the Confusion Matrix Entries**
- $TP = 180$ (correctly detected attacks)
- $FN = 20$ (missed attacks)
- $FP = 50$ (false alarms)
- $TN = 1750$ (correctly passed benign flows)
- Total $N = 2000$

**Step 2: Compute Accuracy (1 Mark)**
$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{N} = \frac{180 + 1750}{2000} = \frac{1930}{2000} = 0.965 \text{ or } 96.5\%
$$

**Step 3: Compute Precision (1 Mark)**
$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{180}{180 + 50} = \frac{180}{230} \approx 0.7826 \text{ or } 78.26\%
$$

**Step 4: Compute Recall / Detection Rate (1 Mark)**
$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90 \text{ or } 90\%
$$

**Step 5: Compute F1-Score (1 Mark)**
$$
F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \times \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7826 \times 0.90}{0.7826 + 0.90}
$$
$$
F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7043}{1.6826} \approx 2 \cdot 0.4186 \approx 0.8372
$$

**Step 6: Compute False Positive Rate (1 Mark)**
$$
\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{50}{50 + 1750} = \frac{50}{1800} \approx 0.0278 \text{ or } 2.78\%
$$

**Step 7: Security Commentary (2 Marks)**

- The IDS achieves a high **accuracy of 96.5%** and an excellent **recall of 90%**, meaning it catches 9 out of every 10 real attacks — a strong performance for an IDS.
- However, the **miss rate (FNR) is 10%** — the IDS missed 20 attacks. In a high-stakes environment, this could allow significant breaches.
- The **FPR of 2.78%** means 50 false alarms were raised. While manageable, this contributes to analyst alert fatigue.
- **F1-Score of 0.84** reflects a healthy balance between precision and recall.
- **Recommendation**: The model is deployable but should be complemented with a secondary anomaly detection layer to catch the 20 missed attacks, and threshold tuning should aim to reduce FPR below 1% for production SOC use.
