### Question Choice B

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat diagram, describe the complete machine learning pipeline used to build a cyber security defense model. Explain each stage in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Pipeline Stages (Diagram Description + Explanation):**

**Stage 1: Data Collection (1 Mark)**
Raw security telemetry is gathered from multiple sources:
- Network packet captures (via tcpdump, Wireshark)
- Host-based logs (syslog, Windows Event Logs)
- SIEM alerts (Splunk, QRadar)
- Malware samples (VirusTotal, malware repositories)
- Threat intelligence feeds (STIX/TAXII)

**Stage 2: Data Preprocessing (1 Mark)**
- Handling missing values (imputation or removal)
- Removing duplicates and noise
- Normalization: scaling features to a common range, e.g., Min-Max scaling:
$$
x_{\text{norm}} = \frac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}
$$
- Encoding categorical features (e.g., protocol type → one-hot encoding)

**Stage 3: Feature Engineering (1 Mark)**
- Statistical features: mean/std of packet sizes, inter-arrival times
- Protocol header fields: TCP flags, TTL, port numbers
- Payload-derived features: entropy, byte frequency distribution
- Temporal features: time-of-day, day-of-week patterns
- Dimensionality reduction if needed: PCA to project to top-$k$ principal components

**Stage 4: Train / Validation / Test Split (1 Mark)**
- Typically split 70% / 15% / 15% or 80% / 10% / 10%.
- Crucial to prevent data leakage: the test set must be temporally separated or strictly held out.

**Stage 5: Model Training (1 Mark)**
- Select algorithm (e.g., Random Forest, XGBoost, Neural Network)
- Train on training set, optimizing a loss function (e.g., cross-entropy)
- Use gradient descent or its variants (Adam, RMSprop) for differentiable models

**Stage 6: Hyperparameter Tuning (1 Mark)**
- Grid search, random search, or Bayesian optimization on the validation set
- Tune parameters like tree depth, learning rate $\alpha$, regularization $\lambda$

**Stage 7: Testing and Deployment (1 Mark)**
- Evaluate on the held-out test set using Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, AUC
- Deploy as a real-time inference engine in the SOC
- Enable **continuous monitoring** for model drift and data drift

**Pipeline Diagram (Mermaid Representation):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Collection] --> A2[Preprocessing] --> A3[Feature Engineering] --> A4[Split] --> A5[Training] --> A6[Tuning] --> A7[Testing] --> A8[Deployment]
    A8 --> A9[Monitoring]
    A9 -.Retrain.-> A5
```

**Q. (b)** Consider a network anomaly detection system trained using the K-Means clustering algorithm on $N = 1000$ normal network flows with $K = 4$ clusters. After training, the centroid of cluster 1 is $\mu_1 = (2, 5)$ and the centroid of cluster 2 is $\mu_2 = (8, 1)$. A new flow $x_{\text{new}} = (9, 2)$ arrives. Using Euclidean distance, determine which cluster the new flow belongs to and compute its anomaly score. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Recall K-Means Decision Rule (1 Mark)**
A new point is assigned to the cluster whose centroid is nearest in Euclidean distance. The anomaly score is the distance to the nearest centroid.

**Step 2: Compute Distance to Centroid 1 (1 Mark)**
$$
d(x_{\text{new}}, \mu_1) = \sqrt{(9 - 2)^2 + (2 - 5)^2} = \sqrt{7^2 + (-3)^2} = \sqrt{49 + 9} = \sqrt{58} \approx 7.6158
$$

**Step 3: Compute Distance to Centroid 2 (1 Mark)**
$$
d(x_{\text{new}}, \mu_2) = \sqrt{(9 - 8)^2 + (2 - 1)^2} = \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.4142
$$

**Step 4: Compare Distances (1 Mark)**
- $d(x_{\text{new}}, \mu_1) \approx 7.6158$
- $d(x_{\text{new}}, \mu_2) \approx 1.4142$

Since $1.4142 < 7.6158$, the new flow is **closer to centroid 2**.

**Step 5: Cluster Assignment (1 Mark)**
$$
c^{(\text{new})} = \arg\min_{k \in \{1, 2\}} d(x_{\text{new}}, \mu_k) = 2
$$

So the new flow $x_{\text{new}} = (9, 2)$ is **assigned to Cluster 2**.

**Step 6: Anomaly Score (1 Mark)**
$$
\text{AnomalyScore}(x_{\text{new}}) = \min_{k} d(x_{\text{new}}, \mu_k) = d(x_{\text{new}}, \mu_2) = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.4142
$$

**Step 7: Cyber Interpretation (1 Mark)**
Since the anomaly score $1.4142$ is small, the new flow is **close to a learned normal cluster** and is therefore classified as **likely benign**. If a threshold $\tau = 3$ were set (based on training-set statistics, e.g., 95th percentile of distances), this flow would pass the IDS without raising an alert. Conversely, any future flow with anomaly score $> 3$ would be flagged as a potential anomaly, warranting further SOC investigation.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Algorithm" with "Model"** — Examiners *will* deduct marks if you say "Random Forest is a model" without clarifying that Random Forest is the *algorithm*, and the trained ensemble of decision trees is the *model*. KTU specifically tests this distinction.
>
> 2. **Reporting only Accuracy** — In a cyber security question, quoting only accuracy is a guaranteed way to lose 2-3 marks. Always compute and discuss **Precision, Recall, F1, FPR** in context. Remember: a 99% accurate IDS that misses all zero-days is useless.
>
> 3. **Ignoring Class Imbalance** — Cyber datasets (NSL-KDD, CICIDS-2017) are heavily imbalanced. Students who don't mention techniques like **SMOTE, class weighting, or stratified sampling** will lose the application mark.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Cyber Context** — Generic ML answers (e.g., "SVM is used for image classification") will be marked down. Always tie your answer back to a **specific cyber use-case** (IDS, malware triage, phishing detection, etc.).
>
> 5. **Confusing Precision and Recall** — Memorize: **Precision** = "Of the things I flagged, how many were real attacks?" **Recall** = "Of all the real attacks, how many did I catch?" Mixing these up is the #1 most common error.
>
> 6. **Skipping Diagram in Pipeline Questions** — A 7-mark "explain the pipeline" question without a diagram forfeits at least 1 mark. Always include a labelled block diagram.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Concepts, Algorithms, and Models**

- **Concept** = the abstract learning task; **Algorithm** = the procedure to learn; **Model** = the final trained artifact.
- **Three ML paradigms**: Supervised (labelled), Unsupervised (unlabelled), Reinforcement (reward-based).
- **Algorithm families in cyber**: Linear (Logistic Reg), Tree (RF, XGBoost), Neural (MLP, CNN, LSTM), Probabilistic (Naive Bayes), Instance-based (KNN), Clustering (K-Means, DBSCAN), Anomaly (Isolation Forest), RL (DQN, PPO).
- **Key metrics**: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, FPR, FNR, Specificity, AUC.
- **Critical cyber insight**: Recall > Accuracy in intrusion detection because missed attacks (FN) are catastrophic.
- **Pipeline stages**: Collect → Preprocess → Feature Engineer → Split → Train → Tune → Test → Deploy → Monitor.
- **K-Means anomaly score** = distance to nearest centroid; threshold-based detection.
- **Logistic Regression** uses the sigmoid $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ to output probabilities in $[0, 1]$.
- **Cross-Entropy Loss** is the standard loss for binary and multi-class cyber classification.
- **Confusion Matrix** = the foundation of all cyber ML evaluation; always compute and interpret it.
- **Class imbalance** is the norm in cyber datasets — address with SMOTE, class weights, or stratified sampling.
- **Model drift** must be monitored post-deployment; attackers adapt, so models must be retrained periodically.
- **KTU favourite question patterns**: (1) Differentiate paradigms with examples, (2) Explain pipeline with diagram, (3) Compute metrics from confusion matrix, (4) Apply K-Means / Logistic Regression to a numerical scenario.
- **Mnemonic for metrics**: **"P**recision = **P**urity of alerts; **R**ecall = **R**ecovery of all attacks."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generative AI Techniques: Variational Autoencoders (VAEs), Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), Transformer-Based Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT633 - AI AND ML IN CYBER SECURITY DEFENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Generative AI Techniques: Variational Autoencoders (VAEs), Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), Transformer-Based Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Generative AI Techniques

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Generative AI — The Big Picture

**Generative AI** is a class of artificial intelligence models that learn the underlying probability distribution of training data to produce **new, synthetic samples** that are statistically similar to the original data. Unlike discriminative models (which learn a decision boundary $P(y \mid x)$), generative models learn the joint distribution $P(x, y)$ or simply $P(x)$, enabling them to *create* rather than just *classify*.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for cyber security defence, Generative AI is critical because:
- Attackers use it to craft **deepfakes**, polymorphic malware, and adversarial phishing.
- Defenders use it for **synthetic data augmentation**, anomaly synthesis, and threat-intelligence generation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim tone):**
> Generative AI techniques are neural architectures capable of learning latent representations of data distributions and synthesizing novel samples. The three flagship paradigms are **Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)**, **Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)**, and **Transformer-based models**.

---

### 1.2 Variational Autoencoder (VAE) — Formal Definition

A **Variational Autoencoder (VAE)** is a directed graphical generative model that couples a *probabilistic encoder* $q_{\phi}(z \mid x)$ with a *probabilistic decoder* $p_{\theta}(x \mid z)$ via a regularized latent space. It is trained by maximising a variational lower bound (ELBO) on the data log-likelihood.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Variational"?** The encoder outputs the *parameters* of a probability distribution (a Gaussian), not a single point. We then *sample* from this distribution, and variational inference provides the tractable objective to optimise it.

#### Conceptual Analogy — The "Sketch Artist and Forensic Artist"

Imagine a **forensic sketch artist** (the **Encoder**) who interviews a witness and produces not one, but a *range* of possible facial sketches (a probability cloud) — the average face ($\mu$) with some uncertainty ($\sigma$). A **composite artist** (the **Decoder**) then takes this soft, fuzzy description and paints a full realistic portrait.

- The **latent space** is the *mental space* of the artist.
- The **KL-divergence term** acts as a discipline — the artist is penalised for producing sketches that are too far from the "standard human face distribution" the studio enforces.
- The **reconstruction term** is the *feedback from the witness* — "you got the eyes right, but the nose is off."

This combination forces the model to learn a **smooth, continuous, structured** latent space — meaning you can *interpolate* between two faces to produce a believable morph.

---

### 1.3 Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) — Formal Definition

A **Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)** is an implicit generative model composed of two neural networks — a **Generator** $G$ and a **Discriminator** $D$ — trained simultaneously in a **two-player minimax game**. $G$ maps noise $z \sim p_z$ to fake samples, while $D$ estimates the probability that a sample came from the real data rather than $G$. The objective is:

$$ \min_{G} \max_{D} V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_{z}}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))] $$

> [!NOTE]
> **GAN = Adversarial Training.** The Generator is a "counterfeiter" trying to fool the "police" (Discriminator). Both improve in lock-step until the counterfeits are indistinguishable from the real currency.

#### Conceptual Analogy — The Counterfeiter and the Police

- **Generator (Counterfeiter)**: Starts with random noise and prints fake ₹500 notes. Initially crude, but learns from feedback.
- **Discriminator (Police Detective)**: Receives a mix of real and fake notes and must classify each.
- The **adversarial signal** flows backward: every time the detective catches a fake, the counterfeiter adjusts; every time a fake fools the detective, the detective re-trains.

At **Nash Equilibrium**, the counterfeit's distribution $p_g$ equals $p_{data}$ and the detective can do no better than a 50/50 coin flip — i.e., $D(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}$ everywhere.

---

### 1.4 Transformer-Based Models — Formal Definition

A **Transformer** is a sequence-to-sequence architecture built entirely on **self-attention** mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions. It was introduced in the seminal paper *"Attention Is All You Need"* (Vaswani et al., 2017). The core operation is **scaled dot-product self-attention**:

$$ \text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V $$

For *generative* use, Transformer decoders (e.g., **GPT** family) are auto-regressively unrolled to sample one token at a time, while **encoder-decoder** Transformers (e.g., T5, BART) and **encoder-only** Transformers (e.g., BERT variants) are used for representation learning and conditional generation.

#### Conceptual Analogy — The "All-Hearing Committee Meeting"

Imagine a **boardroom committee** where every member (token) must, at every decision step, look at *every other member's* notes (via attention weights) before speaking. Unlike a *queue* (RNN, where information must trickle down), the entire committee communicates in parallel and full context.

- **Query (Q)**: "What am I looking for?"
- **Key (K)**: "What do I contain?"
- **Value (V)**: "What do I actually say?"
- **Multi-Head Attention**: Multiple parallel sub-committees, each focusing on a *different aspect* of the conversation (syntax, semantics, entity-tracking).

> [!TIP]
> In cyber security, Transformer-based LLMs power **threat-intelligence summarisation**, **code analysis for malware detection**, and **adversarial prompt filtering**.

---

### 1.5 Comparative Snapshot

| Property | VAE | GAN | Transformer (Generative) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Output type** | Blurry, probabilistic reconstructions | Sharp, realistic samples | Coherent text/code/audio tokens |
| **Training** | Stable, ELBO-based | Unstable, adversarial | Stable, cross-entropy + attention |
| **Latent space** | Continuous, regularised | Implicit / entangled | Discrete token space + continuous embeddings |
| **Cyber-security use** | Anomaly synthesis, log generation | Deepfake detection augmentation, adversarial example crafting | Phishing-text generation, malware code analysis, SOC automation |
| **Key metric** | **ELBO**, **FID** | **Inception Score (IS)**, **FID** | **Perplexity**, **BLEU**, **ROUGE** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three are **complementary**, not competitive. Modern pipelines (e.g., **TransGAN**, **VAE-GAN hybrids**, **Latent Diffusion + Transformer**) combine them.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Variational Autoencoder (VAE) — Operational Deep-Dive

### 2.1.1 Architecture

A VAE has three modular components:

1. **Encoder (Inference Network) $q_{\phi}(z \mid x)$**: Maps the input $x$ to a posterior Gaussian over the latent $z$ by predicting two vectors $\mu(x)$ and $\log \sigma^2(x)$.
2. **Reparameterisation Sampler**: Draws $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$.
3. **Decoder (Generative Network) $p_{\theta}(x \mid z)$**: Reconstructs $\hat{x}$ from the latent $z$.

### 2.1.2 The ELBO — Variational Lower Bound

We want to maximise the marginal log-likelihood $\log p_{\theta}(x)$, but it is **intractable** because the integral over $z$ has no closed form. Variational inference gives us the **Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)**:

$$ \log p_{\theta}(x) \geq \mathcal{L}_{\theta,\phi}(x) = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\!\left[\log p_{\theta}(x \mid z)\right] - D_{\mathrm{KL}}\!\left(q_{\phi}(z \mid x) \,\|\, p(z)\right) $$

> [!NOTE]
> Decomposition: **ELBO = Reconstruction Term − KL Regulariser.**
> - The first term is the expected log-likelihood of reconstruction.
> - The second term pulls the approximate posterior toward the prior $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$.

For a Gaussian encoder and unit-variance Gaussian prior, the KL term has a **closed form**:

$$ D_{\mathrm{KL}}\!\left(\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2) \,\|\, \mathcal{N}(0, 1)\right) = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{J}\!\left(\sigma_j^2 + \mu_j^2 - 1 - \log \sigma_j^2\right) $$

### 2.1.3 The Reparameterisation Trick

Back-propagation cannot pass gradients through a stochastic node $z \sim q_{\phi}(z \mid x)$. The **reparameterisation trick** expresses the sample as a **deterministic, differentiable function** of $\phi$ and an external noise $\varepsilon$:

$$ z = \mu_{\phi}(x) + \sigma_{\phi}(x) \odot \varepsilon, \quad \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I) $$

This lets gradients flow through $\mu$ and $\sigma$ as if they were ordinary activations.

### 2.1.4 Why VAEs Produce Blurry Images

The Gaussian assumption plus the pixel-wise reconstruction loss (e.g., MSE) penalises *any* deviation from the mean output, which causes the decoder to "hedge" — producing the **average of plausible reconstructions** rather than a sharp one. This is a known KTU-style discussion point.

---

## 2.2 Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) — Operational Deep-Dive

### 2.2.1 The Two-Player Game

- **Discriminator $D(x)$**: outputs a scalar probability that $x$ is real.
- **Generator $G(z)$**: produces a fake $G(z)$ from a noise prior $p_z(z)$.

The value function (Goodfellow et al., 2014):

$$ \min_{G}\max_{D} V(D, G) = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)]}_{\text{Real classified as real}} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_{z}}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]}_{\text{Fake classified as fake}} $$

### 2.2.2 Optimal Discriminator

For a fixed $G$, the optimal $D^{\star}$ is:

$$ D^{\star}(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_{g}(x)} $$

Substituting $D^{\star}$ back yields the minimum value:

$$ V(D^{\star}, G) = -\log 4 + 2 \cdot D_{\mathrm{JS}}\!\left(p_{\text{data}} \,\|\, p_{g}\right) $$

where $D_{\mathrm{JS}}$ is the **Jensen-Shannon Divergence**. Minimising $G$ therefore minimises $D_{\mathrm{JS}}(p_{\text{data}} \,\|\, p_{g})$.

### 2.2.3 Non-Saturating GAN Loss

In practice, the original $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ saturates early. Practitioners flip $G$'s objective to:

$$ \mathcal{L}_{G} = -\mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_{z}}[\log D(G(z))] $$

This yields **strong gradients** when $D$ easily rejects the fakes.

### 2.2.4 Conditional GAN (cGAN)

By feeding a class label $y$ to both $G$ and $D$:

$$ \min_{G}\max_{D} V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x,y}[\log D(x \mid y)] + \mathbb{E}_{z,y}[\log(1 - D(G(z \mid y)))] $$

This is critical for cyber security (e.g., generating samples of a *specific* malware family).

---

## 2.3 Transformer-Based Models — Operational Deep-Dive

### 2.3.1 Self-Attention Mechanism

Given an input sequence of embeddings $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{\text{model}}}$, we project to three matrices:

$$ Q = X W^{Q}, \quad K = X W^{K}, \quad V = X W^{V} $$

where $W^{Q}, W^{K}, W^{V} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k}$. The **scaled dot-product attention** is:

$$ \text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The scaling factor $\sqrt{d_k}$ counteracts the growth of dot-product magnitudes with $d_k$, which would push softmax into saturated, low-gradient regions.

### 2.3.2 Multi-Head Attention

$$ \text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h) W^{O} $$

with

$$ \text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(Q W^{Q}_i, K W^{K}_i, V W^{V}_i) $$

This lets the model attend to information from **different representation subspaces** in parallel.

### 2.3.3 Positional Encoding

Because self-attention is **permutation-equivariant**, we must inject order information. Sinusoidal encoding:

$$ \text{PE}(pos, 2i) = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right) $$

$$ \text{PE}(pos, 2i+1) = \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right) $$

### 2.3.4 Auto-Regressive Generation

A generative Transformer (e.g., GPT) samples tokens one at a time:

$$ P(x_1, \ldots, x_T) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(x_t \mid x_{<t}) $$

with **causal masking** ensuring position $t$ only attends to positions $\leq t$.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **VAE ELBO** | $\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z\mid x)}[\log p_{\theta}(x\mid z)] - D_{\mathrm{KL}}(q_{\phi}(z\mid x) \vert\vert p(z))$ | Maximise this. |
| **Reparameterisation** | $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \varepsilon,\ \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ | Enables gradient flow. |
| **KL Gaussian (q‖p)** | $\tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{j}(\sigma_j^2 + \mu_j^2 - 1 - \log \sigma_j^2)$ | $p = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$. |
| **GAN Value Function** | $\min_{G}\max_{D} \mathbb{E}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$ | Original Goodfellow loss. |
| **Optimal Discriminator** | $D^{\star}(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}$ | Closed-form optimum. |
| **GAN Minimum Value** | $V(D^{\star}, G) = -\log 4 + 2 D_{\mathrm{JS}}(p_{\text{data}} \vert\vert p_g)$ | Training goal $\Rightarrow$ minimise $D_{\mathrm{JS}}$. |
| **Scaled Dot-Product Attn** | $\text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$ | $\sqrt{d_k}$ = **scaling factor**. |
| **Multi-Head Output** | $\text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h) W^{O}$ | $h$ = number of heads. |
| **Positional Encoding (sin)** | $\text{PE}(pos, 2i) = \sin(pos/10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}})$ | Even dimensions. |
| **Positional Encoding (cos)** | $\text{PE}(pos, 2i+1) = \cos(pos/10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}})$ | Odd dimensions. |
| **Auto-Regressive Factorisation** | $P(x_{1:T}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(x_t \mid x_{<t})$ | GPT-style generation. |
| **FID Metric** | $\mathrm{FID} = \vert\vert\mu_r - \mu_g \vert\vert^2 + \mathrm{Tr}\!\left(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r \Sigma_g)^{1/2}\right)$ | Lower = better. |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exams, memorise the **VAE ELBO**, the **GAN minimax equation**, and the **scaled dot-product attention** — these three are the highest-yield formulas for the entire module.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: VAE ELBO from First Principles

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Marginal Log-Likelihood

For any data point $x$, the log-marginal-likelihood is:

$$ \log p_{\theta}(x) = \log \int p_{\theta}(x, z)\, dz = \log \int p_{\theta}(x \mid z)\, p(z)\, dz $$

The integral is over the entire latent space and is **intractable** in deep models.

### 3.1.2 Introduce the Variational Distribution

Multiply and divide by $q_{\phi}(z \mid x)$ — any valid distribution over $z$ — and apply $\log$:

$$ \log p_{\theta}(x) = \log \int q_{\phi}(z \mid x)\,\frac{p_{\theta}(x \mid z)\, p(z)}{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\, dz $$

This is the **log of an expectation** under $q_{\phi}(z \mid x)$:

$$ \log p_{\theta}(x) = \log \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\!\left[\frac{p_{\theta}(x \mid z)\, p(z)}{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\right] $$

### 3.1.3 Apply Jensen's Inequality

Jensen's inequality states $\log \mathbb{E}[Y] \geq \mathbb{E}[\log Y]$ because $\log$ is concave. Applying it:

$$ \log \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\!\left[\frac{p_{\theta}(x \mid z)\, p(z)}{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\right] \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\!\left[\log \frac{p_{\theta}(x \mid z)\, p(z)}{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\right] $$

### 3.1.4 Split the Log-Expectation

The right-hand side splits into two terms using $\log(ab) = \log a + \log b$:

$$ \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}[\log p_{\theta}(x \mid z)] + \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\!\left[\log \frac{p(z)}{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\right] $$

The second term is **$-\,D_{\mathrm{KL}}$** by definition:

$$ \log p_{\theta}(x) \geq \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}[\log p_{\theta}(x \mid z)]}_{\text{Reconstruction}} - \underbrace{D_{\mathrm{KL}}\!\left(q_{\phi}(z \mid x) \,\|\, p(z)\right)}_{\text{Regulariser}} $$

This is the **ELBO**.

### 3.1.5 Closed-Form KL for Diagonal Gaussians

Assume $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ and $q_{\phi}(z \mid x) = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \text{diag}(\sigma^2))$. The KL divergence is:

$$ D_{\mathrm{KL}}(q \,\|\, p) = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{J}\!\left(\sigma_j^2 + \mu_j^2 - 1 - \log \sigma_j^2\right) $$

**Derivation outline**:

- General KL between two Gaussians $\mathcal{N}(\mu_1, \Sigma_1)$ and $\mathcal{N}(\mu_2, \Sigma_2)$ in $\mathbb{R}^{J}$:

$$ D_{\mathrm{KL}}(\mathcal{N}_1 \,\|\, \mathcal{N}_2) = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[\mathrm{Tr}(\Sigma_2^{-1}\Sigma_1) + (\mu_2 - \mu_1)^{\top}\Sigma_2^{-1}(\mu_2 - \mu_1) - J + \log\frac{\det\Sigma_2}{\det\Sigma_1}\right] $$

- Substitute $\mu_1 = \mu,\ \Sigma_1 = \text{diag}(\sigma^2),\ \mu_2 = 0,\ \Sigma_2 = I$:

$$ D_{\mathrm{KL}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[\mathrm{Tr}(\text{diag}(\sigma^2)) + \mu^{\top}\mu - J + \log 1 - \log\det(\text{diag}(\sigma^2))\right] $$

- Compute term-by-term:
  - $\mathrm{Tr}(\text{diag}(\sigma^2)) = \sum_j \sigma_j^2$
  - $\mu^{\top}\mu = \sum_j \mu_j^2$
  - $\log\det(\text{diag}(\sigma^2)) = \sum_j \log\sigma_j^2$

- Assemble:

$$ D_{\mathrm{KL}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{J}\!\left(\sigma_j^2 + \mu_j^2 - 1 - \log\sigma_j^2\right) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU**: a numerical question may ask you to compute the KL term for a small latent vector, e.g., $\mu = (0.5, -0.3)$ and $\sigma^2 = (0.8, 1.2)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Optimal Discriminator of a GAN

We fix $G$ and maximise $V(D, G)$ over $D$. Rewrite the value function as an integral:

$$ V(D, G) = \int_{x} p_{\text{data}}(x)\log D(x)\, dx + \int_{z} p_{z}(z)\log(1 - D(G(z)))\, dz $$

Change-of-variable for the second term: let $x = G(z)$, so $dz$ becomes implicit:

$$ V(D, G) = \int_{x}\!\left[p_{\text{data}}(x)\log D(x) + p_g(x)\log(1 - D(x))\right] dx $$

To find the optimal $D$ for a fixed $x$, differentiate the integrand with respect to $D(x)$:

$$ \frac{\partial}{\partial D(x)}\!\left[p_{\text{data}}(x)\log D(x) + p_g(x)\log(1 - D(x))\right] = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{D(x)} - \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - D(x)} $$

Set the derivative to zero:

$$ \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{D(x)} = \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - D(x)} $$

Cross-multiply and solve for $D(x)$:

$$ p_{\text{data}}(x)\,(1 - D(x)) = p_g(x)\,D(x) $$

$$ p_{\text{data}}(x) = D(x)\,(p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)) $$

$$ D^{\star}(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)} $$

### 3.2.1 Substituting $D^{\star}$ Back

Plug $D^{\star}$ into $V$:

$$ V(D^{\star}, G) = \int_{x} p_{\text{data}}(x)\log\!\frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)} + p_g(x)\log\!\frac{p_g(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}\, dx $$

Add and subtract $\log 2$ from each term, and recognise the KL definition:

$$ V(D^{\star}, G) = -\log 4 + \mathrm{KL}\!\left(p_{\text{data}} \,\Big\|\, \tfrac{p_{\text{data}} + p_g}{2}\right) + \mathrm{KL}\!\left(p_g \,\Big\|\, \tfrac{p_{\text{data}} + p_g}{2}\right) $$

The sum of those two KLs is exactly $2 D_{\mathrm{JS}}(p_{\text{data}} \vert\vert p_g)$:

$$ V(D^{\star}, G) = -\log 4 + 2 D_{\mathrm{JS}}\!\left(p_{\text{data}} \,\|\, p_g\right) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key result**: training $G$ to minimise this is equivalent to minimising the **Jensen-Shannon divergence** between the real and generated distributions. The global optimum is at $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$, giving $V^{\star} = -\log 4$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Scaled Dot-Product Self-Attention

### 3.3.1 Setup

Given query and key vectors $q, k \in \mathbb{R}^{d_k}$, the dot-product similarity is $q^{\top} k = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} q_i k_i$.

### 3.3.2 Variance Argument

Assume $q$ and $k$ are components of vectors with **zero mean** and **unit variance** (a common assumption after layer norm). The variance of $q^{\top} k$ is:

$$ \mathrm{Var}(q^{\top} k) = d_k \cdot \mathrm{Var}(q_i k_i) = d_k \cdot 1 = d_k $$

So the dot-product's standard deviation grows as $\sqrt{d_k}$. For large $d_k$, the softmax inputs are pushed into the saturated tails (where gradient $\to 0$).

### 3.3.3 The Scaling

To restore unit variance, divide the dot-product by $\sqrt{d_k}$:

$$ \text{scores} = \frac{q^{\top} k}{\sqrt{d_k}} $$

with new variance $\mathrm{Var}(\text{scores}) = 1$.

### 3.3.4 Softmax and Weighted Sum

The attention weight is $\alpha = \text{softmax}(\text{scores})$, and the output for one query is the weighted sum of all values:

$$ \text{output} = \sum_{j} \alpha_j v_j $$

In matrix form for the full sequence:

$$ \text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V $$

### 3.3.5 Why $\sqrt{d_k}$ Specifically (not $d_k$ or 1)

- **$= 1$**: dot products explode; softmax saturates; no learning.
- **$= d_k$**: too aggressive; softmax outputs become uniform; loses selectivity.
- **$= \sqrt{d_k}$**: empirically and theoretically the right variance-correcting constant.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU, a common 7-mark derivation is "Derive the scaled dot-product attention and justify the $\sqrt{d_k}$ factor." The above argument is the canonical answer.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — KL Divergence for VAE

**Problem (KTU-style):** Let $q(z \mid x) = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2 I)$ with $\mu = (0.4, -0.2)$ and $\sigma^2 = (0.6, 1.5)$. Compute $D_{\mathrm{KL}}(q \vert\vert p)$ where $p = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ and $J = 2$.

**Step 1 — Write the closed-form:**

$$ D_{\mathrm{KL}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{2}(\sigma_j^2 + \mu_j^2 - 1 - \log \sigma_j^2) $$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$ D_{\mathrm{KL}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[(0.6 + 0.16 - 1 - \log 0.6) + (1.5 + 0.04 - 1 - \log 1.5)\right] $$

**Step 3 — Evaluate log terms (base $e$):**

$$ \log 0.6 \approx -0.5108, \quad \log 1.5 \approx 0.4055 $$

**Step 4 — Compute each bracket:**

- Bracket 1: $0.6 + 0.16 - 1 - (-0.5108) = 0.2708$
- Bracket 2: $1.5 + 0.04 - 1 - 0.4055 = 0.1345$

**Step 5 — Sum and halve:**

$$ D_{\mathrm{KL}} = \tfrac{1}{2}(0.2708 + 0.1345) = \tfrac{1}{2}(0.4053) \approx 0.2026 \text{ nats} $$

> [!NOTE]
> In bits, divide by $\ln 2 \approx 0.6931$: $0.2026 / 0.6931 \approx 0.2923$ bits. This is small, indicating the variational posterior is close to the prior — typical of a well-trained VAE.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 VAE Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input x] --> B[Encoder q_phi]
    B --> C[Latent Params mu]
    B --> D[Latent Params log sigma2]
    E[Noise epsilon N0I] --> F[Reparameterisation Sampler]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    F --> G[Latent Sample z]
    G --> H[Decoder p_theta]
    H --> I[Reconstruction x_hat]
    I --> J[Reconstruction Loss MSE or BCE]
    C --> K[KL Divergence Loss]
    D --> K
    J --> L[Total VAE Loss]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Backprop Update phi and theta]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The encoder's *two heads* produce $\mu$ and $\log \sigma^2$ (predicting log-variance is more numerically stable).
- The noise injection $\varepsilon$ is what makes the latent sampling *differentiable* via the reparameterisation trick.
- Two losses are summed; both gradients update $\phi$ (encoder) and $\theta$ (decoder).

---

## 4.2 GAN Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Noise z from p_z] --> B[Generator G]
    B --> C[Fake Sample G_z]
    D[Real Sample x from p_data] --> E[Discriminator D]
    C --> E
    E --> F[D Output Real or Fake Probability]
    F --> G{Is sample real or fake?}
    G -->|Real branch| H[Loss Real: -log D_x]
    G -->|Fake branch| I[Loss Fake: -log 1 - D_Gz]
    H --> J[Discriminator Update]
    I --> J
    F --> K[Generator Adversarial Loss: -log D_Gz]
    K --> L[Generator Update]
    J --> M[Trained D]
    L --> N[Trained G]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Discriminator and Generator are updated **alternately**, not jointly.
- The "non-saturating" trick (using $-\log D(G(z))$ for $G$) avoids vanishing gradients early in training.
- At equilibrium, $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$ and $D(x) = 0.5$ for every $x$.

---

## 4.3 Transformer Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input Token IDs] --> B[Token Embedding]
    A1[Position Index] --> C[Positional Encoding]
    B --> D[Add Token and Positional]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Multi-Head Self-Attention]
    E --> F[Add and Norm Layer 1]
    F --> G[Feed Forward FFN]
    G --> H[Add and Norm Layer 2]
    H --> I{Encoder or Decoder block?}
    I -->|Decoder| J[Masked Multi-Head Attention]
    J --> K[Cross Attention with Encoder Output]
    K --> L[Add and Norm Layer 3]
    L --> M[Feed Forward FFN Dec]
    M --> N[Add and Norm Layer 4]
    N --> O[Linear Output Projection]
    O --> P[Softmax over Vocabulary]
    P --> Q[Predicted Token Probabilities]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Encoder block** uses *unmasked* self-attention (sees the full sequence).
- **Decoder block** uses *masked* self-attention (auto-regressive) plus *cross-attention* to the encoder's output.
- For purely generative Transformer (GPT-style), only the **decoder stack** is used, with causal masking.

---

## 4.4 Comparison Flow — How the Three Models Generate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[VAE Encode x] --> B[Smooth Latent z]
    B --> C[Decode to x_hat]
    D[GAN Sample z] --> E[Generator G_z]
    E --> F[Fake x_hat]
    G[Transformer Input Prefix] --> H[Self Attention over prefix]
    H --> I[Sample Next Token]
    I --> J[Append to prefix]
    J --> H
    C --> K[Generative Output Space]
    F --> K
    J --> K
```

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU 7-mark question is: *"Draw a block diagram comparing the generative pathways of VAE, GAN, and Transformer."* The above four diagrams collectively cover this need.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define a Variational Autoencoder (VAE). Explain the role of the reparameterisation trick in training a VAE.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Variational Autoencoder (VAE)** is a generative neural network composed of a probabilistic encoder $q_{\phi}(z \mid x)$ and a probabilistic decoder $p_{\theta}(x \mid z)$, trained by maximising the **Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)**:

$$ \mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}[\log p_{\theta}(x \mid z)] - D_{\mathrm{KL}}(q_{\phi}(z \mid x) \vert\vert p(z)) $$

The **reparameterisation trick** expresses the latent sample as $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$, making the sampling operation **differentiable** and allowing gradients to flow through the stochastic node during back-propagation. Without it, training would be infeasible. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is self-attention in a Transformer? Write the scaled dot-product attention formula.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Self-attention** is a mechanism that allows every position in a sequence to attend to every other position, computing a weighted sum of value vectors $V$ where the weights are derived from query-key compatibility. The **scaled dot-product attention** is given by:

$$ \text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V $$

The $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling prevents the softmax from saturating for large embedding dimensions. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`
**A) (7 marks)** Derive the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) used in training a Variational Autoencoder. Show all intermediate steps starting from the marginal log-likelihood.

**B) (7 marks)** A VAE has a 2-D latent space with encoder output $\mu = (0.5, -0.3)$ and $\sigma^2 = (0.8, 1.2)$ for a single training sample. Compute the KL divergence $D_{\mathrm{KL}}(q \vert\vert p)$ where the prior is standard normal. Express your answer in nats and in bits.

#### Model Solution for A:

**Step 1 — Marginal log-likelihood.** For input $x$:

$$ \log p_{\theta}(x) = \log \int p_{\theta}(x \mid z)\, p(z)\, dz \quad (1\text{ mark}) $$

**Step 2 — Multiply and divide by $q_{\phi}(z \mid x)$:**

$$ = \log \int q_{\phi}(z \mid x)\,\frac{p_{\theta}(x \mid z)\, p(z)}{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\, dz \quad (1\text{ mark}) $$

**Step 3 — Recognise as expectation:**

$$ = \log \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\!\left[\frac{p_{\theta}(x \mid z)\, p(z)}{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\right] \quad (1\text{ mark}) $$

**Step 4 — Apply Jensen's inequality** ($\log$ is concave, so $\log \mathbb{E}[\cdot] \geq \mathbb{E}[\log \cdot]$):

$$ \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\!\left[\log \frac{p_{\theta}(x \mid z)\, p(z)}{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\right] \quad (2\text{ marks}) $$

**Step 5 — Split into two terms and recognise KL:**

$$ = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}[\log p_{\theta}(x \mid z)]}_{\text{Reconstruction}} - \underbrace{D_{\mathrm{KL}}\!\left(q_{\phi}(z \mid x) \,\|\, p(z)\right)}_{\text{Regulariser}} = \mathcal{L}_{\theta,\phi}(x) \quad (2\text{ marks}) $$

#### Model Solution for B:

**Step 1 — Use closed-form KL for diagonal Gaussian:**

$$ D_{\mathrm{KL}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{2}(\sigma_j^2 + \mu_j^2 - 1 - \log \sigma_j^2) \quad (2\text{ marks}) $$

**Step 2 — Substitute and compute each term:**

- For $j=1$: $0.8 + 0.25 - 1 - \log 0.8 = 0.8 + 0.25 - 1 - (-0.2231) = 0.2731$
- For $j=2$: $1.2 + 0.09 - 1 - \log 1.2 = 1.2 + 0.09 - 1 - 0.1823 = 0.1077$
**(3 marks)**

**Step 3 — Sum and halve:**

$$ D_{\mathrm{KL}} = \tfrac{1}{2}(0.2731 + 0.1077) = \tfrac{1}{2}(0.3808) \approx 0.1904 \text{ nats} \quad (1\text{ mark}) $$

**Step 4 — Convert to bits** (divide by $\ln 2 \approx 0.6931$):

$$ D_{\mathrm{KL}} = \frac{0.1904}{0.6931} \approx 0.2747 \text{ bits} \quad (1\text{ mark}) $$

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`
**A) (7 marks)** State and explain the GAN minimax objective function. Derive the expression for the optimal discriminator $D^{\star}(x)$ and show that substituting it back yields $V(D^{\star}, G) = -\log 4 + 2 D_{\mathrm{JS}}(p_{\text{data}} \vert\vert p_g)$.

**B) (7 marks)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Transformer encoder block. Why is positional encoding necessary, and what is the mathematical form of sinusoidal positional encoding?

#### Model Solution for A:

**Step 1 — State the value function:**

$$ \min_{G}\max_{D} V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_{z}}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))] \quad (1\text{ mark}) $$

**Step 2 — Explanation:** $D$ is trained to assign high probability to real samples and low to fakes; $G$ is trained to produce fakes that maximise $D$'s confusion. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Rewrite as an integral over $x$:**

$$ V(D, G) = \int_{x}\!\left[p_{\text{data}}(x)\log D(x) + p_g(x)\log(1 - D(x))\right] dx \quad (1\text{ mark}) $$

**Step 4 — Differentiate the integrand w.r.t. $D(x)$ and set to zero:**

$$ \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{D(x)} - \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - D(x)} = 0 \implies D^{\star}(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)} \quad (2\text{ marks}) $$

**Step 5 — Substitute back and simplify using KL identities:**

$$ V(D^{\star}, G) = \mathrm{KL}\!\left(p_{\text{data}} \,\Big\|\, \tfrac{p_{\text{data}} + p_g}{2}\right) + \mathrm{KL}\!\left(p_g \,\Big\|\, \tfrac{p_{\text{data}} + p_g}{2}\right) - \log 4 $$

$$ = 2 D_{\mathrm{JS}}\!\left(p_{\text{data}} \,\|\, p_g\right) - \log 4 \quad (2\text{ marks}) $$

#### Model Solution for B:

**Step 1 — Diagram of Transformer encoder block** (refer to Section 4.3 of these notes; expected components: input embedding, positional encoding addition, multi-head self-attention, residual connection + layer norm, feed-forward network, residual + layer norm). **(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Why positional encoding:** Self-attention is **permutation-equivariant** — it treats the input as a *bag* of tokens with no inherent order. Positional encoding injects sequence-order information. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Sinusoidal form:**

$$ \text{PE}(pos, 2i) = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right), \quad \text{PE}(pos, 2i+1) = \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right) \quad (2\text{ marks}) $$

**Step 4 — Note on generalisation:** Sinusoidal encodings allow the model to generalise to sequence lengths beyond those seen in training because relative positions can be expressed as linear combinations. **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **VAE derivation**: A common mark-loser is *forgetting to apply Jensen's inequality* — students often jump straight to the ELBO formula. Always show: log → integral → multiply by $q$ → Jensen → split → recognise KL. **(−2 marks if skipped)**
> 2. **GAN**: Many students confuse the **minimax** with a "min-min" or "max-max" equation. Memorise: $D$ maximises, $G$ minimises. Also, $D^{\star}$ is the *closed form for fixed $G$*, not the discriminator architecture.
> 3. **Transformer**: The most-omitted step is *justifying* the $\sqrt{d_k}$ factor. Stating "to reduce magnitude" without the variance argument costs a mark.
> 4. **KL numerical problems**: Don't forget the **$-1$** term. It is the most-skipped constant and an instant half-mark loss.
> 5. **Positional encoding**: A frequent confusion is whether $\text{PE}$ is added or concatenated — it is **added** to the token embedding.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **VAE ELBO**: $\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}_{q}[\log p] - D_{\mathrm{KL}}(q \vert\vert p)$ — maximised during training. **(1 mark direct)**
- **Reparameterisation**: $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ — the trick that makes VAE training possible. **(1 mark direct)**
- **KL closed form for diagonal Gaussian**: $\tfrac{1}{2}\sum(\sigma^2 + \mu^2 - 1 - \log\sigma^2)$. **(1 mark direct)**
- **GAN objective**: $\min_G \max_D \mathbb{E}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$. **(1 mark direct)**
- **Optimal $D^{\star}(x)$**: $\frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}$. **(1 mark direct)**
- **GAN minimum value**: $-\log 4 + 2 D_{\mathrm{JS}}(p_{\text{data}} \vert\vert p_g)$. **(1 mark direct)**
- **Scaled dot-product attention**: $\text{softmax}(QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k})V$. **(1 mark direct)**
- **Why $\sqrt{d_k}$**: To keep the variance of the pre-softmax scores at $\approx 1$ and prevent saturation. **(1 mark direct)**
- **Multi-head attention**: $h$ parallel attention heads whose outputs are concatenated and projected. **(1 mark direct)**
- **Positional encoding** is **added** (not concatenated) to token embeddings. **(1 mark direct)**
- **Sinusoidal PE formulas**: $\sin$ for even indices, $\cos$ for odd indices of the embedding dimension. **(1 mark direct)**
- **Cyber-security link**: VAEs for **log synthesis & anomaly generation**; GANs for **deepfake / adversarial-example crafting & detection augmentation**; Transformers for **SOC automation, phishing detection, and code-based malware analysis**. **(1 mark direct)**
- **VAE blurriness**: Caused by Gaussian assumption + pixel-wise MSE — model hedges by outputting the mean. **(1 mark direct)**
- **GAN training instability**: Caused by the JS divergence vanishing when $p_g$ and $p_{\text{data}}$ have disjoint support — alleviated by WGAN (Earth-Mover distance) and other variants. **(1 mark direct)**
- **Auto-regressive generation**: GPT-style Transformers factorise $P(x_{1:T}) = \prod_t P(x_t \mid x_{<t})$ with **causal masking**. **(1 mark direct)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Generative AI in Various Domains: Text Generation, Image Synthesis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT633 - AI AND ML IN CYBER SECURITY DEFENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications of Generative AI in Various Domains: Text Generation, Image Synthesis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Generative AI — Formal Definition

**Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI)** is a class of deep-learning models that learn the underlying probability distribution $P_{\text{data}}(x)$ of a training corpus and produce novel, high-fidelity samples that were never explicitly seen during training. Unlike discriminative models that learn the boundary $P(y \mid x)$, generative models learn the joint distribution $P(x, y)$ or simply $P(x)$, enabling *synthesis* of new data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Context):**
> *Generative AI refers to algorithms (Transformers, GANs, VAEs, Diffusion Models) capable of producing realistic text, image, audio, video, and code outputs by sampling from a learned latent distribution. In cyber-security defence, GenAI is dual-use — it both creates offensive threats (deepfakes, polymorphic malware) and powers defensive automation (threat-intelligence summarisation, synthetic SOC training data).*

### 1.2 Two Pillars of Generative AI in This Module

| Pillar | Modal Output | Dominant Architectures | KTU Weightage (Mod-2) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Text Generation** | Natural language, code, security reports | Transformer, GPT, LLaMA, T5 | **High (≈ 60 %)** |
| **Image Synthesis** | Photorealistic / synthetic images | GAN, VAE, Diffusion (DDPM, Stable Diffusion) | **High (≈ 40 %)** |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Counterfeit Detective & Artist"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine a **forger (Generator)** trying to paint fake masterpieces and a **detective (Discriminator)** who has memorised every real painting in the museum. At first, the forger produces clumsy sketches and the detective instantly rejects them. After millions of rounds, the forger learns the *style, brush-strokes, lighting, and composition* of real paintings so well that the detective can no longer tell the forgery from the original. **That equilibrium is what we call a trained Generative Adversarial Network (GAN).** The same principle, but with *language* instead of paint, gives us text generators like GPT.

A student can thus remember:
- **Generator (G)** = "the imaginative brain" — proposes new samples from a noise prior $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$.
- **Discriminator (D)** = "the critic brain" — outputs probability that a sample is real.
- The two networks duel until **G fools D ≈ 50 % of the time** (Nash equilibrium).

### 1.4 Why This Matters in Cyber-Defence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Defensive Use-Cases (Why KTU Tests This):**
> 1. **Automated Threat-Intelligence Reports** — LLMs summarise millions of CTI (Cyber Threat Intelligence) feeds.
> 2. **Synthetic Training Data** — GANs fabricate attack traffic to harden IDS models without leaking real PII.
> 3. **Phishing-Email Forensics** — generative stylometry detects AI-crafted phishing.
> 4. **Deepfake Detection** — diffusion-model residuals expose synthetic media used in BEC (Business Email Compromise) fraud.
> 5. **Code-Assisted SOC Automation** — LLMs generate YARA / Sigma rules from natural-language adversary TTPs.

Standard benchmarks a student **must memorise** (in **bold**):
- **FID (Fréchet Inception Distance)** for image quality — *lower is better*.
- **IS (Inception Score)** for image diversity — *higher is better*.
- **BLEU / ROUGE / METEOR** for text quality.
- **Perplexity (PPL)** for language models — *lower is better*.
- **CLIP-Score** for text-to-image alignment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Text Generation — The Transformer Backbone

Modern text generation is dominated by the **Transformer** architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017). The core unit is the **Scaled Dot-Product Attention**:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

where:
- $Q$ = Query matrix (what we are *looking for*),
- $K$ = Key matrix (what tokens *advertise*),
- $V$ = Value matrix (the actual *information* to retrieve),
- $d_k$ = dimensionality of keys (the scaling factor prevents gradient saturation).

Multi-Head Attention runs $h$ such attentions in parallel and concatenates:

$$\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h)\, W^{O}$$

**Why does it work for cyber-security text?** Because phishing emails, malware command-lines, and CVEs all contain *long-range dependencies* (e.g., a tiny obfuscated token referenced 200 lines away) — attention captures these in **O(1)** path length, whereas RNNs need **O(n)**.

#### 2.1.1 Autoregressive Decoding

Given context $c$, the next-token probability is:

$$P(x_t \mid x_{<t}) = \text{softmax}\!\big(W_o \cdot h_t\big)$$

Three decoding strategies (board-favourite!):

| Strategy | Formula / Behaviour | Use in SOC Triage |
|---|---|---|
| **Greedy** | $x_t = \arg\max P(\cdot)$ | Deterministic rule generation |
| **Beam Search** | Keep top-$k$ partial sequences | Code synthesis, YARA rules |
| **Top-k / Nucleus (Top-p)** | Sample from top-$k$ or top cumulative $p$ | Threat-narrative generation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot-Point:** A common 7-mark question contrasts **Greedy vs. Beam Search vs. Sampling** — remember that *Greedy* is fast but myopic, *Beam* balances quality/diversity, and *Sampling* is creative but uncontrolled.

### 2.2 Image Synthesis — Three Architectural Families

#### 2.2.1 Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)

The original Goodfellow objective is a **two-player min-max game**:

$$\min_{G} \max_{D} \; V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

- $D(x) \in [0,1]$ — probability sample is real.
- $G: z \mapsto x$ — generator mapping noise prior $p_z$ to image space.
- At equilibrium, $D^* = 0.5$ (G fools D half the time).

**Wasserstein GAN (W-GAN)** replaces the Jensen-Shannon divergence with the **Earth-Mover (Wasserstein-1) distance** to solve vanishing-gradient issues:

$$W(p_r, p_g) = \inf_{\gamma \in \Pi(p_r, p_g)} \mathbb{E}_{(x, y) \sim \gamma}\!\big[\| x - y \|\big]$$

#### 2.2.2 Variational Auto-Encoder (VAE)

VAEs learn a *probabilistic encoder* $q_\phi(z \mid x)$ and *decoder* $p_\theta(x \mid z)$ by maximising the **Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)**:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}} = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z\mid x)}\!\big[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)\big]}_{\text{Reconstruction}} - \underbrace{D_{\text{KL}}\!\big(q_\phi(z \mid x) \,\|\, p(z)\big)}_{\text{Regularisation}}$$

The KL term forces the latent distribution to be close to a standard Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$, enabling *smooth sampling*.

#### 2.2.3 Diffusion Models (DDPM — Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models)

Two Markov chains:
- **Forward (noising):** $q(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}\!\big(x_t; \sqrt{1-\beta_t}\, x_{t-1}, \beta_t I\big)$
- **Reverse (denoising):** $p_\theta(x_{t-1} \mid x_t) = \mathcal{N}\!\big(x_{t-1}; \mu_\theta(x_t, t), \Sigma_\theta(x_t, t)\big)$

The simplified training loss is just an MSE between true and predicted noise:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{simple}} = \mathbb{E}_{t, x_0, \epsilon}\!\Big[\,\big\| \epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(\sqrt{\bar\alpha_t}\,x_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar\alpha_t}\,\epsilon, t) \big\|^2\,\Big]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why diffusion beats GAN in fidelity today (and why KTU asks this):** Diffusion models produce higher-diversity, mode-coverage images and are *less prone to mode collapse*, but at the cost of **50–1000× slower sampling**. Stability vs. speed trade-off is a frequent exam theme.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Variable Meanings | Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Scaled Dot-Product Attention | $\text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | $Q, K \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$, $V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$ | Output $\in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$ |
| 2 | GAN Min-Max | $\min_G \max_D V(D,G)$ | $D(x) \in [0,1]$, $G$ maps $z \to x$ | Equilibrium at $D^\*=0.5$ |
| 3 | W-GAN Critic Loss | $L = \mathbb{E}[D(\tilde x)] - \mathbb{E}[D(x)]$ | Critic approximates Wasserstein-1 | 1-Lipschitz via weight-clip / gradient penalty |
| 4 | VAE ELBO | $\mathbb{E}[\log p(x\vert z)] - D_{\text{KL}}(q(z\vert x)\,\Vert\,p(z))$ | Reconstruction − Regularisation | Maximise; $-D_{\text{KL}} \ge 0$ |
| 5 | DDPM Forward | $q(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(\sqrt{1-\beta_t}x_{t-1}, \beta_t I)$ | $\beta_t$ = noise schedule | $T \approx 1000$ typical |
| 6 | DDPM Loss | $\Vert \epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(x_t, t) \Vert^2$ | Predict added Gaussian noise $\epsilon$ | Simple MSE objective |
| 7 | FID | $\Vert \mu_r - \mu_g \Vert^2 + \text{Tr}(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r\Sigma_g)^{1/2})$ | Real vs generated Inception stats | **Lower is better**, $\ge 0$ |
| 8 | BLEU-n | $\exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right) \cdot \text{BP}$ | $p_n$ = n-gram precision, BP = brevity penalty | $[0, 1]$ |
| 9 | Perplexity | $\text{PPL} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\log P(x_i \mid x_{<i})\right)$ | Exponential of cross-entropy | **Lower is better** |
| 10 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $-\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log(\hat y_c)$ | $y$ = one-hot label, $\hat y$ = softmax prob | Used to train all three families |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall — Table Pipe Rule:** In exam answer-sheets, *always* write $\lvert x \rvert$ for absolute value and not a single vertical bar inside a maths environment, or the markdown table will collapse. Same applies if you tabulate formulas in your notebook.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of These Models

- **SOC Automation:** LLM-generated Sigma/YARA rules cut triage time by **~40 %** in real SOCs (MITRE-published case study).
- **Adversarial Robustness:** GAN-synthesised attack traffic strengthens IDS precision against zero-day DDoS patterns.
- **Privacy-Preserving ML:** VAE-generated synthetic logs allow sharing of breach data *without leaking real PII* (differential privacy compatible).
- **Deepfake Defence:** Diffusion-fingerprints (residual noise patterns) are used in C2PA / Content Credentials to mark AI-generated media.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cross-Entropy Loss for an Autoregressive Language Model

**Goal:** Derive the standard cross-entropy objective used to train GPT-style decoders.

**Step 1 — Joint probability of a token sequence.**
For a sequence $x = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N)$, the autoregressive factorisation is:

$$P(x) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(x_i \mid x_{<i})$$

This says: *the probability of the full sentence equals the product of each next-token probability given all previous tokens.*

**Step 2 — Convert product to sum by taking the log.**
Log-probabilities are numerically stable and turn the product into a sum:

$$\log P(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(x_i \mid x_{<i})$$

**Step 3 — Average log-likelihood (one sample).**
The per-sample average is:

$$\ell(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P_\theta(x_i \mid x_{<i})$$

**Step 4 — Take negative to form a *loss* (we minimise, not maximise).**

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}(\theta) = -\ell(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P_\theta(x_i \mid x_{<i})$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate to obtain Perplexity (the KTU-favourite metric).**

$$\text{PPL} = \exp(\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\log P_\theta(x_i \mid x_{<i})\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** If the model assigns probability $0.5$ to the correct next token, loss $\approx 0.69$ and PPL $\approx 2$. If it is confused and assigns $0.01$, loss $\approx 4.6$ and PPL $\approx 99$. **Lower PPL ⇒ model is less "perplexed".**

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Optimal Discriminator of a GAN

**Goal:** Prove that the optimal discriminator $D^*$ satisfies $D^*(x) = \dfrac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}$.

**Step 1 — Write the value function explicitly.**

For a fixed $G$, maximise $V(D, G)$ with respect to $D$:

$$V(D, G) = \int_{x} p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x)\, dx \;+\; \int_{z} p_z(z) \log(1 - D(G(z)))\, dz$$

**Step 2 — Change of variable $x = G(z)$ in the second integral** (assuming $G$ is deterministic and surjective over the support of $p_g$):

$$V(D, G) = \int_{x} \Big[\, p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x) \;+\; p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \,\Big] dx$$

**Step 3 — Pointwise maximise the integrand.**
For a single $x$, take $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial D(x)}$ of the integrand and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial D}\!\left[p_{\text{data}} \log D + p_g \log(1 - D)\right] = \frac{p_{\text{data}}}{D} - \frac{p_g}{1 - D} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $D(x)$.**

$$p_{\text{data}}(1 - D^\*) = p_g D^\* \;\Longrightarrow\; D^\*(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back** to get the GAN generator loss at the optimal $D$:

$$V(D^\*, G) = -2 \log 2 + 2 \cdot \text{JSD}\big(p_{\text{data}} \,\|\, p_g\big)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (Board Favourite):** The GAN value function at the optimal discriminator equals a constant minus twice the **Jensen-Shannon Divergence** between the real and generated distributions. Therefore, *minimising the generator's loss is equivalent to minimising the JSD* — i.e., making $p_g$ converge to $p_{\text{data}}$. This is why GAN training is so elegant mathematically and so painful in practice.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: DDPM Forward-Noise Closed Form

**Goal:** Show that $x_t = \sqrt{\bar\alpha_t}\, x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar\alpha_t}\, \epsilon$, where $\bar\alpha_t = \prod_{s=1}^{t}(1 - \beta_s)$.

**Step 1 — Re-parameterise the forward step.**

$$x_t = \sqrt{1 - \beta_t}\, x_{t-1} + \sqrt{\beta_t}\, \epsilon_{t-1}, \quad \epsilon_{t-1} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute recursively.**
Write the expression for $x_{t-1}$ and plug into $x_t$:

$$x_t = \sqrt{1-\beta_t}\big(\sqrt{1-\beta_{t-1}}\, x_{t-2} + \sqrt{\beta_{t-1}}\, \epsilon_{t-2}\big) + \sqrt{\beta_t}\, \epsilon_{t-1}$$

Expanding:

$$x_t = \sqrt{(1-\beta_t)(1-\beta_{t-1})}\, x_{t-2} + \sqrt{1-\beta_t}\sqrt{\beta_{t-1}}\, \epsilon_{t-2} + \sqrt{\beta_t}\, \epsilon_{t-1}$$

**Step 3 — Merge Gaussian noises.**
The sum of two independent Gaussians $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_1^2 I)$ and $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_2^2 I)$ is $\mathcal{N}(0, (\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2) I)$. After all $t$ merges:

$$x_t = \sqrt{\bar\alpha_t}\, x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar\alpha_t}\, \epsilon, \quad \text{where } \bar\alpha_t = \prod_{s=1}^{t}(1 - \beta_s)$$

**Step 4 — This is the *shortcut* used in training.**
The network $\epsilon_\theta$ only has to predict a single Gaussian $\epsilon$ given $x_t$ and the timestep $t$, enabling tractable training with the simple MSE loss from §2.2.3.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for cyber-defence:** A defender can fingerprint a Stable-Diffusion image by inspecting the **high-frequency noise residual** $x_T - \sqrt{\bar\alpha_T} x_0$ — diffusion artifacts are statistically distinct from camera-sensor noise, enabling deepfake detection.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Text vs. Image Generation

| Dimension | Text Generation (GPT family) | Image Synthesis (GAN / Diffusion) |
|---|---|---|
| **Token type** | Discrete (sub-word BPE) | Continuous (pixels / latents) |
| **Loss** | Cross-entropy (token NLL) | Adversarial (JSD/W) **or** noise MSE |
| **Sampling** | Greedy / Beam / Top-p | Gaussian noise → iterative denoising |
| **Eval metric** | **Perplexity ↓**, BLEU, ROUGE | **FID ↓**, IS ↑, CLIP-Score ↑ |
| **Failure mode** | Hallucination, repetition | Mode collapse, slow sampling |
| **Cyber-defence use** | Phishing detection, SOC report generation | Deepfake detection, synthetic IDS data |
| **Compute** | $O(n^2 d)$ per attention | $O(T \cdot d^2)$ for diffusion, $O(1)$ for GAN inference |
| **Data format** | Token IDs, embeddings | RGB tensor, latent code $z$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Pipeline — Generative AI in a Cyber-SOC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ING["Ingest Layer"]
        A1[Raw CTI Feeds] --> A2[Logs and Alerts]
        A2 --> A3[Network PCAP]
    end

    subgraph GEN["Generative AI Engine"]
        B1[LLM Text Generator]
        B2[GAN Synthetic Traffic Generator]
        B3[Diffusion Deepfake Detector]
        B1 --> B4[Latent Representation]
        B2 --> B4
        B3 --> B4
    end

    subgraph DEF["Defence Analytics"]
        C1[Phishing Classifier]
        C2[IDS Trainer with Synthetic Data]
        C3[Media Forensics Engine]
        C4[Automated YARA Rule Generator]
    end

    ING --> B1
    ING --> B2
    ING --> B3
    B4 --> C1
    B4 --> C2
    B4 --> C3
    B4 --> C4
    C1 --> D1[SOC Dashboard]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
```

### 4.2 GAN Training Loop (Generator vs. Discriminator)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Step1["Step 1: Sample real data"]
        S1A[Real dataset X] --> S1B[Sample mini-batch x_real]
    end

    subgraph Step2["Step 2: Sample noise"]
        S2A[Noise prior p_z] --> S2B[Sample z from N(0, I)]
    end

    subgraph Step3["Step 3: Generate fake"]
        S2B --> S3A[Generator G] --> S3B[Fake x_fake = G of z]
    end

    subgraph Step4["Step 4: Discriminate"]
        S1B --> S4A[Discriminator D]
        S3B --> S4A
        S4A --> S4B[Output D_x in 0 to 1]
    end

    subgraph Step5["Step 5: Update"]
        S4B --> S5A[Compute BCE loss]
        S5A --> S5B[Backprop to D then G]
    end
```

### 4.3 Transformer Decoder Block (Used in GPT-style Text Generation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN[Input Token IDs] --> EMB[Token Embedding]
    EMB --> POS[Positional Encoding]
    POS --> MHA[Masked Multi-Head Attention]
    MHA --> ADD1[Add and Norm]
    ADD1 --> FFN[Feed Forward Network]
    FFN --> ADD2[Add and Norm]
    ADD2 --> SOFT[Softmax over Vocabulary]
    SOFT --> OUT[Next Token Distribution]
```

### 4.4 Diffusion Forward / Reverse Process (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FWD["Forward Diffusion q"]
        X0[x0 real image] --> X1[x1 noisy]
        X1 --> X2[x2 noisier]
        X2 --> X3[xT pure noise N 0 I]
    end

    subgraph REV["Reverse Diffusion p_theta"]
        XT[xT noise input] --> RT1[U Net Predict epsilon]
        RT1 --> RT2[x t-1 less noisy]
        RT2 --> RT3[x t-2]
        RT3 --> RT0[x0 generated image]
    end
```

### 4.5 Cyber-Defence Workflow — Phishing Email Generation & Detection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Adversary collects OSINT] --> B[LLM generates spear-phishing email]
    B --> C[Email sent to victim inbox]
    C --> D[Defensive LLM stylometry detector]
    D --> E{AI generated?}
    E -- yes --> F[Quarantine and SOC alert]
    E -- no --> G[Standard spam filter]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Read this diagram for the exam:** It captures the **dual-use** nature of GenAI — the same technology used by attackers to craft spear-phishing is used by defenders to detect it. KTU loves this "offence vs. defence duality" question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define Generative AI. List any two architectures used for image synthesis and state one evaluation metric for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 m):** Generative AI refers to deep-learning models that learn the data distribution $P(x)$ and synthesise novel samples. Unlike discriminative models, they model $P(x)$ or $P(x, y)$.
- **Architectures (1 m):** (i) **Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)** — two networks duel (Generator + Discriminator). (ii) **Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM)** — learns to iteratively denoise.
- **Metrics (1 m):** GAN evaluated with **FID (Fréchet Inception Distance)** — lower is better; Diffusion also with FID, plus **CLIP-Score** for text-to-image alignment.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Greedy decoding and Beam Search in text generation. In which cyber-security scenario is Beam Search preferred?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Greedy decoding (1 m):** At each step, the single highest-probability token is chosen. It is fast ($O(V)$ per step) but myopic — a locally good token can derail a globally correct sequence.
- **Beam Search (1 m):** Maintains the top-$k$ (beam width) partial sequences at every step and expands all of them, returning the highest joint-probability sequence. Complexity $O(k V)$ per step.
- **Scenario (1 m):** **Beam Search** is preferred when generating structured artefacts such as **YARA / Sigma detection rules** or **IDS signatures** from natural-language adversary TTPs, where syntactic validity and global optimality matter more than creative diversity.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **Transformer** architecture used in modern text generation. Derive the **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** formula and justify the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling factor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the working of a **Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)**. Derive the optimal discriminator $D^\*(x)$ and show that the generator's loss at the optimum equals $-2 \log 2 + 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{\text{data}} \,\|\, p_g)$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Transformer architecture & scaled dot-product attention (7 m):**
1. (2 m) Block diagram: Token Embedding → Positional Encoding → Multi-Head Attention → Add & Norm → Feed-Forward → Add & Norm → Softmax. The decoder is *masked* (causal) for autoregressive generation.
2. (2 m) For input matrices $Q, K \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$ and $V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$, raw attention scores are $QK^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$. Each row is normalised via softmax:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

3. (1 m) The dot-products $q \cdot k$ have variance $\propto d_k$ because $q$ and $k$ components are i.i.d. with zero mean and unit variance. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ keeps the variance of the pre-softmax logits ≈ 1, preventing the softmax from saturating in regions of tiny gradient.
4. (1 m) Multi-Head runs $h$ attentions in parallel with different learned projections $W_i^Q, W_i^K, W_i^V$ and concatenates.
5. (1 m) Application in cyber-security: Phishing-email classification — long-range attention captures the relationship between a sender's display name and obfuscated URLs deep in the email body.

**(b) GAN working & optimal discriminator derivation (7 m):**
1. (1 m) Two networks — Generator $G: z \mapsto x$ with $z \sim p_z$, and Discriminator $D: x \mapsto [0,1]$.
2. (1 m) Value function $V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$
3. (2 m) Change variable $x = G(z)$ in the second integral, pointwise differentiate w.r.t. $D(x)$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial D}\!\left[p_{\text{data}} \log D + p_g \log(1 - D)\right] = \frac{p_{\text{data}}}{D} - \frac{p_g}{1 - D} = 0$$

4. (1 m) Solve: $D^\*(x) = \dfrac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}$.
5. (2 m) Substitute back. Using $\log \tfrac{1}{2} = -\log 2$:

$$V(D^\*, G) = -2 \log 2 + 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{\text{data}} \,\|\, p_g)$$

Thus the generator's objective minimises the JSD, pulling $p_g$ towards $p_{\text{data}}$. Training ends at $D^\* = 0.5$, where $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$ and JSD $\to 0$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain **Perplexity** as an evaluation metric for language models. Derive its relationship with **cross-entropy loss** and compute the perplexity of a model that assigns probabilities $0.4, 0.4, 0.2$ to the correct next tokens in a 3-word sentence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM)**. Show that the forward noising process admits the closed form $x_t = \sqrt{\bar\alpha_t}\, x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar\alpha_t}\, \epsilon$. Discuss **two cyber-security use-cases** of diffusion-based image synthesis. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Perplexity derivation (7 m):**
1. (2 m) For sequence length $N$, cross-entropy loss is:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\log P_\theta(x_i \mid x_{<i})$$

2. (1 m) Perplexity is defined as $\text{PPL} = e^{\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}}$, which is the geometric mean of the reciprocal token probabilities — interpretable as *the effective branching factor of the model*.
3. (1 m) Substitution: $\text{PPL} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\log P_\theta(x_i \mid x_{<i})\right)$.
4. (3 m) Numerical computation — for $N = 3$ with probabilities $0.4, 0.4, 0.2$:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\frac{1}{3}\big[\log 0.4 + \log 0.4 + \log 0.2\big]$$

Using $\log_{e} 0.4 \approx -0.9163$ and $\log_{e} 0.2 \approx -1.6094$:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\frac{1}{3}\big[-0.9163 - 0.9163 - 1.6094\big] = -\frac{1}{3}(-3.4420) \approx 1.1473$$

$$\text{PPL} = e^{1.1473} \approx 3.15$$

A perplexity of ~3.15 means the model is as "confused" as if it had to choose uniformly between ~3 tokens at each step.

**(b) DDPM block diagram & closed-form derivation (7 m):**
1. (2 m) Block diagram: Input $x_0 \to$ [Add noise $\beta_1$] $\to x_1 \to \cdots \to x_T \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$. Reverse: $x_T \to$ [U-Net predict $\epsilon$] $\to x_{T-1} \to \cdots \to x_0$ (generated).
2. (1 m) Forward step equation $x_t = \sqrt{1-\beta_t}\, x_{t-1} + \sqrt{\beta_t}\, \epsilon_{t-1}$.
3. (2 m) Recursive expansion: $x_t = \sqrt{(1-\beta_t)(1-\beta_{t-1})}\, x_{t-2} + \sqrt{(1-\beta_t)\beta_{t-1}}\, \epsilon_{t-2} + \sqrt{\beta_t}\, \epsilon_{t-1}$. Merging independent Gaussians (variances add), and after $t$ recursions:

$$x_t = \sqrt{\bar\alpha_t}\, x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar\alpha_t}\, \epsilon, \quad \bar\alpha_t = \prod_{s=1}^{t}(1 - \beta_s)$$

4. (1 m) **Cyber-security use-case 1:** **Deepfake fingerprinting** — the high-frequency noise residual of a diffusion-generated image is statistically distinguishable from camera-sensor noise, enabling forensic detection of synthetic media used in BEC fraud.
5. (1 m) **Cyber-security use-case 2:** **Synthetic training data for biometric anti-spoofing** — diffusion-generated face images are used to train face-recognition systems against presentation attacks, where labelled real attack data is scarce.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns (Examiner-Verified):**
> 1. **Confusing Generator loss with Discriminator loss (–2 m):** Students often write the GAN value function upside down. Remember: $G$ *minimises* $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$, $D$ *maximises* the full sum.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling (–1 m):** If you write plain $\text{softmax}(QK^{\top})V$, you have *not* reproduced the Transformer. The scaling is what makes training stable.
> 3. **Skipping the closed-form DDPM derivation (–2 m):** A 7-mark question on diffusion *always* expects the recursion and the final $x_t = \sqrt{\bar\alpha_t}x_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar\alpha_t}\epsilon$ result. No shortcut.
> 4. **Mixing up FID and Inception Score (–1 m):** FID is *Fréchet distance* between two Gaussians (lower is better); IS is *KL-based diversity + confidence* (higher is better). They are not interchangeable.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention cyber-security context (–1 m):** In a *cyber-security defence* paper, every architecture discussion must end with at least one defensive application — purely generic AI answers lose the *application mark*.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Generative AI** models $P(x)$ to create new data; **Discriminative AI** models $P(y \mid x)$ to classify.
- **Three dominant image-synthesis families:** **GAN** (adversarial), **VAE** (ELBO), **Diffusion** (iterative denoising).
- **GAN equilibrium:** $D^\*(x) = \dfrac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}$; $D^\* = 0.5$ at convergence; loss at optimum $\propto \text{JSD}$.
- **W-GAN** replaces JSD with **Wasserstein-1** distance and uses a 1-Lipschitz critic to fix vanishing gradients.
- **VAE ELBO** = Reconstruction (decoder log-likelihood) − KL divergence of latent from $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$.
- **DDPM forward closed-form:** $x_t = \sqrt{\bar\alpha_t}\, x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar\alpha_t}\, \epsilon$, with $\bar\alpha_t = \prod_{s=1}^{t}(1-\beta_s)$.
- **DDPM simple loss** = MSE between true and predicted noise: $\Vert \epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(x_t, t) \Vert^2$.
- **Transformer attention:** $\text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$; **scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$** is critical for stable gradients.
- **Decoding strategies:** Greedy (fast, myopic), Beam Search (balanced), Top-p / Top-k Sampling (creative, stochastic).
- **Metrics to memorise:** **Perplexity ↓** (text), **FID ↓** (image fidelity), **IS ↑** (image diversity), **BLEU / ROUGE** (text overlap), **CLIP-Score** (text-to-image alignment).
- **Cyber-defence dual-use (must know):**
  - *Offensive:* LLM-crafted spear-phishing, GAN-generated deepfakes, polymorphic malware variants.
  - *Defensive:* LLM-generated SOC reports / YARA rules, GAN-synthesised IDS training data, diffusion-noise deepfake detection.
- **Mode collapse** is a failure of GANs (generator produces limited variety); mitigated by mini-batch discrimination, spectral normalisation, or switching to diffusion.
- **Hallucination** is a failure of LLMs (fluent but factually false output); mitigated by retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and grounding in CTI feeds.
- **Sampling speed:** GAN ≈ 1 forward pass (ms) ≪ Diffusion (50–1000 denoising steps, seconds) ≫ VAE (single decoder pass, ms).
- **Examination golden rule:** For every 7-marker, present a *diagram + equation + cyber-security application*; that triple maximises the CO (Course Outcome) attainment score.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Case Study: Successful Generative AI Applications and their Impact

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Generative AI (GenAI)** refers to a class of deep learning architectures — primarily **Large Language Models (LLMs)**, **Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)**, **Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)**, and **Diffusion Models** — that learn the underlying probability distribution of a training corpus and synthesize novel, contextually coherent outputs (text, code, images, network traffic, malware signatures, threat narratives).

In the context of **PECCT633 – AI & ML in Cyber Security Defence**, the term *"Generative AI Applications"* specifically denotes production-grade systems that have been **deployed, benchmarked, and validated** in real operational environments (SOC, CSIRT, EDR platforms) to:

- Augment human analysts (Tier-1 / Tier-2 alert triage).
- Generate synthetic threat intelligence feeds.
- Produce explainable incident-response playbooks.
- Simulate adversarial behaviour for purple-team exercises.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A "successful" GenAI application in cyber defence is one that demonstrably reduces **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** and **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR)** while maintaining a **False Positive Rate (FPR) ≤ 5%** and an **analyst-handoff acceptance rate ≥ 85%**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Veteran SOC Mentor"**
> 
> Imagine a newly recruited Tier-1 SOC analyst walking into a Security Operations Centre. They have never seen a *real* zero-day exploit. Standing beside them is a **30-year veteran mentor** who has personally triaged **4.7 million alerts**, written **12,000 incident reports**, and remembers *every* phishing email pattern since 1998.
> 
> The veteran does not "search" the internet for answers. Instead, the veteran **generates** a precise, context-aware response from internalized experience. That is exactly what a Generative AI model does — it is a **statistical mirror of collective human SOC expertise**, compressed into neural weights, capable of producing *new* (never-before-seen) defensive artefacts on demand.

---

## 1.3 Core Building Blocks (Foundation Recap)

| Building Block | Role in Cyber Defence |
|---|---|
| **Transformer Architecture** | Context window analysis of log sequences |
| **Self-Supervised Pre-training** | Learns attack grammar from raw logs |
| **Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)** | Aligns outputs with analyst preferences |
| **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** | Grounds responses in MITRE ATT&CK, CVE feeds |
| **Prompt Engineering** | Tailors output for specific SOC workflows |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Module-2 explicitly links Generative AI success cases to **operational impact metrics** — not merely to model novelty. Memorize the difference between *technical success* (passes benchmarks) and *operational success* (reduces MTTD/MTTR in a live SOC).

---

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Must-Know Bold Constants)

The following **operational KPIs** are universally accepted by KTU examiners for evaluating a GenAI cyber-defence case study:

- **MTTD (Mean Time to Detect):** Industry baseline ≈ **204 days** (IBM Cost of a Data Breach 2023). GenAI-augmented SOCs target ≤ **48 hours**.
- **MTTR (Mean Time to Respond):** Industry baseline ≈ **73 days**. GenAI-augmented target ≤ **24 hours**.
- **TPR (True Positive Rate / Recall):** Target ≥ **0.95**.
- **FPR (False Positive Rate):** Target ≤ **0.05** (i.e., **5%**).
- **F1-Score:** Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall.
- **Analyst Time Saved per Alert:** Typically **60–80%** in GenAI-co-piloted SOCs.
- **Hallucination Rate:** LLM-generated factually incorrect security claim. Target ≤ **2%** for production deployment.

---

## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

This is a **case-study topic** — the examiner tests your ability to:
1. Identify a *named, real-world* GenAI deployment.
2. Quantify its *operational* impact (not just technical novelty).
3. Critically evaluate limitations (hallucination, adversarial prompt injection, data leakage).
4. Map the application's behaviour to the **CIA Triad** and **MITRE ATT&CK** defensive framework.
