# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a "Successful" GenAI Cyber-Defence Application

A deployment qualifies as **operationally successful** only if it clears the following 5-stage maturity ladder. KTU examiners expect students to identify *which stage* any cited case study occupies.

### Stage 1 — Data Ingestion
- Ingests **structured** (SIEM alerts, EDR telemetry) and **unstructured** (threat-intel blogs, dark-web chatter) data.
- Normalizes using schemas such as **OCSF (Open Cybersecurity Schema Framework)** or **ECS (Elastic Common Schema)**.

### Stage 2 — Model Foundation
- Pre-trained LLM (e.g., GPT-4 class, Llama-3 class, Mistral class) or fine-tuned **CyberBERT / SecureBERT**.
- Embedding dimensionality typically **d = 768** or **d = 4096**.

### Stage 3 — Domain Adaptation
- Fine-tuning on **MITRE ATT&CK** technique descriptions (≈ **200 techniques**, 14 tactics).
- Continuous Learning from SOC analyst corrections.

### Stage 4 — Operational Integration
- Plug-in to **SIEM** (Splunk, QRadar, Sentinel), **SOAR** (Cortex XSOAR, Splunk SOAR), and **Ticketing** (ServiceNow, Jira).
- Exposed via **RAG pipelines** over the customer's private threat-intel vault.

### Stage 5 — Feedback Loop
- Analyst upvote/downvote (RLHF signals).
- Auto-evaluation against labelled red-team datasets (e.g., **DARPA TC**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A common 14-mark question pattern is *"Critically evaluate the success of GenAI tool X."* The model answer must explicitly state *which stages* are mature and *which are aspirational*. A tool with strong Stage 1–3 but weak Stage 4–5 is **technically impressive but operationally immature** — examiners expect this distinction.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Metric Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important revision asset** for this topic. All metrics are examinable in both Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

| # | Metric / Formula | Definition | Typical Target Value | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $MTTD = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(T_{detected,i} - T_{occurred,i})$ | Mean Time to Detect | ≤ 48 h (GenAI-augmented) | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | $MTTR = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(T_{resolved,i} - T_{detected,i})$ | Mean Time to Respond | ≤ 24 h | ★★★★★ |
| 3 | $TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | True Positive Rate / Recall | ≥ 0.95 | ★★★★★ |
| 4 | $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | False Positive Rate | ≤ 0.05 | ★★★★★ |
| 5 | $Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Positive Predictive Value | ≥ 0.90 | ★★★★ |
| 6 | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P & R | ≥ 0.92 | ★★★★★ |
| 7 | $Analyst\,Time\,Saved = \frac{T_{manual} - T_{GenAI}}{T_{manual}} \times 100\%$ | Efficiency gain | 60–80% | ★★★★ |
| 8 | $Hallucination\,Rate = \frac{N_{fabricated\,claims}}{N_{total\,claims}} \times 100\%$ | LLM factual-error rate | ≤ 2% | ★★★ |
| 9 | $ROI = \frac{(Benefit - Cost)}{Cost} \times 100\%$ | Return on Investment | ≥ 200% (Year 1) | ★★★ |
| 10 | $Coverage = \frac{\vert MITRE\,Techniques\,Covered \vert}{\vert Total\,MITRE\,Techniques \vert} \times 100\%$ | ATT&CK coverage | ≥ 60% | ★★★★ |

> [!CAUTION]
> **LaTeX Table-Syntax Rule:** Notice the use of `\vert` (not the vertical pipe `\vert`) inside the Coverage formula. KTU paper setters and PDF converters frequently break markdown tables when raw `\vert` symbols appear inside cells. Always escape them as `\vert` or `\mid`.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SOC Automation:** Reduces Tier-1 analyst headcount requirement for alert triage by ~70% (Microsoft Security Copilot case study).
- **Threat-Intel Synthesis:** Condenses 1,200 IOC feeds/day into a 3-page executive brief.
- **Vulnerability Prioritization:** Auto-enriches CVEs with EPSS scores and exploit-availability flags.
- **Purple-Teaming:** Generates realistic phishing lures and C2 traffic for adversary emulation (MITRE Caldera integration).
- **Compliance Reporting:** Auto-drafts PCI-DSS, ISO 27001, and DPDP Act evidence narratives.

---

## 2.4 Critical Limitations (Examiner Favourite)

| Limitation | Technical Description |
|---|---|
| **Hallucination** | LLM invents a CVE-ID that does not exist. Risk to operational trust. |
| **Prompt Injection** | Attacker embeds adversarial instructions inside malware strings to hijack the SOC co-pilot. |
| **Data Leakage** | Sensitive log fragments may be memorized and regurgitated. |
| **Context-Window Bound** | A 128K-token LLM cannot ingest a full year of packet captures. |
| **Cost-to-Scale** | Inference cost per alert can exceed the analyst's hourly wage in low-margin MSSPs. |

## 3.1 The Five Canonical Case Studies (KTU Board Favourites)

The KTU 2024 syllabus, in alignment with the AICTE model curriculum for AI/ML in Cyber Security, expects students to be able to discuss **at least 3 of the following 5** named case studies with quantified impact. The complete analytical matrix is derived below.

### Case Study B — CrowdStrike Charlotte AI

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Case Study A: Security Copilot Impact Analysis

**Stated Vendor Metrics (Microsoft Ignite 2023, RSA 2024 disclosures):**
- **34%** faster incident triage.
- **22%** faster end-to-end incident resolution.
- Analyst time saved per incident ≈ **40 minutes** (from baseline 110 min → 66 min).

### Derivation Step 1 — Quantify Time Saved per Alert

$$
T_{saved} = T_{manual} - T_{GenAI}
$$

$$
T_{saved} = 110\,\text{min} - 66\,\text{min} = 44\,\text{min}
$$

### Derivation Step 2 — Compute Efficiency Gain (%)

$$
E_{gain} = \frac{T_{saved}}{T_{manual}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
E_{gain} = \frac{44}{110} \times 100\% = 40.0\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** The vendor claim of "34% faster triage" is consistent with our 40% efficiency gain derivation (the 6% delta is attributable to triage-only scope vs. full-resolution scope).

### Derivation Step 3 — Annualized Cost Savings (Hypothetical SOC of 50 Analysts)

**Assumptions (standard KTU paper-setting baseline):**
- 50 SOC analysts.
- Average loaded salary = **₹12,00,000 / year** (≈ **$14,500 USD**).
- Avg alerts handled per analyst per day = **40**.
- Working days = **250 / year**.

Total annual alerts handled:
$$
A_{total} = 50 \times 40 \times 250 = 5{,}00{,}000\,\text{alerts/yr}
$$

Time saved per alert = **44 min** = **0.7333 hr**.

Total hours saved annually:
$$
H_{saved} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7333 = 3{,}66{,}650\,\text{hr/yr}
$$

Equivalent FTEs reclaimed (assuming 2,000 productive hours/yr per analyst):
$$
FTE_{reclaimed} = \frac{3{,}66{,}650}{2{,}000} = 183.3\,FTE
$$

Monetized savings:
$$
S_{annual} = 183.3 \times ₹12{,}00{,}000 = ₹21{,}99{,}60{,}000
$$

> **Translation:** Approximately **₹22 Crore (≈ $2.66 M USD) per year** in recovered analyst productivity for a 50-seat SOC.

## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation — Step-by-Step Impact Calculation for CrowdStrike Charlotte AI

**Vendor Disclosure (CrowdStrike 2024 Threat Hunting Report):**
- Alert volume reduced by **75%** through GenAI deduplication.
- Mean time to investigate (MTTI) reduced by **40%**.

### Step 1 — Alert Volume Post-GenAI

$$
A_{post} = A_{pre} \times (1 - 0.75) = A_{pre} \times 0.25
$$

If $A_{pre} = 10{,}000$ alerts/day (typical mid-size SOC), then:

$$
A_{post} = 10{,}000 \times 0.25 = 2{,}500\,\text{alerts/day}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Investigator Throughput Multiplier

Assuming each investigator resolves **20 alerts/day** in baseline conditions:

$$
T_{baseline} = \frac{10{,}000}{20} = 500\,\text{investigator-days/day}
$$

After GenAI reduction (and the residual alerts being higher-fidelity, so each takes longer ≈ **1.4×** the original effort per alert due to deeper context):

$$
T_{post} = \frac{2{,}500 \times 1.4}{20} = 175\,\text{investigator-days/day}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Net FTE Reduction

$$
\Delta FTE = T_{baseline} - T_{post} = 500 - 175 = 325\,FTE-days/day
$$

For 250 working days:
$$
\Delta FTE_{annual} = 325 \times 250 = 81{,}250\,FTE-days/yr
$$

Equivalent to:
$$
FTE_{saved} = \frac{81{,}250}{250} = 325\,FTEs
$$

> **Conclusion:** For a SOC ingesting 10,000 alerts/day, the CrowdStrike Charlotte AI pipeline can reclaim **~325 analyst-FTEs** in throughput-equivalent terms — illustrating why GenAI deployment is rapidly moving from *optional* to *strategically mandatory* in Tier-1 MSSPs.

## 3.5 Critical-Evaluation Framework (For 14-Mark Answers)

Every KTU 14-mark answer on this topic **must** end with a balanced critical evaluation. Use the **PESTLE-Plus** lens:

1. **P — Performance:** Quantified MTTD/MTTR gains.
2. **E — Economic:** TCO vs. ROI calculation.
3. **S — Security:** Hallucination, prompt-injection, data-leakage risks.
4. **T — Trust:** Auditability, explainability (XAI) of generated incident reports.
5. **L — Legal:** DPDP Act 2023, GDPR, and cross-border log-transfer compliance.
6. **E — Ethics:** Workforce displacement of junior analysts; bias in training data.
7. **Plus — Practical Maturity:** Is the deployment Stage-1 (pilot) or Stage-5 (full feedback loop)?

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Writing a "cheerleading" 14-mark answer that lists only benefits (no limitations) will be capped at **8/14 marks** maximum. The examiner always allocates at least **4 marks** to critical analysis and **2 marks** to quantified numerical impact.

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A questions expect **3–4 sentence crisp answers** with at least one *quantified* or *named* element. Vague definitions are penalized.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Define "Generative AI" in the context of cyber security defence. Differentiate it from traditional discriminative ML models used in spam or malware detection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Generative AI is a class of deep learning models — primarily **Transformer-based LLMs**, **GANs**, and **Diffusion Models** — that *learn the joint probability distribution* of a dataset and can *synthesize new* artefacts (text, code, network signatures). In cyber defence, it is used to **generate** incident summaries, threat-intel narratives, and synthetic attack traffic.

By contrast, **discriminative ML** (e.g., Random Forest, SVM, classical CNN) *learns the conditional probability* $P(y \mid x)$ and only **classifies** whether an input is malicious or benign — it cannot *generate* novel defensive content. **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for generative mechanism, 1 for contrast with discriminative.)**

---

3. **Palo Alto Cortex XSIAM Copilot** — Reduces MTTR by approximately **60%** via auto-generated playbooks *(1 mark)*.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choose A or B

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / June 2024 Model Pattern]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With reference to the Microsoft Security Copilot case study, explain the architecture of a GenAI-augmented SOC. How does RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) reduce hallucination in security reports?**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Architecture Overview (4 marks):**
The Microsoft Security Copilot architecture comprises five layers:
- **Ingestion Layer** — Pulls telemetry from M365 Defender, Sentinel, Intune, and third-party SIEMs.
- **Preprocessing Layer** — Normalizes events into the **OSSEM (Open Source Security Events Metadata)** schema; de-duplicates alerts using embedding similarity (cosine ≥ **0.92** threshold).
- **Generative Core** — GPT-4 class LLM fine-tuned on Microsoft's internal incident corpus (≈ **15 million** resolved tickets).
- **RAG Layer** — Retrieves the top-**k = 5** most semantically similar past incidents from a vector store (FAISS or Azure AI Search) before generating a new response.
- **Action Layer** — Surfaces recommendations to analysts via a chat UI; integrates with **Logic Apps** for one-click containment.

**2. RAG and Hallucination Reduction (3 marks):**
RAG works in 3 steps:
- **Step 1 — Retrieval:** For each query $q$, retrieve top-**k** documents $D = \{d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_k\}$ from the vector store using similarity $\text{sim}(q, d_i) = \cos(\mathbf{v}_q, \mathbf{v}_{d_i})$.
- **Step 2 — Augmentation:** Concatenate the retrieved documents with the user prompt: $\text{context} = q \oplus d_1 \oplus d_2 \oplus \ldots \oplus d_k$.
- **Step 3 — Grounded Generation:** The LLM conditions its output on the retrieved context, reducing hallucination rate from baseline **~12%** to **≤ 2%** in production SOCs.

> **Quantitative claim:** A peer-reviewed study by Microsoft Research (2024) measured a **hallucination reduction of ~83%** in CVE-citation tasks when RAG is enabled.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compute the ROI of deploying a GenAI SOC co-pilot for a 100-analyst MSSP. Assume: avg analyst cost = ₹15 LPA; alert volume = 12,000/day; baseline time per alert = 8 min; GenAI time per alert = 3 min; GenAI license cost = ₹8 Cr/year.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute Baseline Analyst Hours / Year**

$$
H_{baseline} = 12{,}000 \times \frac{8}{60} \times 250 = 4{,}00{,}000\,\text{hr/yr}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Post-GenAI Analyst Hours / Year**

$$
H_{GenAI} = 12{,}000 \times \frac{3}{60} \times 250 = 1{,}50{,}000\,\text{hr/yr}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Hours Saved**

$$
H_{saved} = 4{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000 = 2{,}50{,}000\,\text{hr/yr}
$$

**Step 4 — Monetize Savings**

$$
S = 2{,}50{,}000 \times \frac{₹15{,}00{,}000}{2{,}000\,\text{hr/yr}} = ₹18{,}75{,}00{,}000 \approx ₹18.75\,\text{Cr/yr}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Net Benefit**

$$
\text{Net Benefit} = S - C_{license} = ₹18.75\,\text{Cr} - ₹8\,\text{Cr} = ₹10.75\,\text{Cr/yr}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute ROI**

$$
ROI = \frac{\text{Net Benefit}}{C_{license}} \times 100\% = \frac{10.75}{8} \times 100\% = 134.4\%
$$

> **Conclusion:** A 100-analyst MSSP deploying the GenAI co-pilot at the stated parameters achieves a **first-year ROI of ≈ 134.4%** and reclaims the equivalent of **125 analyst FTEs** in throughput terms. **(7 marks: 1 for each step + 1 for the final conclusion.)**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Critically evaluate the CrowdStrike Charlotte AI case study. What operational metrics improved, and what limitations remained unaddressed?**

**Model Answer:**

**Operational Wins (4 marks):**
- **Alert volume reduced by 75%** through embedding-based clustering of duplicate alerts.
- **MTTI reduced by 40%** as Charlotte pre-classifies alerts as True/False positive with cited evidence.
- **Analyst handoff acceptance ≥ 88%**, indicating strong human-AI trust.
- **MITRE ATT&CK coverage > 85%** of the 14-tactic matrix.

**Unaddressed Limitations (3 marks):**
- **Hallucination of CVE-IDs** — Charlotte occasionally fabricates CVE references for novel malware families not yet in the corpus.
- **Adversarial prompt injection** — Embedded in PowerShell or macro payloads, malicious instructions can hijack the assistant into disabling EDR rules.
- **Cost-to-scale** — Per-alert inference cost remains a friction point for cost-sensitive APAC markets.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Design a 5-stage maturity model for evaluating the success of any GenAI cyber-defence deployment. Apply it to score an open-source deployment (e.g., LangChain + MITRE Caldera + Counterfit) on a scale of 1–5.**

**Model Answer:**

**The 5-Stage Model (4 marks):**

| Stage | Capability | Score 1–5 |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Data Ingestion (logs, IOCs, tickets) | 1=ad-hoc, 5=automated, OCSF-normalized |
| 2 | Pre-trained Foundation (LLM backbone) | 1=toy, 5=frontier-class |
| 3 | Domain Fine-Tuning (MITRE/CVE-aware) | 1=zero, 5=continuous fine-tuning |
| 4 | SIEM/SOAR Integration | 1=standalone, 5=native plugins |
| 5 | Closed-Loop RLHF | 1=none, 5=fully automated feedback |

**Scoring the Open-Source Stack (3 marks):**
- **Stage 1:** **3/5** — Capable of ingesting logs, but lacks OCSF normalization out-of-the-box.
- **Stage 2:** **2/5** — Relies on community LLMs (e.g., Llama-3-8B); not frontier-grade.
- **Stage 3:** **3/5** — MITRE Caldera provides ATT&CK alignment, but fine-tuning depth is shallow.
- **Stage 4:** **2/5** — SIEM integration requires custom scripting.
- **Stage 5:** **1/5** — No native RLHF; relies on manual prompt iteration.

**Total Score: 11/25 (44%)** → **Operationally Immature**, suitable for academic R&D but **not** yet for production SOC deployment.

> **Examiner's Note:** A 14-mark answer that *applies* a model to a *named* deployment (rather than describing the model in the abstract) scores higher. Always quantify.

- ✅ **Top 3 limitations:** Hallucination, Prompt Injection, Data Leakage.
- ✅ **Maturity ladder:** Stage 1 (Data) → Stage 2 (Foundation) → Stage 3 (Fine-Tune) → Stage 4 (Integrate) → Stage 5 (RLHF).
- ✅ **ROI formula always ends with a critical evaluation** — never write a "cheerleader" answer.
- ✅ **Always cite at least one numerical value** in every 7-mark sub-answer.
- ✅ **Frameworks to mention:** MITRE ATT&CK, OCSF, CIA Triad, NIST CSF 2.0, DPDP Act 2023.
- ✅ **CIA Triad mapping:** GenAI primarily defends **Confidentiality** (RAG over private intel) and **Integrity** (synthetic decoys for deception) and indirectly **Availability** (faster MTTR).
- ✅ **One-line punchline for viva:** *"Successful GenAI in cyber defence is measured not by model novelty but by quantified MTTD/MTTR reduction in a live SOC."*

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Case Study: Successful Generative AI Applications and their Impact**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Generative AI in Cybersecurity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT633 - AI AND ML IN CYBER SECURITY DEFENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Applications of Generative AI in Cybersecurity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Generative AI (GenAI) in Cybersecurity** refers to the application of deep generative models — including **Large Language Models (LLMs)**, **Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)**, **Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)**, **Diffusion Models**, and **Transformer-based architectures** — to autonomously synthesize data, content, code, or analytical artifacts that enhance the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information systems, while simultaneously addressing the novel threats these same models introduce into the threat landscape.

According to the **NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0, 2023)** and **MITRE ATLAS** adversarial AI taxonomy, Generative AI in cyber defence is positioned as a **dual-use capability** with offensive, defensive, and intelligence functions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Key Concept (PECCT633 M3):**
> Generative AI is *not* merely "AI that writes text." It is a class of models that learn the underlying probability distribution $P(X)$ of legitimate system telemetry, network flows, source code, or attacker behaviour, and use that distribution to *create* new samples, *score* anomalies, or *reason* over complex security contexts.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cyber Twin"

Imagine a **forensic sketch artist** who has memorized millions of faces.

- **Discriminative AI** (traditional ML) is like a *security guard* at a building entrance: it checks your ID against a pre-approved list and says **YES/NO**.
- **Generative AI** is like the *sketch artist* who has seen every face in the city. They can:
  1. **Draw a completely new face** (synthesize novel phishing emails, malware variants, attack scenarios).
  2. **Recognise when a face "doesn't fit" the city's pattern** (anomaly / zero-day detection via reconstruction error).
  3. **Describe what a missing suspect might look like** (threat intelligence, IOC prediction, attack-path simulation).
  4. **Create thousands of training faces for new guards** (data augmentation for under-represented attack classes).

In the cyber world, the "city" is your enterprise network, and the "faces" are legitimate traffic patterns, normal user behaviours, clean binaries, and benign source code.

## 1.3 Core Building Blocks — The Generative AI Family

| Model Family | Core Mechanism | Primary Cybersecurity Use |
|---|---|---|
| **GAN (Generative Adversarial Network)** | Generator vs Discriminator min-max game | Adversarial example crafting, synthetic malware, evasion testing |
| **VAE (Variational Autoencoder)** | Probabilistic encoder-decoder in latent space | Anomaly detection via reconstruction error |
| **Transformer / LLM** | Self-attention over token sequences | Phishing email analysis, log summarization, SOC copilot |
| **Diffusion Model** | Iterative denoising of Gaussian noise | Deepfake detection, synthetic training data |
| **Autoregressive Models** | $P(x_t \mid x_{<t})$ next-token prediction | Code vulnerability discovery, payload generation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Module 3 Anchors:**
> The KTU 2024 PECCT633 syllabus expects students to understand *how* these generative architectures map to **(i) Threat Detection**, **(ii) Adversarial Attack/Defence**, **(iii) Threat Intelligence Automation**, **(iv) Security Operations Augmentation**, and **(v) Synthetic Data Generation for model training**.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in 2024–2025

The **ENISA Threat Landscape Report 2024** and **IBM Cost of a Data Breach 2024** both identify AI-augmented attacks (deepfake vishing, LLM-crafted spear phishing, polymorphic malware) as a top emerging risk. Simultaneously, defenders are deploying GenAI to:
- Cut **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** from 204 days to under 48 hours.
- Automate **Tier-1 SOC analyst** workflows.
- Generate **red-team simulation content** at machine speed.
- Synthesize **balanced training datasets** for fraud, APT, and ransomware classifiers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Functional Pillars of GenAI in Cyber Defence

### Pillar 1 — Anomaly & Zero-Day Detection (Reconstruction-Based)
A VAE or autoencoder is trained **only on benign traffic** $X_{benign} \sim P_{normal}$. At inference, the model reconstructs inputs. The **reconstruction error** $L_{recon}$ is the anomaly score.

**Why it works for zero-days:** The model has never seen the attack pattern, so it cannot reconstruct it accurately → high error → flagged anomaly.

### Pillar 2 — Adversarial Example Generation (Offensive Research)
GANs and gradient-based attacks (FGSM, PGD) generate **adversarial perturbations** $\delta$ such that:

$$x_{adv} = x + \delta, \quad \|\delta\|_p \leq \epsilon$$

These are used for **adversarial robustness testing** of deployed ML-based IDS, malware classifiers, and biometric systems (per **MITRE ATLAS AML.T0043**).

### Pillar 3 — Threat Intelligence & Report Automation
LLMs fine-tuned on **MITRE ATT&CK**, **CVE databases**, and **STIX/TAXII feeds** can:
- Map IOCs to ATT&CK techniques automatically.
- Generate executive-level incident summaries.
- Translate raw PCAP strings into natural-language playbooks.

### Pillar 4 — SOC Copilot & Incident Response
LLM-based assistants (e.g., Microsoft Security Copilot, IBM watsonx, Google Gemini for Security) augment Tier-1 analysts by:
- Summarizing thousands of SIEM events into a single incident narrative.
- Recommending KQL/SPL/Sigma queries.
- Drafting customer breach notifications.

### Pillar 5 — Synthetic Data & Red-Team Content
Diffusion models and GANs generate **synthetic phishing pages, malware byte streams, deepfake audio, and adversarial network flows** for training defensive classifiers — solving the chronic class-imbalance problem (e.g., only 0.01% of emails are true positives).

## 2.2 Operational Logic — The GenAI Cyber-Defence Loop

1. **Data Ingestion:** Collect telemetry from EDR, NDR, SIEM, email gateways, cloud logs.
2. **Model Pretraining:** Train a foundation model (LLM or encoder) on large public corpora (CVE, ATT&CK, code repos).
3. **Domain Fine-Tuning:** Fine-tune on enterprise-specific logs, runbooks, and incident tickets (RAG + LoRA).
4. **Inference Layer:** Run real-time on streaming events for scoring/summarization.
5. **Human-in-the-Loop:** Analyst validates, overrides, and feeds corrections back into the model (online learning / RLHF).
6. **Adversarial Hardening:** Continuously test the deployed model with GAN-generated attacks.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Equation Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Description | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_{GAN} = \min_G \max_D \; V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1-D(G(z)))]$ | Generative Adversarial Network value function (Goodfellow et al., 2014) | Adversarial example generation, synthetic malware |
| 2 | $\mathcal{L}_{VAE} = -\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \mid x)}[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)] + D_{KL}(q_\phi(z \mid x) \,\|\, p(z))$ | Variational Autoencoder ELBO loss | Anomaly detection, reconstruction-based IDS |
| 3 | $L_{recon}(x) = \|x - \hat{x}\|_2^2 = \|x - D(E(x))\|_2^2$ | Anomaly score as MSE reconstruction error | Zero-day detection, UBA/UEBA |
| 4 | $x_{adv} = x + \epsilon \cdot \text{sign}(\nabla_x J(\theta, x, y))$ | FGSM adversarial perturbation (Goodfellow 2015) | Evasion attacks, adversarial robustness |
| 5 | $\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | Scaled dot-product attention (Vaswani et al., 2017) | LLM-based SOC, log understanding |
| 6 | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}, \quad P = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}, \quad R = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Precision, Recall, F1-score | Evaluating GenAI-based detectors |
| 7 | $H(p) = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Shannon entropy — used to detect packed/encrypted malware | Malware triage |
| 8 | $\text{MCC} = \dfrac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | Matthews Correlation Coefficient — robust metric for imbalanced security datasets | SOC alert evaluation |
| 9 | $L_{diffusion} = \mathbb{E}_{t, x_0, \epsilon}\!\left[\,\|\epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(\sqrt{\bar\alpha_t} x_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar\alpha_t}\,\epsilon,\, t)\|^2\right]$ | Diffusion model training loss (Ho et al., 2020) | Deepfake/synthetic media detection |
| 10 | $s_{anomaly}(x) = -\log p_\theta(x) \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\|x - \hat\mu\|^2 \Sigma^{-1} + \text{const}$ | Mahalanobis-style anomaly score in latent space | Advanced UEBA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Markdown Table Safety Note:** All absolute-value and norm notations above use the LaTeX vertical bar `\|` or `\vert` rendered inside math mode — never the raw pipe character `|` — to preserve Markdown table integrity during KTU PDF/HTML export.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | GenAI Cyber Application | Business Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Banking & FinTech** | Synthetic fraud transactions for AML model training | Reduces false positives by 30–60% (per NVIDIA/PayPal 2024 case studies) |
| **Healthcare** | PHI-aware LLMs for HIPAA log triage | 24×7 SOC coverage at fraction of human cost |
| **Critical Infrastructure (OT/ICS)** | LLM-based SCADA log analysis + anomaly VAEs | Earlier detection of TRITON, Industroyer-class threats |
| **Cloud (AWS/Azure/GCP)** | Copilot tools for IAM policy review | Eliminates over-privileged roles (a top cloud breach vector) |
| **Government / Defence** | Red-team simulation with LLM-generated TTPs | Continuous purple-team validation |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — The VAE Anomaly Score for Network Intrusion Detection

We derive, from first principles, the **reconstruction-error anomaly score** used by a VAE-based Network Intrusion Detection System (NIDS).

### Step 1 — Define the Network Flow Feature Vector

Each network flow $x \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is a feature vector of length $d$ (e.g., duration, bytes, protocol flags, packet statistics).

### Step 2 — Encode into a Probabilistic Latent Space

The encoder $q_\phi(z \mid x)$ maps $x$ to a Gaussian in latent space $z \in \mathbb{R}^k$ ($k \ll d$):

$$q_\phi(z \mid x) = \mathcal{N}(z \,\vert\, \mu_\phi(x),\; \sigma_\phi^2(x) I)$$

where $\mu_\phi(x)$ and $\sigma_\phi(x)$ are neural network outputs (the **mean** and **standard deviation** vectors).

### Step 3 — Reparameterization Trick (Kingma & Welling, 2014)

To allow backpropagation through the random sampling step:

$$z = \mu_\phi(x) + \sigma_\phi(x) \odot \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$$

Here $\odot$ denotes the element-wise (Hadamard) product. This is essential because the sampling operation is otherwise non-differentiable.

### Step 4 — Decoder Reconstructs the Input

The decoder $p_\theta(x \mid z)$ reconstructs $\hat{x} = D_\theta(z)$. For continuous flow features, a Gaussian decoder is used:

$$p_\theta(x \mid z) = \mathcal{N}(x \,\vert\, D_\theta(z),\; \sigma^2 I)$$

### Step 5 — Write the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)

The log-likelihood $\log p_\theta(x)$ is intractable, so we maximize its lower bound:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log p_\theta(x) &\geq \mathcal{L}_{ELBO}(\theta, \phi; x) \\
&= \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \mid x)}\!\left[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)\right] - D_{KL}\!\left(q_\phi(z \mid x) \,\|\, p(z)\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The first term is the **reconstruction likelihood**; the second is the **KL regularizer** that keeps the latent space close to a standard normal prior $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$.

### Step 6 — Closed-Form KL Divergence for Diagonal Gaussians

For two diagonal Gaussians $\mathcal{N}(\mu_q, \sigma_q^2)$ and $\mathcal{N}(\mu_p, \sigma_p^2)$:

$$
D_{KL} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{k}\!\left[\, \frac{\sigma_q^2(j) + (\mu_q(j) - \mu_p(j))^2}{\sigma_p^2(j)} - 1 + \log\frac{\sigma_p^2(j)}{\sigma_q^2(j)} \right]
$$

For a unit prior $\mu_p = 0,\; \sigma_p^2 = 1$, this simplifies to:

$$D_{KL} = -\frac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{k}\!\left[\,1 + \log \sigma_q^2(j) - \mu_q^2(j) - \sigma_q^2(j) \right]$$

### Step 7 — Define the Anomaly Score

At inference, the anomaly score is the **negative log-likelihood proxy**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_{anomaly}(x) &= -\mathcal{L}_{ELBO}(\theta, \phi; x) \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\|x - \hat{x}\|_2^2 + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{k}\!\left[\mu_q^2(j) + \sigma_q^2(j) - \log\sigma_q^2(j) - 1\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

The first term is the **MSE reconstruction error**; the second is the **regularization penalty**. A flow is flagged as an attack if $s_{anomaly}(x) > \tau$, where $\tau$ is chosen on a validation set to achieve a target FPR (typically 1%).

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for Students:** Benign flows lie in well-learned regions of the latent space, so $\hat{x} \approx x$ and the KL term is small. Novel attack flows fall *outside* the training manifold, so both terms spike — that is the **zero-day detection signal**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Adversarial FGSM Perturbation on a Malware Classifier

**Setup:** A logistic-regression malware classifier $f_\theta(x) = \sigma(w^\top x + b)$ classifies a PE file by its 4-byte feature vector. We have:

$$w = [0.5, -0.3, 0.8, -0.2]^\top, \quad b = 0.1, \quad x = [1.0, 0.5, 0.0, 1.0]^\top, \quad y = 1 \;(\text{malware})$$

**Step 1 — Compute the clean prediction and loss.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
z &= w^\top x + b = (0.5)(1.0) + (-0.3)(0.5) + (0.8)(0.0) + (-0.2)(1.0) + 0.1 \\
&= 0.5 - 0.15 + 0 - 0.2 + 0.1 = 0.25 \\
\hat{p} &= \sigma(0.25) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.25}} \approx 0.5622
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Binary cross-entropy loss for the true label $y=1$:**

$$J = -\log(\hat{p}) = -\log(0.5622) \approx 0.5764$$

**Step 3 — Compute the gradient of $J$ w.r.t. $x$** (using $\partial J/\partial z = \hat{p} - y$ and $\partial z/\partial x = w$):

$$\nabla_x J = (\hat{p} - y) \cdot w = (0.5622 - 1.0) \cdot [0.5,\; -0.3,\; 0.8,\; -0.2]^\top = -0.4378 \cdot w$$

$$\nabla_x J = [-0.2189,\; 0.1313,\; -0.3502,\; 0.0876]^\top$$

**Step 4 — Apply the FGSM update with $\epsilon = 0.1$:**

$$\delta = \epsilon \cdot \text{sign}(\nabla_x J) = 0.1 \cdot [-1,\; +1,\; -1,\; +1]^\top = [-0.1,\; 0.1,\; -0.1,\; 0.1]^\top$$

$$x_{adv} = x + \delta = [0.9,\; 0.6,\; -0.1,\; 1.1]^\top$$

**Step 5 — Re-evaluate the classifier:**

$$z_{adv} = 0.5(0.9) + (-0.3)(0.6) + 0.8(-0.1) + (-0.2)(1.1) + 0.1 = 0.45 - 0.18 - 0.08 - 0.22 + 0.1 = 0.07$$

$$\hat{p}_{adv} = \sigma(0.07) \approx 0.5175$$

**Step 6 — Interpret.** The confidence dropped from **0.5622 → 0.5175** with an $L_\infty$-bounded perturbation of magnitude 0.1. If we iterate this PGD-style for $T=20$ steps, the malware can be made to cross the decision boundary (i.e., classifier says *benign*) while the byte-level changes are imperceptible to signature engines — this is the **evasion attack** threat described in MITRE ATLAS **AML.T0043**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing GenAI Application Domains

| Application Domain | GenAI Architecture | Input Modality | Output | Defender Benefit | Attacker Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Anomaly Detection (NIDS)** | VAE / Autoencoder | NetFlow, PCAP, Zeek logs | Anomaly score + reconstruction | Zero-day detection, low FPR | Adversarial noise can hide C2 channels |
| **Phishing Email Triage** | Fine-tuned LLM (BERT/RoBERTa) | Email headers + body | Phish / Benign + rationale | 95%+ precision, explainable | LLM-crafted spear phishing at scale |
| **Malware Variant Synthesis** | GAN / Diffusion | PE byte sequences | New malware samples | Train robust classifiers | Generate polymorphic malware |
| **Adversarial Robustness Testing** | GAN + PGD | Images, audio, network features | Adversarial samples | Harden ML models | Bypass deployed ML defences |
| **Threat Intel Report Gen** | RAG-LLM (GPT, Llama, Mistral) | STIX/TAXII, CVE, blogs | Executive summary, ATT&CK mapping | 10× faster intel production | LLM hallucination of fake IOCs |
| **Deepfake / Vishing Detection** | CNN + Diffusion classifier | Audio, video, voice biometrics | Real / Synthetic + confidence | Counter $50M+ deepfake fraud (per Sumsub 2024) | Real-time voice cloning for CEO fraud |
| **Code Vulnerability Discovery** | Code LLM (CodeLlama, StarCoder) | Source code | Vulnerability report + patch | Catch OWASP Top-10 early | Automated exploit synthesis |
| **SIEM / SOC Copilot** | LLM + Tool-use / Agents | SIEM alerts, runbooks | Narrative, KQL/Sigma queries, playbooks | Reduce MTTR from days to minutes | Prompt injection against the copilot |
| **Synthetic Training Data** | Tabular GAN, Diffusion | Fraud, IoT, OT datasets | Balanced synthetic rows | Solve class-imbalance | Data poisoning via backdoor injection |
| **Red-Team Simulation** | LLM agents (ReAct, AutoGen) | MITRE ATT&CK, network topology | Realistic attack playbooks | Continuous purple-team | Agent jailbreak → live attack plan |

## 3.4 Derivation — LLM Attention for Log Sequence Analysis

A self-attention layer over $n$ log events, each embedded as $d$-dimensional vectors, uses learnable projection matrices $W^Q, W^K, W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= X W^Q, \quad K = X W^K, \quad V = X W^V \\
\text{head}_i &= \text{Attention}(Q W_i^Q,\; K W_i^K,\; V W_i^V) \\
\text{MultiHead}(X) &= \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h) W^O
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **log-anomaly LLM**, the input $X$ is the sequence of tokenized log templates (e.g., `User <USR> logged in from <IP> with status <CODE>`). Self-attention learns to **correlate a failed login in event $t-3$ with a successful login from a new geo in event $t-1$** — a classic credential-stuffling pattern.

The scaling factor $\sqrt{d_k}$ prevents the softmax from saturating when $d_k$ is large.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
