#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a **Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)** as applied to synthetic malware sample generation. Derive the GAN value function from the discriminator's binary cross-entropy loss. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss how **Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)** can be used for zero-day anomaly detection. Derive the ELBO objective and explain the role of the KL-divergence term. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — GAN for Synthetic Malware Generation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Architecture (2 Marks):**

A GAN consists of two neural networks trained in opposition:
- **Generator $G$**: Takes a random noise vector $z \sim p_z(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ and outputs a synthetic malware binary feature vector $G(z) \in \mathbb{R}^n$.
- **Discriminator $D$**: A binary classifier that outputs $D(x) \in [0, 1]$, the probability that input $x$ is a real malware sample.

In the cybersecurity context:
- The training corpus contains **real malware feature vectors** (PE header bytes, opcode frequencies, API call sequences).
- $G$ learns to produce **plausible mutations** of known malware — useful for training downstream IDS classifiers that must generalize to **zero-day variants**.

**Step 2 — Derivation of the Value Function (3 Marks):**

For a real sample $x$, the discriminator's negative log-likelihood is $-\log D(x)$.
For a fake sample $G(z)$, the probability that $D$ correctly rejects it is $1 - D(G(z))$, giving loss $-\log(1 - D(G(z)))$.

Taking expectations and negating:

$$\begin{aligned}
V(D, G) &= \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]
\end{aligned}$$

The training game is:

$$\boxed{\min_G \max_D V(D, G)}$$

**Step 3 — Training Dynamics (2 Marks):**
- $D$ is trained to **maximize** $V$ (correctly classify real vs. fake).
- $G$ is trained to **minimize** $V$ (fool the discriminator).
- At Nash equilibrium, $D(x) = 0.5$ for all $x$ — $G$ has perfectly modeled $p_{\text{data}}$.

---

#### Part (b) — VAE for Zero-Day Detection (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — VAE Architecture (2 Marks):**

A VAE has an **encoder** $q_\phi(z \mid x)$ mapping input $x$ to a probabilistic latent $z \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_\phi(x), \sigma_\phi^2(x) I)$, and a **decoder** $p_\theta(x \mid z)$ reconstructing $\hat{x}$ from $z$.

**Step 2 — ELBO Derivation (3 Marks):**

Starting from the marginal log-likelihood $\log p_\theta(x)$ and applying Jensen's inequality:

$$\log p_\theta(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \mid x)}[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)] - D_{\text{KL}}(q_\phi(z \mid x) \,\|\, p(z))$$

Thus the training objective is to **maximize the ELBO**:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}} = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi}[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)]}_{\text{Reconstruction}} - \underbrace{D_{\text{KL}}(q_\phi(z \mid x) \,\|\, p(z))}_{\text{Regularization}}$$

**Step 3 — Role of KL Term and Anomaly Scoring (2 Marks):**

- The KL term forces the latent space to follow $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$, ensuring a **smooth, regularized manifold**.
- For a **zero-day attack**, $q_\phi(z \mid x)$ must deviate strongly from the prior, increasing the reconstruction error.
- The anomaly score is:

$$A(x) = \Vert x - D_\theta(E_\phi(x)) \Vert_2^2$$

If $A(x) > \tau$, the event is flagged as a potential zero-day.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **end-to-end pipeline** of a Generative-AI-driven threat prediction system. Identify the role of LLMs in the final stage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Diffusion Models** for threat detection. Derive the forward noising process $q(x_t \mid x_{t-1})$ and discuss how the **anomaly score** is computed via reverse-denoising deviation. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — End-to-End Pipeline (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Pipeline Stages (5 Marks, 1 per stage):**

1. **Data Ingestion**: Collect logs from SIEM, EDR, NetFlow, dark-web CTI feeds.
2. **Normalization & Tokenization**: Convert heterogeneous logs into numerical vectors or text tokens.
3. **Feature Engineering**: Extract byte $n$-grams, time-window statistics, protocol flags, and BERT embeddings for textual threat reports.
4. **Generative Model Training**: Train GAN/VAE/Diffusion/LLM on combined benign + known-malicious corpora.
5. **Real-Time Prediction & Anomaly Scoring**: For each incoming event, compute $A(x)$ and raise alert if $A(x) > \tau$.

**Step 2 — Role of LLM in Final Stage (2 Marks):**

- LLMs (e.g., **fine-tuned GPT, Llama, or Mistral**) auto-generate **human-readable threat narratives** in STIX 2.1 format.
- Using **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)**, the LLM pulls relevant **MITRE ATT&CK** TTP entries and produces an executive summary for SOC analysts.
- This reduces analyst cognitive load and accelerates incident response by **2–5×** (industry benchmarks).

*(Reference block diagram: see Section 4.1 above.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Diffusion Models for Threat Detection (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Forward Diffusion Process (3 Marks):**

A fixed Markov chain gradually adds Gaussian noise over $T$ steps:

$$q(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(x_t;\, \sqrt{1 - \beta_t}\, x_{t-1},\, \beta_t I\right)$$

where $\beta_t \in (0, 1)$ is a variance schedule. Marginalizing over intermediate steps:

$$q(x_t \mid x_0) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(x_t;\, \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\, x_0,\, (1 - \bar{\alpha}_t) I\right)$$

with $\alpha_t = 1 - \beta_t$ and $\bar{\alpha}_t = \prod_{s=1}^{t} \alpha_s$.

**Step 2 — Reverse Denoising Process (2 Marks):**

A neural network $p_\theta(x_{t-1} \mid x_t)$ learns to predict the denoised $x_{t-1}$:

$$p_\theta(x_{t-1} \mid x_t) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(x_{t-1};\, \mu_\theta(x_t, t),\, \Sigma_\theta(x_t, t)\right)$$

**Step 3 — Anomaly Score (2 Marks):**

For an input $x_0$, the model performs reverse denoising from $t = T$ down to $t = 0$. The anomaly score averages reconstruction error across all timesteps:

$$A(x_0) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \Vert x_0 - \hat{x}_0^{(t)} \Vert_2^2$$

A **zero-day anomaly** lies off the training distribution, producing a high residual at multiple timesteps and thus a high score.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must memorize for KTU ESE & University Exam:**

- ✅ **Predictive Analytics** = proactive forecasting; *not* post-incident forensics.
- ✅ The **four generative paradigms**: **GAN, VAE, Diffusion, LLM** — know one cyber use-case of each.
- ✅ **GAN value function**: $\min_G \max_D \mathbb{E}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$
- ✅ **Discriminator loss** is binary cross-entropy; **Generator loss** is the saturating form $-\log D(G(z))$.
- ✅ **VAE ELBO** = Reconstruction $-$ KL Divergence. The KL term enforces a Gaussian latent prior.
- ✅ **Anomaly score** for autoencoders: $A(x) = \Vert x - D(E(x)) \Vert_2^2$.
- ✅ **Diffusion forward process**: $q(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(\sqrt{1-\beta_t} x_{t-1}, \beta_t I)$.
- ✅ **LLM objective**: $p_\theta(x) = \prod_i p_\theta(x_i \mid x_{<i})$ — autoregressive next-token prediction.
- ✅ **Evaluation metrics**: Precision, Recall, **F1-score**, **AUC-ROC** — always quote numbers in the range $[0, 1]$.
- ✅ **MITRE ATT&CK** mapping is expected in 14-mark answers — always include at least one TTP reference (e.g., T1059 Command and Scripting Interpreter).
- ✅ **Class imbalance** in malware datasets is the *primary* justification for using GenAI augmentation.
- ✅ **Zero-day detection** is *unsupervised* — generative models trained only on benign data flag deviations.
- ✅ **Threshold $\tau$** for anomaly scoring is set via the **validation set** quantile (commonly 95th or 99th percentile).
- ✅ **RAG + LLM** is the modern standard for automated threat-report generation in **STIX 2.1** format.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automated Security Patch Generation and Vulnerability Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT633 - AI AND ML IN CYBER SECURITY DEFENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Automated Security Patch Generation and Vulnerability Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Vulnerability Management** is the systematic, continuous, and cyclical process of identifying, classifying, prioritizing, remediating, and verifying security vulnerabilities across an organization's software, hardware, and network infrastructure. In the context of AI/ML-driven cyber defence, vulnerability management is augmented by intelligent automation that uses machine learning classifiers, graph neural networks, and large language models (LLMs) to predict exploitability, rank severity, and even synthesize remediation code.

**Automated Security Patch Generation** is the AI/ML-driven engineering process of automatically producing code transformations (patches/fixes) that eliminate a software vulnerability without requiring human programmer intervention. These systems typically employ deep neural networks — particularly **Neural Machine Translation (NMT)**, **encoder-decoder transformer architectures**, or **reinforcement learning agents** — to map an input pair of (vulnerable code, vulnerability description) to a corrected, non-vulnerable code output.

Together, these two domains form the **"Identify → Prioritize → Patch → Verify"** closed-loop engine that modern Security Operations Centers (SOCs) and DevSecOps pipelines depend upon.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PECCT633 Module 4):**
> Students must be able to articulate the role of AI/ML in **CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures)** classification, **CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System)** scoring, exploit prediction (EPSS), and the technical workflow of LLM-based code repair tools such as *VRepair*, *Recoder*, *VulFix*, and *Prophet*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **hospital administrator** managing thousands of patients (your software assets):

- **Vulnerability Management** is the hospital's full diagnostic-and-treatment cycle: scanning patients (vulnerability scanning), reading their charts (CVE/NVD databases), triaging them by urgency (CVSS/EPSS scoring), assigning doctors to treat the most critical cases first (prioritization), applying the right medicine (patch generation), and finally running a follow-up check to ensure recovery (patch verification).
- **Automated Patch Generation** is the dream of an **AI robot surgeon** that watches the patient's X-ray (the buggy code), understands the disease pattern (the vulnerability type — buffer overflow, SQL injection, use-after-free), and operates with precision to stitch the wound shut. It learns from millions of past successful surgeries (training data of historical bug-fix commits) and never gets tired.

The "intelligence" in this system comes from training on massive corpora of open-source bug-fix pairs (e.g., from GitHub commits, OSS-Fuzz reports, and the **NIST NVD database**), enabling the model to generalize to *zero-day* (previously unseen) vulnerabilities.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Metric:** The average **Mean Time To Patch (MTTP)** for critical CVEs in enterprise environments is **30–60 days**, while active exploitation often occurs within **7–15 days** of disclosure. AI/ML-driven automation aims to compress MTTP from weeks to minutes.

## 1.3 Standardized Metrics & Constants

- **CVSS Base Score Range:** $0.0 \leq \text{CVSS} \leq 10.0$
- **EPSS Percentile:** $0\% \leq \text{EPSS} \leq 100\%$ (probability of exploitation in the next 30 days)
- **Industry Standard:** **CVE** numbering (e.g., CVE-2024-3094 — XZ Utils backdoor)
- **Patch Format Standards:** **Unified Diff Format (UDIFF)**, **Combined Diff Format**, **Common Patch Format**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Vulnerability Management Lifecycle (VML)

The VML is a **five-stage continuous loop** mandated by frameworks such as **NIST SP 800-40 Rev. 4** and **ISO/IEC 27002:2022**:

### Stage 1 — Asset Discovery & Inventory
- Map every software component, library, container, and API endpoint.
- Generate a **Software Bill of Materials (SBOM)** in **SPDX** or **CycloneDX** format.
- AI augmentation: NLP-based asset tagging from configuration management databases (CMDBs).

### Stage 2 — Vulnerability Identification
- **Active scanning:** Nessus, Qualys, OpenVAS.
- **Passive scanning:** network traffic analysis with ML anomaly detection.
- **Software Composition Analysis (SCA):** Trivy, Snyk, Dependabot.
- AI augmentation: **fuzzing with reinforcement learning** to discover zero-days (e.g., AFL++, LibFuzzer guided by neural reward functions).

### Stage 3 — Vulnerability Prioritization
- Multi-factor ranking combining **CVSS**, **EPSS**, **asset criticality**, and **threat intelligence feeds**.
- AI models: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) trained on **CWE → exploit chains**.

### Stage 4 — Remediation (Patch Generation & Deployment)
- AI-driven code transformation (the heart of this module).
- Staged rollout: dev → staging → canary → production.

### Stage 5 — Verification & Reporting
- **Regression testing**, **mutation testing**, **SAST re-scans**.
- ML models: program-analysis-based proof-of-fix and test-case synthesis.

## 2.2 Automated Patch Generation — The Deep Learning Pipeline

The dominant paradigm in modern research (since the seminal **"Automated Patch Generation via Neural Machine Translation"** by Tufano et al., 2019) is to treat patch generation as a **sequence-to-sequence (Seq2Seq) translation problem**, where:

$$\text{Input} = \text{ buggy code token sequence } (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$$
$$\text{Output} = \text{ fixed code token sequence } (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_m)$$

### Core Architectural Components:

| Component | Function | Typical Implementation |
|---|---|---|
| **Tokenizer** | Converts source code into tokens | BPE, WordPiece, or custom AST-based tokenizer |
| **Encoder** | Captures semantic context of buggy code | Bi-LSTM, Transformer encoder, CodeBERT |
| **Decoder** | Generates fixed code token-by-token | LSTM, Transformer decoder, GPT-style autoregressive |
| **Constraint Layer** | Ensures syntactic validity | AST-guided beam search, grammar-constrained decoding |
| **Verifier** | Confirms fix correctness | Test execution, static analysis, proof-of-fix |

### State-of-the-Art Models:

- **Prophet (Long & Rinard, 2016):** Uses candidate ranking with learned probabilistic models over program changes.
- **SequenceR (Chen et al., 2019):** Copy-augmented Seq2Seq with RL-based refinement.
- **Recoder (Zhu et al., 2021):** Syntax-guided edit decoder using AST traversals.
- **VulFix (Fu et al., 2022):** Pre-trained T5-based model fine-tuned on security-fix commits.
- **VRepair (Chen et al., 2022):** Transfer learning from code translation to vulnerability repair.
- **LLM4Patch (2024):** LLM (Code Llama, GPT-4) prompted with vulnerability context.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Application / Units |
|---|---|---|
| CVSS Base Score | $\text{CVSS} = \text{round}(\min(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\, 10))$ | Severity score (0.0–10.0) |
| Impact Sub-score (Simplified) | $\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times (1 - (1 - C)(1 - I)(1 - A))$ | Confidentiality/Integrity/Availability (0.0–6.42) |
| Exploitability Sub-score | $\text{Exploit} = 8.22 \times \text{AV} \times \text{AC} \times \text{PR} \times \text{UI}$ | Attack Vector, Complexity, Privileges, UI (0.0–10.0) |
| EPSS Score | $P(\text{exploit} \leq 30 \text{ days} \vert \text{CVE})$ | Probability (0.0–1.0) |
| Risk Prioritization Index (RPI) | $\text{RPI} = w_1 \cdot \text{CVSS} + w_2 \cdot \text{EPSS} + w_3 \cdot \text{AC} + w_4 \cdot \text{EXP}$ | Weighted composite (weights sum to 1) |
| BLEU Score (patch similarity) | $\text{BLEU} = \text{BP} \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | n-gram overlap metric (0.0–1.0) |
| CodeBLEU | $\text{CodeBLEU} = \alpha \cdot \text{BLEU} + \beta \cdot \text{BLEU}_w + \gamma \cdot \text{AST} + \delta \cdot \text{DF}$ | Code-aware similarity |
| Patch Correctness (empirical) | $\text{PC} = \frac{\vert \text{Plausible Patches} \cap \text{Correct Patches} \vert}{\vert \text{Generated Patches} \vert}$ | Ratio (0.0–1.0) |
| MTTP Metric | $\text{MTTP} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (t_{\text{patch}, i} - t_{\text{disclosure}, i})$ | Mean Time To Patch in days/hours |
| Cross-Entropy Loss (training) | $\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{t=1}^{T_i} \log P(y_t^{(i)} \vert y_{<t}^{(i)}, x^{(i)})$ | Token-level training loss |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently test the **CVSS Impact equation** and the **Risk Prioritization Index (RPI)** weighting scheme. Memorize the constants **6.42** and **8.22** in the CVSS formula — they are direct CVSS v3.1 specification values.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Software Industry:** Microsoft, Google, and Meta use ML-augmented fuzzing and patch suggestion in their internal CI/CD (e.g., Google's **OSS-Fuzz** discovers 30,000+ bugs/year).
- **Government / Defense:** DARPA's **Cyber Grand Challenge (CGC, 2016)** and **CHESS program** pioneered fully autonomous vulnerability discovery + patching systems.
- **Healthcare/IoT:** Zero-day patches must roll out to millions of medical devices — AI prioritization prevents cascading failures.
- **Cloud Security:** SBOM-driven AI patch recommendation is now **US Executive Order 14028 (2021)** compliant.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: CVSS v3.1 Base Score (Full KTU Board-Style Working)

The KTU board loves asking students to compute the **CVSS Base Score** from a given vector. We will derive it from first principles.

### Step 1 — Set Up the Base Equation

The CVSS v3.1 Base Score is defined by FIRST.org as:

$$\text{BaseScore} = \text{round\_up}\Big(\min(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\, 10)\Big)$$

where $\text{round\_up}$ rounds to one decimal place. If $\text{Impact} \leq 0$, the score is set to 0.

### Step 2 — Compute Impact Sub-Score (ISC)

Let $C$, $I$, $A \in \{0.0, 0.22, 0.56\}$ be the **Confidentiality**, **Integrity**, and **Availability Impact** metric values (None=0.0, Low=0.22, High=0.56).

$$\text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} = 1 - (1 - C)(1 - I)(1 - A)$$

$$\text{Impact} = \begin{cases} 6.42 \times \text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} & \text{if Scope} = U \\ 7.52 \times (\text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} - 0.029) - 3.25 \times (\text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} - 0.02)^{15} & \text{if Scope} = C \end{cases}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Exploitability Sub-Score

The metric values (from FIRST.org specification):

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| AV (Attack Vector) | Network=0.85, Adjacent=0.62, Local=0.55, Physical=0.2 |
| AC (Attack Complexity) | Low=0.77, High=0.44 |
| PR (Privileges Required) | None=0.85, Low=0.62 (0.68 if Scope=C), High=0.27 (0.5 if Scope=C) |
| UI (User Interaction) | None=0.85, Required=0.62 |

$$\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times \text{AV} \times \text{AC} \times \text{PR} \times \text{UI}$$

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example

**Given Vector:** `CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H`

This represents a **critical** remote, unauthenticated, no-user-interaction, scope-unchanged, high-CIA vulnerability (e.g., a typical RCE in a web server).

**Substitution into Impact equation:**

$$\text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} = 1 - (1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.56) = 1 - (0.44)^3 = 1 - 0.085184 = 0.914816$$

$$\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times 0.914816 = 5.8739$$

**Substitution into Exploitability equation:**

$$\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 = 8.22 \times 0.473 \approx 3.887$$

**Final Base Score:**

$$\text{BaseScore} = \text{round\_up}\big(\min(5.8739 + 3.887,\, 10)\big) = \text{round\_up}(9.7609) = 9.8$$

**Severity:** **CRITICAL** (since $9.0 \leq \text{Score} \leq 10.0$).

> [!NOTE]
> This is a classic **CVE-2021-44228 (Log4Shell)** style result. KTU examiners regularly use Log4Shell or EternalBlue (CVE-2017-0144) as worked examples.

## 3.2 Derivation: AI Patch Generation — Cross-Entropy Loss for Seq2Seq Training

A model that generates patches $\hat{y} = (\hat{y}_1, \hat{y}_2, \ldots, \hat{y}_T)$ for ground-truth fix $y = (y_1, \ldots, y_T)$ is trained by minimizing the **negative log-likelihood** over the entire training corpus $\mathcal{D}$ of $N$ vulnerable/fixed pairs.

### Step 1 — Per-Token Likelihood

For an autoregressive decoder, the probability of a sequence is decomposed via the chain rule:

$$P(y \vert x) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(y_t \vert y_{<t}, x)$$

### Step 2 — Log Transformation

Taking the natural logarithm converts products to summations (numerically stable):

$$\log P(y \vert x) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P(y_t \vert y_{<t}, x)$$

### Step 3 — Negative for Loss

Since we minimize loss but maximize likelihood, we negate:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{NLL}}(x, y) = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P(y_t \vert y_{<t}, x)$$

### Step 4 — Average Across Corpus

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{t=1}^{T_i} \log P_\theta\big(y_t^{(i)} \mid y_{<t}^{(i)}, x^{(i)}\big)$$

where $\theta$ denotes all learnable parameters. This is precisely the **Cross-Entropy Loss** used in transformer-based patch generators (CodeBERT, T5, Code Llama fine-tuning).

## 3.3 Derivation: Risk Prioritization Index (RPI)

Modern AI-driven triage systems weight multiple signals. A canonical RPI formula is:

$$\text{RPI} = w_1 \cdot \text{CVSS}_{\text{norm}} + w_2 \cdot \text{EPSS} + w_3 \cdot \text{AC} + w_4 \cdot \text{EXP}$$

where:
- $\text{CVSS}_{\text{norm}} = \text{CVSS}/10$ (normalized to $[0,1]$)
- $\text{EPSS} \in [0,1]$
- $\text{AC} \in [0,1]$ (Asset Criticality)
- $\text{EXP} \in [0,1]$ (Exploit availability in the wild, e.g., from CISA KEV)
- $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$

**Example:** Suppose $w_1 = 0.4, w_2 = 0.3, w_3 = 0.2, w_4 = 0.1$, CVSS = 9.8, EPSS = 0.7, AC = 0.9, EXP = 1.0 (in CISA KEV):

$$\text{RPI} = 0.4 \cdot 0.98 + 0.3 \cdot 0.7 + 0.2 \cdot 0.9 + 0.1 \cdot 1.0 = 0.392 + 0.21 + 0.18 + 0.10 = 0.882$$

A patch with **RPI > 0.8** should be deployed within 24 hours (SLA).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Vulnerability Management Lifecycle — AI-Augmented

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Asset Discovery SBOM Generation] --> B[Vulnerability Identification Fuzzing and SCA]
    B --> C[AI Triage CVSS and EPSS Prediction]
    C --> D{Priority Threshold RPI greater than 0.7}
    D -- Yes --> E[Automated Patch Generation NMT or LLM]
    D -- No --> F[Defer to Next Cycle]
    E --> G[Static Verification SAST and AST Check]
    G --> H[Dynamic Verification Test Suite Execution]
    H --> I{Patch Plausible and Correct}
    I -- Yes --> J[Deploy via CI CD Pipeline]
    I -- No --> K[Retrain Model with New Fix Pair]
    K --> E
    J --> L[Post Deployment Monitoring SIEM and EDR]
    L --> A
```

## 4.2 Automated Patch Generation — Deep Learning Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Code Corpus] --> B[Tokenizer BPE or WordPiece]
    B --> C[Encoder Transformer or BiLSTM]
    C --> D[Context Vector Hidden State]
    D --> E[Decoder Autoregressive Transformer]
    E --> F[Token Probabilities Softmax]
    F --> G[Beam Search Top K Candidates]
    G --> H[AST Constraint Filter]
    H --> I[Verifier Compile and Test Run]
    I --> J{All Tests Pass}
    J -- Yes --> K[Final Patch Output]
    J -- No --> L[Re-rank with Reward Model RLHF]
    L --> G
```

## 4.3 AI-Driven Vulnerability Triage & Prioritization Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CVE Feed NVD and GHSA] --> B[Feature Extractor]
    B --> C1[Text Description Embedding BERT]
    B --> C2[Code Snippet Embedding CodeBERT]
    B --> C3[CWE Taxonomy Mapping]
    C1 --> D[Multimodal Fusion Layer]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[GNN over CVE CVE Graph]
    E --> F[Priority Score RPI Output]
    F --> G{Score Tier}
    G -- Critical --> H[Auto Patch Queue]
    G -- High --> I[Human Review Queue]
    G -- Medium Low --> J[Scheduled Remediation]
```

## 4.4 End-to-End DevSecOps Patch Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Developer Commit] --> B[CI Trigger]
    B --> C[SAST Scan]
    B --> D[SCA Scan]
    C --> E[Vulnerability Found]
    D --> E
    E --> F[AI Patch Suggester VRepair or VulFix]
    F --> G[Pull Request Auto Generated]
    G --> H[Reviewer Approval]
    H --> I[Merge and Deploy]
    I --> J[Runtime Monitoring]
    J --> K[Feedback Loop to Model Retraining]
    K --> F
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE) and Common Weakness Enumeration (CWE). Differentiate them with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures):** A CVE is a unique, standardized identifier (e.g., `CVE-2024-3094`) assigned by the **MITRE Corporation** to a *specific, real-world vulnerability* in a *specific product/version*. It tracks **instances** of vulnerabilities.
- **CWE (Common Weakness Enumeration):** A CWE is a *category or class* of weakness (e.g., `CWE-119: Buffer Errors`, `CWE-89: SQL Injection`). It tracks **types** of software flaws, not specific incidents.
- **Differentiation:** A CVE is an *instance*; a CWE is a *class*. For example, **Log4Shell** is CVE-2021-44228 (instance) belonging to CWE-502 (Deserialization of Untrusted Data) and CWE-20 (Improper Input Validation). One CWE can have thousands of CVEs mapped to it.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**List and briefly explain the three core components of the CVSS v3.1 framework.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS) v3.1** is composed of three metric groups:

1. **Base Metrics:** Intrinsic, vendor-neutral properties of the vulnerability that do not change over time or across user environments. Includes Attack Vector, Attack Complexity, Privileges Required, User Interaction, Scope, Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability Impact. Produces a score in the range $0.0$ to $10.0$.
2. **Temporal Metrics:** Time-dependent characteristics that reflect the *current* state of exploitation and patch availability. Includes Exploit Code Maturity, Remediation Level, Report Confidence.
3. **Environmental Metrics:** User-environment-specific factors that allow customization of the score based on the importance of the affected IT asset to the organization. Includes Confidentiality/Integrity/Availability Requirements, Modified Base metrics.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question must be answered as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Below we provide **TWO complete alternative questions**, of which the student answers ONE.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **AI-augmented Vulnerability Management Lifecycle (VML)**. Discuss the role of machine learning at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **CVSS v3.1 Base Score** for the following vector and state its severity rating:

$$\text{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:C/C:L/I:L/A:N}$$

Show all substitution steps. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — AI-Augmented VML (7 marks):**

The VML is a **continuous, five-stage feedback loop**:

1. **Asset Discovery & SBOM Generation:** AI uses NLP and GNNs to auto-classify software components from CI logs, package manifests, and container images into a normalized **CycloneDX/SPDX SBOM**. *(1 mark)*
2. **Vulnerability Identification:** ML-augmented fuzzers (AFL++, LibFuzzer with neural reward shaping) generate edge-coverage-guided inputs to discover zero-days. SCA tools (Snyk, Dependabot) use **graph embeddings** to detect vulnerable dependency chains. *(1.5 marks)*
3. **AI Triage & Prioritization:** Transformer-based models (BERT fine-tuned on CVE descriptions) predict **EPSS scores** and **CWE categories**. GNNs over a **CVE-CVE similarity graph** (built from shared code features) predict exploit chaining. Output: **RPI** score. *(1.5 marks)*
4. **Automated Patch Generation:** Seq2Seq / LLM models (VRepair, VulFix, Code Llama) translate vulnerable code → fixed code. AST-constrained beam search ensures syntactic validity. RLHF reward model re-ranks candidate patches. *(1.5 marks)*
5. **Verification & Deployment:** Reinforcement learning agents synthesize regression tests. Program-analysis proof-of-fix verifies that the vulnerability-triggering input no longer succeeds. Staged deployment via canary + blue/green CI/CD. *(1.5 marks)*

**Block diagram (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes).**

#### **Part (b) — CVSS Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Extract metrics from the vector `AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:C/C:L/I:L/A:N`:** *(1 mark)*

| Metric | Value | Numerical Value |
|---|---|---|
| AV | Network | 0.85 |
| AC | Low | 0.77 |
| PR | None | 0.85 |
| UI | Required | 0.62 |
| Scope | Changed (C) | — |
| C | Low | 0.22 |
| I | Low | 0.22 |
| A | None | 0.0 |

**Step 2 — Compute Impact Sub-Score Base:** *(2 marks)*

$$\text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} = 1 - (1 - 0.22)(1 - 0.22)(1 - 0.0) = 1 - (0.78)(0.78)(1.0) = 1 - 0.6084 = 0.3916$$

**Step 3 — Apply Scope=C formula for Impact:** *(2 marks)*

$$\text{Impact} = 7.52 \times (\text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} - 0.029) - 3.25 \times (\text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} - 0.02)^{15}$$

$$= 7.52 \times (0.3916 - 0.029) - 3.25 \times (0.3916 - 0.02)^{15}$$

$$= 7.52 \times 0.3626 - 3.25 \times (0.3716)^{15}$$

$$= 2.7268 - 3.25 \times (3.7 \times 10^{-10})$$

$$\approx 2.7268 - 0.0000 \approx 2.73$$

**Step 4 — Compute Exploitability:** *(1 mark)*

$$\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.62$$

$$= 8.22 \times 0.3470 \approx 2.852$$

**Step 5 — Final Base Score:** *(1 mark)*

$$\text{BaseScore} = \text{round\_up}\big(\min(2.73 + 2.852,\, 10)\big) = \text{round\_up}(5.582) = 5.6$$

**Severity Rating:** **MEDIUM** (since $4.0 \leq 5.6 < 7.0$). ✅

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of an **automated security patch generation system based on Neural Machine Translation (NMT)**. List any four state-of-the-art tools/models and their distinguishing features. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vulnerability is reported with the following metrics: **CVSS = 8.5, EPSS = 0.45, Asset Criticality = 0.8, and it is present in the CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog.** Calculate the **Risk Prioritization Index (RPI)** assuming weights $w_1 = 0.35, w_2 = 0.25, w_3 = 0.20, w_4 = 0.20$. Justify whether this CVE should be auto-patched or sent for human review (threshold = 0.70). **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — NMT-Based Patch Generation Architecture (7 marks):**

The system treats patch generation as a **language translation problem** — translating "buggy code" into "fixed code."

**Architecture pipeline (refer to Section 4.2 diagram):** *(4 marks)*

1. **Input Preprocessing:** Source code is tokenized using a **Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE)** tokenizer trained on a large code corpus. Optional: extract **AST paths** as additional features.
2. **Encoder:** A **Transformer encoder** (e.g., CodeBERT, GraphCodeBERT) processes the tokenized buggy code into a **contextual hidden state representation** $H = (h_1, \ldots, h_n)$.
3. **Decoder:** A **Transformer decoder** autoregressively generates the fixed code one token at a time, attending to the encoder's hidden states.
4. **Constraint Layer:** An **AST-guided beam search** filters out candidates that fail to parse, ensuring syntactic validity.
5. **Verifier & Reward Model:** Generated patches are compiled and tested; a **reward model trained via RLHF** (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) re-ranks candidates.
6. **Output:** The top-1 plausible, correct patch is returned.

**Four State-of-the-Art Tools/Models:** *(3 marks — 0.75 each)*

| Tool/Model | Year | Distinguishing Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Prophet** | 2016 | Probabilistic ranking of fix candidates over abstract program changes |
| **SequenceR** | 2019 | Copy-augmented Seq2Seq + RL-based refinement loop |
| **Recoder** | 2021 | Syntax-guided edit decoder using AST traversals for high validity |
| **VulFix** | 2022 | Pre-trained T5 (Text-To-Text Transfer Transformer) fine-tuned on security-fix commits |

#### **Part (b) — RPI Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Normalize CVSS:** *(1 mark)*

$$\text{CVSS}_{\text{norm}} = \frac{8.5}{10} = 0.85$$

**Step 2 — Identify the values:** *(1 mark)*

- EPSS = 0.45
- Asset Criticality (AC) = 0.80
- EXP (presence in CISA KEV) = 1.00
- Weights: $w_1 = 0.35, w_2 = 0.25, w_3 = 0.20, w_4 = 0.20$

**Step 3 — Verify weights sum to 1:** *(1 mark)*

$$0.35 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.20 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Compute RPI:** *(2 marks)*

$$\text{RPI} = 0.35 \cdot 0.85 + 0.25 \cdot 0.45 + 0.20 \cdot 0.80 + 0.20 \cdot 1.00$$

$$= 0.2975 + 0.1125 + 0.1600 + 0.2000 = 0.7700$$

**Step 5 — Decision and Justification:** *(2 marks)*

$$\text{RPI} = 0.77 > 0.70 \text{ (threshold)} \implies \textbf{AUTO-PATCH} \text{ is triggered.}$$

**Justification:** The CVE scores **0.77**, which exceeds the auto-patch SLA threshold of 0.70. It is also listed in **CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog**, indicating **active exploitation in the wild** — this is the highest-priority "EXP" signal ($= 1.0$). The system should dispatch the CVE to the **Automated Patch Generation Queue** for AI-driven remediation, with a 24-hour deployment SLA. ✅

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Module 4 Questions:**
>
> 1. **CVSS Formula Errors:** Forgetting that when **Scope = Changed (C)**, the Impact formula is **completely different** from the Scope = Unchanged case. Many students blindly use $6.42 \times \text{ISC}_{\text{Base}}$ for both, losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Unit / Range Errors:** Not normalizing CVSS to $[0,1]$ before plugging into composite formulas like RPI. CVSS is on a $0$–$10$ scale, EPSS on $0$–$1$. Mixing them gives garbage results.
> 3. **Confusing CVE vs. CWE:** Examiners explicitly test this distinction. CVE = instance; CWE = class. Mixing them is an instant 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Forgetting Beam Search Constraints:** When asked about NMT patch generation, students often omit the **AST-guided decoding** step. Without it, generated code is often syntactically invalid.
> 5. **Skipping the Reinforcement Learning refinement loop:** Modern systems like VRepair use RL/Reward Models as a second pass. Omitting this loses 1 mark in 7-mark architecture questions.
> 6. **Mis-stating EPSS:** EPSS is the **probability of exploitation within 30 days**, not the "exploitability score" or "severity." Many textbooks loosely use these terms — on the KTU exam, be precise.
> 7. **Omitting $\text{round\_up}$:** CVSS must be rounded to **one decimal place** using the standard `round_up` (ceiling-style) function, not normal rounding. Score of 9.7999 → 9.8, not 9.7.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **Module 4 — Quick-Fire Revision:**
>
> - **Vulnerability Management** = continuous Identify → Prioritize → Patch → Verify loop.
> - **CVE** = instance, **CWE** = class, **CVSS** = severity score, **EPSS** = 30-day exploitation probability.
> - **CVSS v3.1** range: **$0.0$–$10.0$**; constants to memorize: **6.42** (ISC Scope=U), **7.52** (Scope=C), **8.22** (Exploitability).
> - **Severity Tiers:** None (0.0), Low (0.1–3.9), Medium (4.0–6.9), High (7.0–8.9), Critical (9.0–10.0).
> - **Automated patch generation = Seq2Seq translation** from buggy code → fixed code.
> - **Key SOTA tools:** Prophet, SequenceR, Recoder, VRepair, VulFix, LLM4Patch.
> - **AST-guided beam search** + **RL reward model** = state-of-the-art post-processing.
> - **RPI** formula weights must sum to **1.0**; components are CVSS_norm, EPSS, AC, EXP.
> - **SBOM standards:** SPDX, CycloneDX (driven by US Executive Order 14028).
> - **MTTP** (Mean Time To Patch) is the key SLA KPI; AI aims to compress from weeks → minutes.
> - **DevSecOps** integration: SAST + SCA + AI patch suggester + reviewer approval + canary deploy.
> - **DARPA CGC (2016)** = first fully autonomous vulnerability discovery + patching competition.
> - **CISA KEV catalog** = authoritative list of actively exploited CVEs — highest priority for patching.
> - **CodeBLEU** = code-aware patch similarity metric (adds AST match + dataflow match to BLEU).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strengthening Encryption Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT633 - AI AND ML IN CYBER SECURITY DEFENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Strengthening Encryption Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Encryption Protocol Strengthening** is the systematic process of augmenting classical and modern cryptographic protocols (e.g., AES, RSA, ECC, TLS, IPsec) with advanced hardening techniques — including post-quantum algorithms, adaptive key management, homomorphic computation, and **AI/ML-driven anomaly detection** — to resist evolving adversarial threats, quantum attacks, side-channel leaks, and cryptanalytic exploitation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "Strengthening encryption protocols involves the integration of machine learning, deep learning, and reinforcement learning techniques into cryptographic key management, protocol negotiation, traffic obfuscation, side-channel resistance, and post-quantum migration to produce self-adaptive, threat-aware encryption systems."

The two principal pillars of the modern strengthening framework are:

1. **Cryptographic Hardening** — algorithmic robustness (AES-256, SHA-3, CRYSTALS-Kyber, lattice-based schemes).
2. **AI-Augmented Defence** — data-driven policy decisions (ML-based key entropy estimation, deep learning for side-channel leakage detection, RL for adaptive cipher selection).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Bank Vault" Intuition

Imagine a **traditional bank vault** with a fixed combination lock. A determined thief can study the lock mechanism, try common combinations, and eventually break in (brute force). Now imagine the vault has:

- A **biometric scanner** that adapts to the holder's stress levels (continuous authentication),
- A **guard dog** trained to detect faint vibrations on the wall (side-channel analysis),
- A **self-destruct mechanism** triggered by an AI monitoring attack patterns,
- And the lock's mechanism **rotates** every few minutes to a new cryptographic key (adaptive re-keying).

This is exactly what "strengthening encryption protocols" means in modern cybersecurity — the encryption is no longer static mathematics alone; it is a **living, learning defence system** that uses AI to anticipate, detect, and counter attacks in real time.

### 1.3 Core Pillars at a Glance

| Pillar | Traditional Focus | AI/ML Enhancement |
|---|---|---|
| **Key Generation** | PRNG, TRNG | ML-predicted entropy enhancement, GAN-based true random sources |
| **Algorithm Selection** | Static (AES, RSA) | RL agents choose optimal cipher per workload |
| **Side-Channel Defence** | Masking, hiding | Deep nets detect micro-leakage in power/EM traces |
| **Protocol Negotiation** | TLS handshake rules | ML classifiers detect downgrade attacks |
| **Key Lifecycle** | Manual rotation | Predictive ML triggers re-keying on threat detection |
| **Post-Quantum** | Lattice, code-based | Auto-discovery of hybrid safe parameter sets |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Bolded:** Symmetric security is measured in **bits of security** ($\lambda$); today's KTU-relevant baseline is **AES-128 ($\lambda = 128$ bits)** and the long-term target is **AES-256 / SHA-3-512 ($\lambda \geq 256$ bits)**. The NIST Post-Quantum Security Levels are **Level 1, 3, 5** (matching AES-128, AES-192, AES-256 brute-force equivalents).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Threat Model That Justifies Strengthening

Modern adversaries possess capabilities that classical cryptography was never designed to withstand:

- **Harvest-Now-Decrypt-Later (HNDL):** adversaries record ciphertext today, awaiting quantum decryption tomorrow via Shor's algorithm.
- **Adversarial ML:** gradient-based attacks that fool intrusion detection systems, indirectly exposing keying material.
- **Side-Channel Emanations:** power analysis (DPA, CPA), cache-timing (Flush+Reload), acoustic and EM leakages.
- **Protocol Downgrade Attacks:** POODLE, FREAK, Logjam — forcing a session to the weakest mutually supported cipher.
- **Cryptanalytic Advances:** improvements in lattice reduction (BKZ), Gröbner basis attacks, and the rise of Grover's quadratic speedup on symmetric keys.

### 2.2 Layered Defence Architecture

Encryption protocol strengthening is best understood as **four concentric defensive rings**:

1. **Mathematical Core Layer** — algorithm choice (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305, CRYSTALS-Kyber).
2. **Protocol Negotiation Layer** — handshake integrity (TLS 1.3, hybrid X25519+Kyber).
3. **Operational Hardening Layer** — key lifecycle, HSM integration, certificate pinning.
4. **Cognitive/Augmented Layer** — AI/ML monitors the first three layers and dynamically re-tunes them.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **KTU 2024 Scheme high-yield** and frequently appear in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Domain / Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RSA Modulus Size** | $n = p \cdot q$, with $\vert p \vert \approx \vert q \vert \approx 1024$ bits | bits | Defines RSA security strength |
| **RSA Encryption** | $c \equiv m^{e} \pmod{n}$ | — | Public-key encryption primitive |
| **RSA Decryption** | $m \equiv c^{d} \pmod{n}$ | — | Private-key recovery |
| **Euler Totient** | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | — | Used in $d \cdot e \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ |
| **Discrete Log Hardness** | Given $g, h$, find $x$ such that $h = g^{x} \pmod p$ | — | Foundation of Diffie–Hellman, DSA, ECC |
| **ECC Point Multiplication** | $Q = k \cdot P$, where $k \in \mathbb{Z}_{n}$ | — | ECC public-key derivation |
| **Shannon Entropy of Key** | $H(K) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} p(k_{i}) \log_{2} p(k_{i})$ | bits | Measures key randomness; ML maximises this |
| **AES Round Count** | $N_{r} = 6 + \max(\text{Nk}, \text{Nb})$ for Nk, Nb $\in \{4, 6, 8\}$ | rounds | 10 / 12 / 14 for 128 / 192 / 256-bit keys |
| **AES S-Box** | $S(x) = A \cdot x^{-1} \oplus b$ in $\text{GF}(2^{8})$ | 8-bit non-linearity | Source of AES confusion |
| **Grover's Speedup** | $T_{Grover} = O(\sqrt{2^{\lambda}}) = O(2^{\lambda/2})$ | operations | Doubles required symmetric key length |
| **Shor's Algorithm** | $T_{Shor} = O((\log N)^{3})$ | polynomial | Breaks RSA/ECC in quantum era |
| **NIST PQ Levels** | $\lambda \in \{128, 192, 256\}$ | bits | Matches AES-128/192/256 brute-force |
| **Side-Channel SNR** | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{signal}}^{2}}{\sigma_{\text{noise}}^{2}}$ | dimensionless | DPA detection threshold |
| **Key Re-key Period** | $T_{rekey} = \dfrac{\text{Target Work-Factor}}{\text{Adversary Rate}}$ | seconds | ML-optimised rotation interval |
| **Bit Security of AES-k** | $\lambda_{AES-k} = k$ (classical), $k/2$ (quantum) | bits | 128/192/256 classical; 64/96/128 quantum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Safety Rule Applied:** In all tables, absolute values and "such that" dividers use `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table breakage. No raw `|` pipes appear inside cell content.

### 2.4 Why AI/ML Augments Cryptography

Cryptography is fundamentally deterministic and provably secure under hard mathematical assumptions. AI/ML cannot "break" strong cryptography directly — but it excels at the **non-deterministic, contextual, probabilistic dimensions** of the surrounding system:

- **Predicting attack surfaces** before they are exploited.
- **Detecting micro-anomalies** in side-channel traces that humans or thresholds miss.
- **Optimising parameter selection** (e.g., choosing lattice dimensions, polynomial degrees).
- **Adversarial defence** — generating hardened training data via GANs to make NIDS robust.
- **Autonomous incident response** — auto-rotating keys when anomalous traffic is detected.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sector | Application of Strengthened Encryption |
|---|---|
| **Banking / FinTech** | TLS 1.3 + hybrid PQC for transaction confidentiality |
| **Healthcare** | Homomorphic encryption for encrypted ML on patient data |
| **Defence / Government** | Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) over fibre + ML anomaly alerts |
| **IoT / Edge** | Lightweight AEAD (ChaCha20-Poly1305) + RL-based duty cycling of crypto ops |
| **Cloud Computing** | Attribute-Based Encryption (ABE) + ML-driven access control |
| **5G / 6G Networks** | SUCI (Subscription Concealed Identifier) + AI-enhanced slice isolation |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: RSA Key Generation and the Decryption Exponent

**Step 1 — Choose two large primes.**
Select $p$ and $q$ independently and uniformly at random, each satisfying $p, q \geq 2^{1023}$.

**Step 2 — Compute the modulus.**
The RSA modulus is the product of the two primes:

$$n = p \cdot q$$

This step transforms two secret primes into a public modulus whose factorisation is computationally infeasible for $n \geq 2048$ bits under the **Integer Factorisation Assumption (IFA)**.

**Step 3 — Compute Euler's totient.**

$$\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1)$$

By Euler's theorem, for any $m$ coprime to $n$, $m^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$. This is the algebraic foundation of RSA's asymmetry.

**Step 4 — Choose the public exponent.**
Select $e$ such that $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$. The standard choice is $e = 65537 = 2^{16} + 1$ (Fermat prime $F_{4}$), offering fast encryption with verified non-triviality.

**Step 5 — Derive the private exponent.**
Compute $d$ as the modular inverse of $e$ modulo $\phi(n)$:

$$d \cdot e \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$$

By the Extended Euclidean Algorithm, such a $d$ exists and is unique modulo $\phi(n)$.

**Step 6 — Encryption of plaintext $m$.**
The holder of the public key $(e, n)$ computes:

$$c \equiv m^{e} \pmod{n}$$

**Step 7 — Decryption of ciphertext $c$.**
The private key holder computes:

$$m \equiv c^{d} \pmod{n}$$

**Verification of correctness:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c^{d} &\equiv (m^{e})^{d} \pmod{n} \\
&\equiv m^{e \cdot d} \pmod{n} \\
&\equiv m^{1 + k \cdot \phi(n)} \pmod{n} \\
&\equiv m^{1} \cdot (m^{\phi(n)})^{k} \pmod{n} \\
&\equiv m \cdot 1^{k} \pmod{n} \\
&\equiv m \pmod{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

The penultimate step invokes Euler's theorem. The decryption recovers the plaintext exactly.

### 3.2 Derivation: AES Round Count and the $N_{r} = 6 + \max(N_{k}, N_{b})$ Formula

The AES round count ensures sufficient **confusion + diffusion** to resist linear and differential cryptanalysis over the full key lifetime.

For AES, the key length $N_{k}$ and block size $N_{b}$ are measured in **32-bit words**:

| Variant | $N_{b}$ (block) | $N_{k}$ (key) | $N_{r}$ (rounds) |
|---|---|---|---|
| AES-128 | 4 | 4 | $6 + \max(4,4) = 10$ |
| AES-192 | 4 | 6 | $6 + \max(6,4) = 12$ |
| AES-256 | 4 | 8 | $6 + \max(8,4) = 14$ |

**Origin of the +6 term:**
The 6 extra rounds (over the bare minimum $\max(N_k, N_b)$) act as a **cryptanalytic safety margin** to defeat:
- Square attack (effective up to 6 rounds for AES-128),
- Truncated differential attack,
- Interpolation attack,
- Related-key attacks.

The final round omits the MixColumns step, and the first round adds an initial AddRoundKey — a design feature enhancing diffusion while keeping the cipher invertible.

### 3.3 Derivation: Elliptic Curve Diffie–Hellman (ECDH) Shared Secret

Let $E$ be an elliptic curve over a finite field $\mathbb{F}_{p}$ and $G$ a generator of the cyclic subgroup of order $n$.

**Alice** chooses $d_{A} \in_{R} \{1, \ldots, n-1\}$ and publishes $Q_{A} = d_{A} \cdot G$.
**Bob** chooses $d_{B} \in_{R} \{1, \ldots, n-1\}$ and publishes $Q_{B} = d_{B} \cdot G$.

The shared secret is computed independently by both parties:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Alice: } S &= d_{A} \cdot Q_{B} = d_{A} \cdot d_{B} \cdot G \\
\text{Bob: } S &= d_{B} \cdot Q_{A} = d_{B} \cdot d_{A} \cdot G
\end{aligned}
$$

Scalar multiplication is associative, so both parties obtain the same point $S$. The **Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP)** — given $G$ and $Q = d \cdot G$, find $d$ — is believed to be exponentially hard in $\log_{2} n$ for general curves, giving ECC its ~256-bit equivalent security with only ~256-bit keys.

### 3.4 Derivation: Grover's Algorithm and the Doubling of Symmetric Key Length

Grover's quantum search algorithm finds a marked item in an unsorted database of size $N$ in:

$$T_{Grover} = O(\sqrt{N})$$

For a symmetric cipher with $k$-bit key, brute force requires $N = 2^{k}$ operations classically. Quantumly:

$$
T_{Grover} = O(\sqrt{2^{k}}) = O(2^{k/2})
$$

**Engineering consequence:** AES-128 (classically $\lambda = 128$) provides only $\lambda = 64$ bits of security against a quantum adversary. Hence NIST recommends **AES-256** for long-term post-quantum security ($\lambda_{quantum} = 128$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The same is NOT true for public-key schemes:** Shor's algorithm factors $n$ in polynomial time, fully breaking RSA/ECC. Migration to lattice, code, hash, or isogeny-based schemes is mandatory.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Classical vs Post-Quantum Migration

| Property | RSA-2048 | ECC-256 | CRYSTALS-Kyber-768 | CRYSTALS-Dilithium-3 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Integer factorisation | Discrete log | LWE / Module-LWE | Lattice signatures |
| **Public Key Size** | ~256 bytes | ~64 bytes | ~1184 bytes | ~1952 bytes |
| **Ciphertext Size** | ~256 bytes | ~64 bytes | ~1088 bytes | — |
| **Signature Size** | — | ~64 bytes | — | ~3293 bytes |
| **Classical Security** | ~112 bits | ~128 bits | ~192 bits | ~192 bits |
| **Quantum Security** | 0 (Shor) | 0 (Shor) | ~192 bits | ~192 bits |
| **Speed** | Moderate | Fast | Very fast | Fast |
| **KTU 2024 Status** | Migrate out | Migrate out | Recommended | Recommended |

### 3.6 ML-Driven Key Entropy Maximisation

Given a candidate bit stream $K = \{k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_N\}$ from a hardware TRNG, classical Shannon entropy is:

$$H(K) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} p(k_{i}) \log_{2} p(k_{i})$$

A Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) can be trained as a **distinguisher** between TRNG output and a uniform distribution; the generator is rewarded for outputs the discriminator misclassifies, driving $H(K) \to \log_2 N$ (perfect entropy for $N$ symbols).

This is a direct application of the **Goodfellow et al. (2014)** GAN framework to cryptographic entropy sources.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Four-Layer Defence Architecture

